AREA_SUMMARIES.Strategy, Governance and Investment: How tightly IT strategy is aligned to business outcomes, the strength of governance and decision-making structures, the discipline applied to IT investment, and the construction of the IT organisation itself — covering whether IT operates as a reactive cost centre or a strategic partner driving measurable value.
AREA_SUMMARIES.Operations, Platform and Service Deliver: The maturity of underlying infrastructure and operations — architecture, cloud platforms, observability, automated maintenance, and recovery — alongside the quality and responsiveness of end-user support and the working relationship between infrastructure and application teams.
AREA_SUMMARIES.Security & Business Resilience: Readiness to keep operating through outages, breaches and other disruptions, plus the strength of cyber controls including risk identification, identity and access, vulnerability management, threat detection, incident response and security awareness across the workforce.
AREA_SUMMARIES.Digital Enablement and Future Capability: Maturity of the modern digital workplace, process automation, AI strategy and adoption, and enterprise information management — examining whether technology genuinely empowers the workforce, delivers measurable productivity gains, and positions the business to compete on data and AI.

QUESTIONS.trait: Documented and actively maintained IT / digital strategy aligned to the business’s strategy
QUESTIONS.q: How does the organisation think about aligning IT initiatives to broader business outcomes?
a: IT is a reactive cost center focusing solely on keeping the lights on.
Alignment is ad-hoc, with IT informed of business decisions only after they occur.
IT leaders participate in business planning to support identified strategic priorities.
Every major IT initiative is explicitly linked to a specific, measurable business KPI.
Technology and business strategies are indistinguishable, proactively driving new revenue streams and competitive differentiation.
QUESTIONS.fr: Without IT being deliberately tied to business outcomes, every other technology investment is misdirected. Strategic alignment is the prerequisite that gives every downstream activity its purpose and a defensible ROI lens.

QUESTIONS.trait: Documented and actively maintained IT / digital strategy aligned to the business’s strategy
QUESTIONS.q: How tailored is the IT roadmap to the specific needs and priorities of the business?
a: No formal roadmap exists; technology decisions are made in isolation.
The roadmap is generic, following industry trends with little regard for internal business drivers.
The roadmap identifies major business priorities and aligns investments to core operational requirements.
Technical milestones are explicitly mapped to specific business performance targets, timelines, and measurable success criteria
The roadmap is a dynamic strategic asset, continuously evolving to exploit technology for competitive differentiation.

QUESTIONS.trait: Documented and actively maintained IT / digital strategy aligned to the business’s strategy
QUESTIONS.q: How is ownership for developing and maintaining the roadmap typically assigned?
a: Ownership is undefined, resulting in fragmented and uncoordinated technology efforts.
Individual departments own their own roadmaps with minimal central IT oversight.
A central IT leader is responsible for the roadmap, seeking occasional input from business heads.
Clear governance structures, like steering committees, co-own the roadmap with defined business ownership.
Strategic ownership is shared at the executive level, with IT and business leaders equally accountable for delivery.

QUESTIONS.trait: Documented and actively maintained IT / digital strategy aligned to the business’s strategy
QUESTIONS.q: How is progress against the roadmap shared with leadership or key stakeholders?
a: Progress is rarely shared or only discussed when major failures occur.
Updates are provided ad-hoc through informal meetings or manual reports when requested.
Regular, scheduled reporting on major project milestones is provided to key business stakeholders.
Dashboards provide transparent, frequent updates on roadmap progress and its impact on business value.
Real-time, automated visibility into roadmap execution and ROI is accessible to all executive leadership.

QUESTIONS.trait: Documented and actively maintained IT / digital strategy aligned to the business’s strategy
QUESTIONS.q: How is funding for roadmap initiatives generally planned and approved?
a: Funding is ad-hoc and reactive, often requiring emergency approval for critical needs.
IT must compete for budget against other departments without a clear framework for prioritization.
An annual IT budget is set based on the roadmap and historical spending patterns.
A transparent, business-value-driven process prioritizes and funds initiatives based on clear ROI and TCO awareness.
Dynamic, agile funding models allow for rapid pivots to exploit emerging technology and innovation opportunities.

QUESTIONS.trait: Clear IT governance and decision‑making structures
QUESTIONS.q: How is IT represented in strategic discussions today?
a: IT is excluded from strategic discussions and is only consulted for troubleshooting or maintenance.
IT is occasionally invited to provide technical feedback, but only after strategy is already set.
IT leadership has a consistent seat at the management table to align tech with business goals.
IT is a proactive strategic partner co-designing business outcomes and driving executive-level decisions.
Technology leadership is indistinguishable from business leadership; defining the future of the company.

QUESTIONS.trait: Clear IT governance and decision‑making structures
QUESTIONS.q: Are there clear IT governance structures (e.g., steering committees, defined ownership)?
a: No formal governance structures exist; resulting in uncoordinated and overlapping technology efforts.
Governance is ad-hoc with roles and responsibilities defined only for specific major projects.
A formal IT steering committee meets regularly to provide centralized oversight and accountability.
Strategic committees co-own technology outcomes with defined business owners for every major system.
Governance is embedded and automated, with real-time transparency across all technology ownership and performance.

QUESTIONS.trait: Clear IT governance and decision‑making structures
QUESTIONS.q: How are IT investment decisions usually prioritised and approved?
a: Prioritization is based on immediate crises or the influence of individual department heads.
Investment requests are reviewed ad-hoc without a standardized framework for comparing value.
A standard scoring model prioritizes investments based on cost; risk; and general business benefit.
A multi-stakeholder board prioritizes investments using detailed business cases and projected ROI metrics.
Resource allocation is dynamic and data-driven; automatically shifting to the highest-value opportunities in real-time.

QUESTIONS.trait: Clear IT governance and decision‑making structures
QUESTIONS.q: How are technology‑related risks identified and managed within the organisation?
a: Risk management is non-existent or purely reactive after a significant security or operational failure.
Risks are identified informally but are not documented in a central register or tracked over time.
A formal technology risk register is maintained and reviewed quarterly by IT leadership.
IT risk is integrated into the enterprise risk management framework with clear business mitigation owners.
AI-driven analytics provide autonomous; real-time visibility into the organization’s total risk and compliance posture.

QUESTIONS.trait: Clear IT governance and decision‑making structures
QUESTIONS.q: What is the level of alignment between IT and the broader business planning process?
a: IT and business planning are entirely disconnected; often resulting in mismatched goals and timelines.
IT planning follows business planning with a significant delay; leading to reactive technology execution.
The IT roadmap is aligned to the annual business plan to support identified operational priorities.
Planning is a continuous and collaborative process where IT and business goals are co-developed.
Business planning is tech-led; where digital capabilities define the possible scope of business strategy.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective IT investment approach
QUESTIONS.q: How does the organisation typically approach IT funding across capital and operational expenditure?
a: Funding is unplanned and reactive; occurring only when hardware breaks.
Budgets are managed in departmental silos with little coordination between CapEx and OpEx.
IT budgets are set annually with a clear distinction between maintenance and project spend.
A balanced multi-year funding model supports continuous lifecycle management and debt reduction.
Dynamic outcome-based funding allows IT to pivot resources rapidly to innovation opportunities.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective IT investment approach
QUESTIONS.q: From a financial perspective, how is IT spend reviewed and managed?
a: Spend is tracked via basic invoices with no central oversight or review.
IT costs are reviewed monthly against a high-level budget focusing on minimizing expenses.
Formal monthly reviews track variance across infrastructure and licensing with cost-center allocation.
Advanced FinOps practices provide granular visibility into cloud and service costs with active optimization.
IT spend is managed as a value-driven investment portfolio with real-time transparency.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective IT investment approach
QUESTIONS.q: How do you assess visibility and confidence in IT spend relative to the value it delivers?
a: There is zero visibility into the ROI or business value of technology spending.
Leadership views IT spend as a necessary burden rather than a contributor to business value.
Major project costs are tracked against basic delivery milestones to ensure project accountability.
Formal value-realization metrics track the direct impact of technology spend on operational efficiency.
High confidence exists as automated dashboards link IT consumption directly to business growth.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective IT investment approach
QUESTIONS.q: How are IT investment opportunities evaluated and prioritised over time?
a: Investments are prioritized by the loudest voice or the most urgent technical failure.
Projects are selected based on executive requests without a standardized evaluation framework.
A scoring system evaluates project requests based on cost risk and broad business alignment.
A structured steering committee prioritizes investments using detailed business cases and ROI projections.
Continuous portfolio management prioritizes high-impact innovation based on real-time market needs.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective IT investment approach
QUESTIONS.q: How would you describe relationships with key technology vendors – more transactional, or more strategic?
a: Relationships are purely transactional focusing only on the lowest purchase price during procurement.
Vendors are managed as basic service providers with interactions limited to renewals and support.
Key partners are identified and managed through regular account reviews and service monitoring.
Strategic partners are integrated into long-term roadmapping and co-design of future solutions.
Partnerships are deep and collaborative sharing risk and reward to drive joint innovation.

QUESTIONS.trait: Well constructed IT Organization
QUESTIONS.q: How has the IT organization been constructed?
a: IT roles are undefined with individuals performing jack-of-all-trades support tasks.
The team is structured around legacy technology silos with limited cross-functional collaboration.
A clear organizational chart defines functional teams with specific responsibilities.
The structure is modernized around service-delivery and product teams that align with business functions.
A highly agile organization utilizes global talent and automated platforms to scale with demand.

QUESTIONS.trait: Well constructed IT Organization
QUESTIONS.q: Where does IT report and to whom?
a: IT reports to a middle manager and is excluded from all executive-level discussions.
IT reports to Finance or Operations as a support function rather than a strategic partner.
IT leadership reports to the CEO and participates in regular management team meetings.
IT is a core member of the executive leadership team influencing every major business decision.
The technology leader is a peer to the business owners co-leading the strategic direction.

QUESTIONS.trait: Well constructed IT Organization
QUESTIONS.q: How are key personnel compensated, and evaluated?
a: Compensation is flat and evaluations are informal focusing only on basic task completion.
Performance is measured by basic uptime metrics with standard cost-of-living salary increases.
Personnel have formal KPIs linked to technical performance and professional development goals.
Compensation includes incentives tied to successful project delivery and measurable efficiency gains.
Evaluations are based on innovation and value-creation with incentives linked to company growth.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective and efficient IT Operations & Infrastructure
QUESTIONS.q: Is there a comprehensive, regularly updated architecture guiding all infrastructure decisions?
a: No formal architecture exists; systems are built ad-hoc as needs arise.
A basic network diagram exists but does not guide future purchasing decisions.
A documented Enterprise Architecture guides standard infrastructure deployments and lifecycle replacements.
Advanced architecture frameworks drive continuous optimization and modernization of all platforms.
Architecture is dynamic and self-documenting automatically enforcing standards through policy-driven code.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective and efficient IT Operations & Infrastructure
QUESTIONS.q: Is the infrastructure based on cloud‑first, scalable platforms with effective use of current technology (e.g., PaaS / automation, Infrastructure as Code, etc.)?
a: Infrastructure is entirely on-premises manual and relies on aging legacy hardware.
A lift-and-shift cloud approach is used treating cloud servers like on-premises VMs.
A hybrid cloud strategy is in place with significant use of managed PaaS services.
Cloud-native principles and Infrastructure as Code (IaC) drive the majority of environment deployments.
Fully automated serverless architecture scales dynamically and self-heals without human intervention.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective and efficient IT Operations & Infrastructure
QUESTIONS.q: Are all significant operations and infrastructure monitored, observed and performance proactively managed in real time?
a: Monitoring is non-existent; IT learns about failures when users report them.
Basic up/down monitoring alerts IT to hardware failures after they have occurred.
Centralized logging and alerting provide real-time visibility into the health of all critical systems.
Automated remediation scripts respond to monitoring signals to fix issues in real-time.
Full-stack observability and AIOps predicts and remediates performance degradation before user impact occurs.
QUESTIONS.fr: You cannot operate, secure or improve what you cannot see. Observability is the prerequisite control that incident response, performance management and cost optimisation all depend on.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective and efficient IT Operations & Infrastructure
QUESTIONS.q: How much of infrastructure and operations management, patching, and maintenance is policy‑driven and automated?
a: All patching and maintenance are performed manually and irregularly by IT staff.
Basic automated tools are used for OS patching but maintenance remains a manual effort.
Policy-driven update management ensures all systems are patched within defined monthly windows.
Infrastructure is maintained as code with automated CI/CD pipelines managing all configuration.
Governance is enforced by automated policy preventing non-compliant or unpatched resources from existing.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective and efficient IT Operations & Infrastructure
QUESTIONS.q: How quickly can infrastructure and operations environments be recovered or rebuilt if degraded?
a: Recovery is manual and slow often taking days or weeks to rebuild from scratch.
Environments can be recovered from traditional backups but the process is time-consuming.
Documented recovery procedures and server images allow for restoration within 24-48 hours.
Automated recovery orchestration allows full site restoration within hours using modern replication tools.
Environments are immutable and can be redeployed from code in minutes to a known-good state.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective and efficient End-User IT support
QUESTIONS.q: How effectively and efficiently do you training all end user on how to leverage available technology and upskill them as appropriate (e.g., for AI)?
a: No formal training exists; users are left to self-teach or use word-of-mouth.
Periodic training is provided via documents or videos but lacks engagement tracking.
Formal training sessions are scheduled during major rollouts with basic completion tracking.
Proactive digital literacy programs utilize champions and data to target specific skill gaps.
A continuous learning culture is embedded using automated platforms to drive deep technology adoption.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective and efficient End-User IT support
QUESTIONS.q: How effectively and efficiently do you support the ad hoc requirements of end‑user’ IT infrastructure?
a: Support is entirely reactive and slow with no formal tracking of end-user requests.
A basic helpdesk handles requests manually with inconsistent resolution times.
Standardized service catalogs allow users to log requests with defined SLA tracking.
Automated self-service portals and zero-touch provisioning handle the majority of hardware requests.
Predictive analytics identify and resolve device issues before the end-user even notices a problem.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective and efficient End-User IT support
QUESTIONS.q: How effectively and efficiently do you support the end‑users’ applications needs?
a: Application support is ad-hoc with users frequently bypassing IT for fixes.
IT provides basic support for core applications but lacks specialized expertise for modern apps.
A dedicated team manages application support with documented troubleshooting and update guides.
Application lifecycles are managed through automated deployment and self-service software centers.
Users are empowered with automated help and AI assistants that resolve app issues instantly.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective and efficient End-User IT support
QUESTIONS.q: How do you securely provide support to your remote workforce?
a: Remote support is difficult and insecure; often requiring users to return to the office.
Support is provided via basic VPN and remote desktop tools with manual intervention.
Modern remote management tools (e.g. Intune) provide secure support regardless of user location.
Secure remote support is integrated into the workspace with automated health checks and identity verification.
A global zero-trust support model provides seamless secure assistance and device recovery anywhere in the world.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective and efficient End-User IT support
QUESTIONS.q: How responsive and consistent is end‑user support?
a: Response times are long and unpredictable with high variation in quality between staff.
Support is consistent for basic issues but complex problems often go unresolved.
Defined SLAs and KPIs drive consistent response and resolution times for all support tiers.
Data-driven support queues prioritize issues based on business impact with high first-call resolution.
AI-driven support triage and resolution provide near-instant consistent responses for all common requirements.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective and efficient support to Applications/DevOps
QUESTIONS.q: How effectively and efficiently does IT support business‑critical applications?
a: Critical apps are managed reactively with significant downtime during updates or failures.
Standard maintenance is performed but the relationship between IT and App owners is siloed.
Defined service levels and regular health checks ensure stability for all business-critical apps.
Proactive application monitoring and automated scaling ensure high performance and availability.
Applications are managed as modern digital products with continuous integration and zero-downtime deployments.
QUESTIONS.fr: When business-critical applications fail, the business stops. Reliable support for these systems is the core promise IT makes to the business — without it, no other digital capability matters.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective and efficient support to Applications/DevOps
QUESTIONS.q: How effective is the RACI between the IT Infrastructure and Application teams (DevOps)?
a: No formal RACI exists; teams often blame each other during outages or deployments.
Roles are documented but communication is infrequent leading to friction during complex changes.
A clear RACI is in place with regular joint planning meetings between Infrastructure and App teams.
Teams utilize shared tools and automated pipelines to coordinate infrastructure and application changes.
A true DevOps culture exists with cross-functional teams owning the entire lifecycle from code to production.

QUESTIONS.trait: Assured continuity of operations in the event of outages, hacks and other disruptions
QUESTIONS.q: Do you perform Business Continuity planning for continued operating during an outage [for any reason]?
a: No formal BCP exists; the organization relies on best effort during an outage.
An informal BCP exists but is not documented or shared across the organization.
A documented BCP identifies critical business functions and manual workarounds during failure.
BCP is integrated into operations with regular staff training and annual tabletop simulations.
Resilience is built into the business model with seamless transitions to alternative operating modes.
QUESTIONS.fr: Outages are inevitable — from cyber events, suppliers, sites or people. A documented BC plan is what converts an existential event into a managed one; without it, a single disruption can threaten the company.

QUESTIONS.trait: Assured continuity of operations in the event of outages, hacks and other disruptions
QUESTIONS.q: Do you have defined, achievable and validated RTO/RPO objectives for critical systems, based on the business impact if a major system is unavailable?
a: Recovery targets are undefined and have never been discussed with business owners.
General recovery targets exist but are not validated against actual technical capabilities.
Specific RTO/RPO targets are documented for all critical systems and agreed upon by the business.
Recovery targets are technically validated through quarterly testing to ensure requirements are met.
Near-zero RTO/RPO targets are achieved through continuous replication and automated failover.

QUESTIONS.trait: Assured continuity of operations in the event of outages, hacks and other disruptions
QUESTIONS.q: Do you have immutable, recoverable and regularly tested backups of all data and systems?
a: Backups are inconsistent manual and have not been tested for recovery in over a year.
Automated daily backups exist but are stored on-site and are vulnerable to ransomware.
Off-site automated backups are performed daily with monthly sample recovery tests to verify integrity.
Immutable air-gapped backups protect against ransomware with automated success-rate reporting.
Continuous multi-region data protection ensures backups are always available and instantly recoverable.
QUESTIONS.fr: Tested, immutable backups are the last line of defence against ransomware, corruption and accidental deletion. Without them, every other resilience and security control is one bad day away from catastrophic data loss.

QUESTIONS.trait: Assured continuity of operations in the event of outages, hacks and other disruptions
QUESTIONS.q: Do you have regularly tested High Availability  and Disaster Recovery plans, using techniques such as Chaos Engineering & Stress Testing, which achieve your RTO/RPO objectives?
a: No HA or DR plans exist; recovery is manual and untested.
Basic DR plans exist but are not regularly tested or aligned to business requirements.
High Availability is configured for critical systems with annual DR testing to validate targets.
Automated failover and regular chaos engineering exercises ensure recovery targets are always met.
Resilience is continuous and self-healing with automated recovery validation across all regions.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective cyber security to protect the company’s data, IP and infrastructure from current and evolving threats
QUESTIONS.q: Do you perform structured cyber risk identification utilizing a comprehensive asset inventory and risk register; and update on a regular basis?
a: Risk identification is non-existent; assets are not tracked and no risk register exists.
Partial asset lists exist but are not linked to a formal risk assessment process.
A comprehensive asset inventory and risk register are maintained and reviewed quarterly.
Automated asset discovery and continuous risk assessments are integrated into the security posture.
Real-time asset intelligence and risk-scoring drive proactive security investments and response.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective cyber security to protect the company’s data, IP and infrastructure from current and evolving threats
QUESTIONS.q: How effective and tightly controlled are your identity and access management systems?
a: Passwords are weak MFA is not enforced and former employees retain access indefinitely.
MFA is used for some systems but identity management remains manual and decentralized.
Centralized identity (SSO) and MFA are enforced for all users and critical applications.
Conditional Access policies and Just-In-Time admin rights ensure secure risk-based access control.
Full Zero-Trust architecture continuously verifies every access request based on full device context.
QUESTIONS.fr: Identity is the modern perimeter. Strong IAM — MFA, least privilege, joiner/mover/leaver — is the foundation that virtually every other security control assumes is already in place.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective cyber security to protect the company’s data, IP and infrastructure from current and evolving threats
QUESTIONS.q: Have you defined and implemented vulnerability and patch management processes?
a: Patching is performed manually and irregularly with no tracking of vulnerabilities.
Basic automated patching is in place for OS but third-party apps remain unmanaged.
A formal patch management policy ensures all critical systems are updated monthly.
Vulnerability scanning and automated patching are integrated into a risk-based remediation workflow.
Real-time vulnerability orchestration automatically remediates threats across all endpoints and cloud resources.
QUESTIONS.fr: The majority of breaches exploit known, already-patchable vulnerabilities. Disciplined patch and vulnerability management is basic cyber hygiene; without it, investment in more advanced controls is largely wasted.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective cyber security to protect the company’s data, IP and infrastructure from current and evolving threats
QUESTIONS.q: Do you perform continuous threat detection, monitoring and posture management, capable of countering the current and evolving threat landscape?
a: Threat detection is non-existent; IT learns of breaches from external sources.
Basic antivirus and log checking provide limited visibility into security events.
A centralized SOC or SIEM provides 24/7 monitoring and alerting for critical security events.
XDR and automated threat hunting provide proactive detection and response across the environment.
AI-driven security operations provide autonomous threat detection and posture management in real-time.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective cyber security to protect the company’s data, IP and infrastructure from current and evolving threats
QUESTIONS.q: Do you have a documented and tested incident response capability?
a: No incident response plan exists; breaches are handled ad-hoc by IT.
A basic IR plan is documented but has never been tested with stakeholders.
A formal IR plan defines roles and procedures with annual tabletop testing.
Automated IR playbooks and a 24/7 response partner ensure rapid containment of breaches.
Incident response is integrated into a continuous improvement cycle using AI to predict and block attacks.
QUESTIONS.fr: A security or major operational incident is a question of when, not if. A documented and rehearsed IR plan is what separates a contained event from a public crisis — and is increasingly required by insurers and regulators.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective cyber security to protect the company’s data, IP and infrastructure from current and evolving threats
QUESTIONS.q: Is cyber security awareness embedded, and owned, across the organization?
a: No security training is provided; users are unaware of common threats like phishing.
Annual security training is mandated but there is no measurement of behavior change.
Regular phishing simulations and monthly awareness updates drive a foundational security culture.
Security is a shared responsibility with department champions and clear reporting of suspicious activity.
A proactive security-first culture is embedded in all business processes driven by executive leadership.

QUESTIONS.trait: Modern Digital Workplace, Experience & Adoption
QUESTIONS.q: How well does IT train and enable the workforce to utilize all available technology assets to work productively, effectively, securely and flexibly?
a: Training is non-existent; users struggle to use basic productivity tools effectively.
Ad-hoc training is provided but lacks a cohesive strategy for modern ways of working.
A formal digital enablement program provides targeted training for M365 and remote work.
Proactive coaching and data-driven learning paths drive high productivity across all digital tools.
IT is a business enabler providing seamless onboarding and advanced digital literacy for every role.

QUESTIONS.trait: Modern Digital Workplace, Experience & Adoption
QUESTIONS.q: Do you proactively monitor and measure employee experience and use of (current and) of available technology assets ?
a: Employee experience is not measured; IT is unaware of user frustration.
Ad-hoc surveys provide occasional feedback on user satisfaction with IT.
Standard DEX tools monitor device health and basic application performance.
Comprehensive telemetry measures digital friction and employee sentiment across the entire estate.
AI-driven insights proactively improve the digital experience before employees encounter friction.

QUESTIONS.trait: Modern Digital Workplace, Experience & Adoption
QUESTIONS.q: Do you understand and address the challenges in workforce adoption and use of available technology assets?
a: Adoption challenges are ignored; technology is deployed without regard for user use.
IT is aware of low adoption but lacks a formal process to address it.
A formal change management process identifies and addresses adoption barriers for new tools.
Data-driven adoption programs target specific groups to maximize the value of existing tech.
Continuous feedback loops and AI-assisted support ensure frictionless adoption of all new capabilities.

QUESTIONS.trait: Comprehensive use of Automation as appropriate
QUESTIONS.q: What is IT’s role in process automation?
a: IT has no role in automation; business processes are manual or siloed.
IT provides basic tools for automation but does not lead or support initiatives.
IT leads a centralized automation team that delivers projects for specific business units.
IT provides a governed platform for citizen developers to build their own automations.
IT is an automation-first organization where every repeatable process is candidates for AI/RPA.

QUESTIONS.trait: Comprehensive use of Automation as appropriate
QUESTIONS.q: Is there a systematic, comprehensive approach to identify, prioritize, plan and prosecute potential areas of automation across the company?
a: Automation opportunities are identified ad-hoc with no formal prioritization.
A list of potential automation projects exists but lacks a structured delivery plan.
A formal automation roadmap prioritizes projects based on complexity and business benefit.
A center of excellence (CoE) proactively identifies and delivers automation across all departments.
Automation is core to the business strategy with a continuous pipeline of high-impact initiatives.

QUESTIONS.trait: Comprehensive use of Automation as appropriate
QUESTIONS.q: What have been and what are projected to be the ROI with each discrete automation initiative?
a: ROI for automation is not measured or tracked.
Basic time-savings are estimated but not validated after implementation.
Standardized ROI models track the financial and operational impact of all major automations.
Comprehensive value tracking measures hard and soft savings across the entire automation portfolio.
Automation ROI is visible in real-time with continuous optimization of bots and workflows for value.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective approach for reviewing Innovation & AI to provide for performance improvement and competitive differentiation
QUESTIONS.q: Is there a defined IT Innovation / AI strategy, encompassing governance and responsible use (including personal AI accounts with company data), tied to clear business goals?
a: No AI strategy exists; users utilize shadow AI without any governance.
A high-level AI policy exists but is not linked to business goals or technical controls.
A formal AI strategy defines governed use cases and ethical guardrails for the business.
The AI strategy is integrated into the business roadmap with specific investment in secure AI platforms.
Innovation is constant with AI embedded in the business model under autonomous ethical governance.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective approach for reviewing Innovation & AI to provide for performance improvement and competitive differentiation
QUESTIONS.q: Does you provide AI (and other innovation tools) to all users outside of devops, such that end users can build, deploy, test, iterate, and learn from AI failures? Is AI AI integrated into real workflows, secure and delivering measurable outcomes?
a: AI access is restricted to IT or blocked entirely for general users.
Users have access to public AI tools but with no integration into business workflows.
Enterprise AI tools (e.g. Copilot) are provided with basic training for standard users.
AI is integrated into core business workflows with secure sandboxes for user experimentation.
Every employee is an innovator with AI assistants co-creating value across all secure workflows.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective approach for reviewing Innovation & AI to provide for performance improvement and competitive differentiation
QUESTIONS.q: Does you provide "Just-Enough-Access" to ensure that LLMs don't index and expose sensitive or over-shared internal data while fostering a "Citizen Developer" community where business users build their own AI-driven automations under IT guardrails?
a: Data access is ungoverned; AI models risk exposing sensitive internal information.
Basic permissions are in place but data over-sharing remains a significant risk.
Data classification and sensitivity labels protect sensitive info from being indexed by AI.
A zero-trust data model and governed Power Platform enable safe citizen development at scale.
AI-driven governance automatically protects sensitive data while allowing full innovation freedom.

QUESTIONS.trait: Effective approach for reviewing Innovation & AI to provide for performance improvement and competitive differentiation
QUESTIONS.q: Do you measure the specific value AI delivers (e.g., time saved)?
a: AI value is not measured; use is considered experimental only.
Qualitative feedback is collected on AI use but lacks financial metrics.
Standardized metrics track time-savings and productivity gains from AI tool adoption.
Comprehensive value-realization dashboards track AI impact on business growth and innovation.
AI value is measured in real-time as a core driver of business profitability and performance.

QUESTIONS.trait: Comprehensive, Usable, Data Intelligence, Analytics and Information Management
QUESTIONS.q: Do you have a single source of truth, centralized data repository (e.g., Data Warehouse or Data Lake) that is used as the primary source for reporting
a: Data is stored in disconnected spreadsheets and departmental silos with no centralized reporting.
A basic data repository exists but requires manual data entry and suffers from poor accuracy.
A centralized Data Warehouse or Lakehouse provides a single source of truth for all key reporting.
Advanced data pipelines automate the flow of real-time data into a unified analytics platform.
A self-healing data ecosystem provides a unified view of the business to all users and AI models.

QUESTIONS.trait: Comprehensive, Usable, Data Intelligence, Analytics and Information Management
QUESTIONS.q: Have you categorized your data (e.g., as IP or PII) and implemented access and data management systems appropriately allow access to your data?
a: Data is not categorized; access is granted ad-hoc with high risk of exposure.
Manual data classification is attempted but is incomplete and rarely updated.
A formal data classification policy is enforced with automated tagging for sensitive data.
Identity-governed data access ensures only authorized users can see sensitive IP or PII.
AI-driven data management automatically classifies and secures all data throughout its lifecycle.

QUESTIONS.trait: Comprehensive, Usable, Data Intelligence, Analytics and Information Management
QUESTIONS.q: Is the movement of data from source systems (ERP, CRM) to the warehouse automated and monitored for failure?
a: Data movement is manual; involving fragmented exports and spreadsheet imports.
Basic ETL scripts move data but lack centralized monitoring or failure alerting.
Automated data pipelines move data nightly with central monitoring and IT alerts.
Real-time data streaming and automated error remediation ensure data is always fresh and accurate.
Autonomous data orchestration manages all pipelines with self-healing capabilities for zero downtime.

QUESTIONS.trait: Comprehensive, Usable, Data Intelligence, Analytics and Information Management
QUESTIONS.q: Can business users easily and effectively “self-service” with AI and other tools to do their own analysis and reporting (e.g., via Power BI) using the centralized data repository ?
a: Users must request all reports from IT leading to long delays and manual workarounds.
Some users have access to BI tools but lack the training or clean data to be effective.
Certified datasets and standardized BI tools allow trained users to build their own reports.
A robust self-service portal provides all users with access to pre-modeled data and AI insights.
Data is democratized with users building sophisticated governed analytics and automations independently.

QUESTIONS.trait: Comprehensive, Usable, Data Intelligence, Analytics and Information Management
QUESTIONS.q: Have you implemented an effective, policy-driven approach to Data Retention & Archiving?
a: No data retention policy exists; all data is kept indefinitely by default.
A formal retention policy is documented but relies on manual deletion by users.
Policy-driven automated retention and archiving are implemented for core business systems.
Comprehensive data lifecycle management automatically archives or deletes data based on risk and value.
AI-driven data governance optimizes retention to minimize risk and storage costs automatically.

QUESTIONS.trait: Comprehensive, Usable, Data Intelligence, Analytics and Information Management
QUESTIONS.q: Can you articulate the specific financial value or cost-savings derived from data-driven decisions and user-led analysis?
a: The business cannot identify any financial benefit from its data and analysis.
Anecdotal evidence suggests some data-driven improvements but lacks hard data.
Specific business cases track the financial impact of major data and analytics projects.
A formal value-realization framework measures the ROI of all data-led business decisions.
Data is treated as a strategic balance-sheet asset with real-time value tracking and growth.

RECS.r: Get IT leadership a standing invite to the monthly business leadership meeting
RECS.b: Stops IT learning about decisions weeks after they're made — even passive presence shifts the dynamic.
RECS.i: Ask the CEO or COO for a recurring seat (even read-only) at the monthly leadership meeting. Send the IT lead, not a deputy. Take notes on every decision with a tech implication and circulate them back into IT planning the next day.

RECS.r: Build a one-page register of every active IT initiative
RECS.b: You can't align work you can't see — visibility is the first step out of pure firefighting.
RECS.i: Spend a week pulling together a single list of every project, vendor engagement, and BAU stream IT is currently delivering. Capture cost, owner, and a one-line description of what it's for. Share it back to leadership as a baseline.

RECS.r: Schedule quarterly catch-ups between the IT lead and each business unit head
RECS.b: Creates a low-effort feedback channel so business priorities surface earlier than crisis.
RECS.i: Block 30 minutes per BU lead each quarter with a simple agenda: what's working, what's frustrating, what's coming. No slides. Capture three takeaways per session and feed them into the IT plan.

RECS.r: Open a single intake channel for business teams to flag tech needs
RECS.b: Replaces shoulder-tapping and shadow IT with one front door, even if the back end is still informal.
RECS.i: Stand up a shared mailbox or simple form where any business team can raise a tech need. Triage weekly. Acknowledge every request within 48 hours, even if the answer is "not yet" — the responsiveness is what builds trust.

RECS.r: Start each IT team meeting with a 5-minute business context update
RECS.b: Plants the seed of business literacy across IT — alignment can't happen if the team doesn't know what the business is doing.
RECS.i: Have the IT lead share recent business news, customer wins, or strategic announcements at the start of every team meeting. Pull from the leadership meeting notes. Over time, the team will start asking the right questions about their own work.

RECS.r: Secure IT a permanent seat at the annual strategic planning cycle
RECS.b: Moves IT from informed-after to involved-during — where priorities are still shapeable.
RECS.i: Negotiate with the executive team for IT leadership to participate in the annual planning offsite and the quarterly refresh. Bring a tech-implications view of the draft strategy and a capacity view of what IT can deliver against it.

RECS.r: Establish a quarterly tech-strategy forum chaired by an executive sponsor
RECS.b: Creates a structured cadence where business and IT priorities are negotiated, not assumed.
RECS.i: Set up a 90-minute quarterly forum with the COO or equivalent in the chair. Standing agenda: portfolio review, capacity vs demand, emerging risks, decisions needed. Document and circulate decisions within 48 hours.

RECS.r: Embed IT business analysts into major BU project teams
RECS.b: Puts a tech-aware voice inside business decision-making instead of bolted on at the end.
RECS.i: Identify your strongest IT BAs and second them part-time into the 3–5 most strategically important business initiatives. Their job is to surface tech implications early — not to deliver code. Review the placements quarterly.

RECS.r: Train IT leaders in business-outcome framing
RECS.b: IT can't credibly participate in planning if its leaders speak only in technical terms.
RECS.i: Run a structured 2-day workshop for IT managers and above on financial fluency, business model literacy, and outcome storytelling. Reinforce with a monthly book club or peer review of executive communications.

RECS.r: Publish a quarterly tech-priorities one-pager aligned to the business plan
RECS.b: Forces IT to explicitly map its own work to business priorities and gives leadership something concrete to react to.
RECS.i: At the start of each quarter, publish a one-pager listing IT's top 5–7 priorities and the business goals they support. Distribute to the leadership team. Use their pushback to refine alignment for the next quarter.

RECS.r: Mandate a documented business outcome and KPI for every funded initiative
RECS.b: Eliminates fuzzy alignment — if you can't name the KPI it moves, it doesn't get funded.
RECS.i: Update the project intake template to require a named business owner, the specific KPI the work will improve, and the expected magnitude of impact. Reject incomplete submissions. Audit funded work quarterly to confirm the linkage still holds.

RECS.r: Build a portfolio dashboard linking IT spend to business KPI movement
RECS.b: Gives leadership one view of whether the IT portfolio is actually shifting the metrics it claimed to.
RECS.i: Combine finance spend data, project status, and the relevant business KPIs into a single monthly dashboard. Show trailing impact for completed work and projected impact for in-flight. Review with the executive team monthly.

RECS.r: Require a named business sponsor accountable for every initiative's outcome
RECS.b: Stops post-go-live projects drifting because nobody on the business side owned the result.
RECS.i: For every funded project, name a business sponsor (not IT) explicitly accountable for the outcome — measured 6 and 12 months post go-live. Build that accountability into their performance objectives, not just the project charter.

RECS.r: Run formal post-implementation reviews on every major initiative
RECS.b: Creates a feedback loop that improves the quality of business cases over time.
RECS.i: Six and twelve months after go-live, run a structured review comparing actual KPI movement to the original business case. Publish findings to the leadership team. Use systematic gaps to recalibrate future estimates and intake decisions.

RECS.r: Tie IT leadership scorecards to business KPI delivery, not just project delivery
RECS.b: Aligns personal incentives with business outcome — closes the loop between IT effort and business result.
RECS.i: Add 2–3 business KPIs (revenue, cost, customer experience) to senior IT scorecards. Weight them at 30–50%. Review against actuals at the same cadence as business unit leaders. The CIO should be measured the same way the COO is.

RECS.r: Stand up an IT-led innovation pod with authority to prototype new revenue concepts
RECS.b: Moves IT from supporting the business plan to actively shaping it — generating opportunities, not just enabling them.
RECS.i: Create a small (4–6 person) cross-functional pod reporting to the CIO with a ringfenced budget and a quarterly mandate to prototype one new digital revenue or customer-experience concept. Show outputs at executive forums and graduate winners into the main portfolio.

RECS.r: Make the CIO co-accountable for enterprise growth metrics
RECS.b: Embeds tech leadership inside the growth conversation — strategy can't be tech-led if the CIO is only measured on IT delivery.
RECS.i: Add top-of-house growth metrics (revenue, market share, NPS) to the CIO scorecard alongside the COO and CRO. Review at the same board cadence. Pair with explicit authority over digital products and customer experience platforms.

RECS.r: Build a continuous tech-trend scanning function feeding into corporate strategy
RECS.b: Ensures the strategy refresh is informed by emerging capability, not just market dynamics.
RECS.i: Stand up a small horizon-scanning team (or partner with a research firm) that produces a quarterly view of emerging tech relevant to your industry. Feed findings into corporate strategy reviews. Track which trends become initiatives over time.

RECS.r: Run portfolio reviews with authority to redirect business strategy based on tech opportunity
RECS.b: Makes the strategy genuinely two-way — tech opportunity can rewrite the plan, not just respond to it.
RECS.i: At least twice a year, run a portfolio review where the executive team can reallocate strategic focus based on what tech makes newly possible. Document strategy changes triggered by tech findings as a leading indicator of maturity.

RECS.r: Measure and publish tech-driven revenue and competitive differentiation as a board KPI
RECS.b: What gets measured at the board level gets prioritised — making this visible cements tech as a strategic driver.
RECS.i: Define a metric (% revenue from products less than 3 years old, % customers using digital channels, time-to-market vs competitors) and report it to the board quarterly alongside financials. Tie it to executive incentives at the top of the house.

RECS.r: Capture the current technology landscape on a single page
RECS.b: You can't plan forward if you can't see what you've already got.
RECS.i: Spend a week pulling together one diagram or spreadsheet listing every system, vendor, and major commitment IT is currently running. Don't aim for perfection — aim for visible. Share it with leadership as the starting point.

RECS.r: Write down the top 5 things IT is trying to deliver in the next 12 months
RECS.b: A simple shared list beats no plan and ends the 'what is IT actually doing' conversation.
RECS.i: List the 5 most important pieces of work IT is committed to over the next 12 months. Include a sentence on why each one matters. Distribute to leadership. Refresh quarterly. The list becomes a baseline for harder roadmap conversations later.

RECS.r: Run a short conversation with each business unit head about their pain points
RECS.b: Surfaces what's actually broken before you commit to fixing the wrong things.
RECS.i: Book 30 minutes with each BU lead. Ask one question: what's the single biggest tech-related frustration you have right now? Capture answers verbatim. Use the patterns to inform what makes the next iteration of the list.

RECS.r: Establish a monthly check-in where IT shares progress against the list
RECS.b: Builds the habit of reporting against a plan, even before the plan is sophisticated.
RECS.i: Hold a 30-minute monthly forum with leadership covering progress on the top-5 list, blockers, and any new asks. Even an informal cadence builds the muscle of reporting against commitment — the structure can mature later.

RECS.r: Stop new tech work from starting unless it appears on the list
RECS.b: Disrupts the pattern of every request becoming a project regardless of priority.
RECS.i: Push back politely on requests that don't fit the top-5 list. Track them in a parking lot rather than rejecting outright. The discipline forces real prioritisation conversations and makes capacity visible to the requester.

RECS.r: Run structured interviews with each BU to capture top three priorities
RECS.b: Anchors the roadmap in real business needs, not IT's view of what matters.
RECS.i: Run 60-minute structured interviews with each BU lead to capture their three most important outcomes for the next 12–18 months. Synthesise themes into a single list and validate with the executive team before roadmap planning.

RECS.r: Map every roadmap item to a specific business priority
RECS.b: Forces every line of the roadmap to defend itself against business value, not just technical merit.
RECS.i: For each roadmap item, document the business priority it serves and a one-line success criterion. Items that can't be mapped get either reframed or removed. The exercise reveals how much of IT's work has been driven by technical pull rather than business pull.

RECS.r: Group roadmap work into clear horizons (now / next / later)
RECS.b: Replaces a flat list with a portfolio view that supports real prioritisation discussions.
RECS.i: Sort roadmap items into three time horizons: now (in flight), next (next 6 months), later (6–18 months). Make the now horizon capacity-bound so it can't grow without something else moving out. This visualisation is what makes trade-offs concrete.

RECS.r: Set up quarterly roadmap review forums with business sponsors
RECS.b: Builds the cadence that keeps the roadmap a live artefact rather than an annual document.
RECS.i: Hold a 90-minute quarterly review with business sponsors covering portfolio status, trade-offs, and changes. Use a consistent template so trends are visible across quarters. Capture decisions in writing and circulate within 48 hours.

RECS.r: Publish a one-page summary of the roadmap accessible to all staff
RECS.b: Removes the sense that IT priorities are a black box and invites the right kind of feedback.
RECS.i: Distil the roadmap into a one-page summary using business language. Publish on the intranet or staff portal. Update at each quarterly review. Visibility at this level builds trust and surfaces useful pushback before it becomes a complaint.

RECS.r: Mandate documented business outcomes and measurable success criteria for every initiative
RECS.b: Turns a list of projects into a portfolio with traceable, defensible value.
RECS.i: Update the roadmap template to require, for each item: named business owner, specific KPI that will move, target movement, and measurement approach. Reject incomplete entries. Refresh the mapping at every quarterly review and validate against actuals.

RECS.r: Build a roadmap dashboard linking initiatives to KPI movement
RECS.b: Provides one view of whether the roadmap is delivering on the value it promised.
RECS.i: Combine project status, business KPI data, and value forecasts in a single live dashboard. Refresh monthly. Shows where on-track delivery is moving the right metrics — and where on-track delivery isn't shifting anything, which is the harder conversation.

RECS.r: Run formal post-implementation reviews 6–12 months after delivery
RECS.b: Closes the loop between forecast and reality, improving the quality of future business cases.
RECS.i: Six and twelve months after every major go-live, compare actual KPI movement to the original case. Publish findings to the steering committee. Use systematic forecast gaps to recalibrate intake decisions and challenge consistently optimistic owners.

RECS.r: Allow the roadmap to be reprioritised based on KPI performance
RECS.b: Ensures the roadmap stays responsive to real-world signal rather than locked to original plans.
RECS.i: At each quarterly review, use KPI movement data to redirect, accelerate, or retire roadmap items. Communicate every change with the rationale. Static roadmaps quickly drift from value; dynamic reprioritisation is what keeps them honest.

RECS.r: Tie technical milestone reporting to business KPI movement
RECS.b: Stops 'on track' status from masking projects that aren't actually moving the dial.
RECS.i: In status reports, pair every technical milestone with its associated business KPI status. Highlight cases where delivery is on track but the KPI isn't moving — that's the leading indicator of a flawed business case that needs intervention before more spend lands.

RECS.r: Run continuous portfolio rebalancing rather than fixed annual cycles
RECS.b: Treats the roadmap as a living asset that responds to opportunity in real time.
RECS.i: Move from annual roadmap setting to continuous rebalancing — monthly or more often for the most strategic horizon. Empower a small portfolio team to redirect capacity and budget within agreed guardrails. Annual cycles become too slow once the rest of the business is moving fast.

RECS.r: Embed competitive intelligence into roadmap decisions
RECS.b: Ensures the roadmap captures opportunity created by what competitors are or aren't doing.
RECS.i: Stand up a small horizon-scanning function (or partner with a research firm) that produces a quarterly view of competitor moves, emerging tech, and adjacent industries. Feed findings directly into roadmap reviews. Track which signals translated into roadmap changes.

RECS.r: Use real-time business and customer data to drive roadmap pivots
RECS.b: Pulls roadmap decisions toward customer outcome rather than internal opinion.
RECS.i: Wire customer behaviour, NPS, churn, and revenue signals into the roadmap dashboard. When the data moves, the roadmap moves. Pair with explicit triggers — e.g. NPS dropping more than 5 points triggers a portfolio review — so the data drives action.

RECS.r: Make the roadmap a board-level competitive differentiator
RECS.b: Lifts the roadmap from internal planning artefact to a tool the board uses to assess strategic fit.
RECS.i: Present the IT roadmap quarterly to the board, framed in competitive and strategic terms. Show how it positions the business against market shifts. The board's questions and pushback shape the roadmap; the roadmap shapes the board's view of where the business is heading.

RECS.r: Build customer co-design into roadmap items where the work serves customer outcomes
RECS.b: Ensures customer-facing roadmap work is shaped with customers, not just for them.
RECS.i: For roadmap items targeting customer outcomes, build co-design into the cycle — interviews, beta cohorts, advisory boards. Use the input to refine scope before commitment. Significantly reduces the rework cost of building the wrong thing.

RECS.r: Map who currently owns each piece of the technology estate
RECS.b: You can't fix fragmented ownership until you can see the fragments.
RECS.i: Spend a week documenting which department or person currently makes decisions for each major system, vendor, and platform. Don't try to change anything yet — just produce the map. The picture itself is usually enough to start the conversation.

RECS.r: Acknowledge departmental ownership where it already exists
RECS.b: Trying to centralise everything at once provokes resistance; recognising current reality buys cooperation.
RECS.i: Where business units already own and run their own technology decisions, formally acknowledge it. Document the boundaries. The acknowledgement removes the friction that comes from IT trying to grab control before it has earned the right to.

RECS.r: Set up a quarterly inter-departmental tech catch-up
RECS.b: Surfaces conflicts and overlaps before they become incidents or duplicated spend.
RECS.i: Convene a 90-minute quarterly forum with each department's tech lead. Standing agenda: what's coming, where you need help, where you might step on each other. Light-touch coordination beats heavy-handed governance at this stage.

RECS.r: Identify the top three areas where uncoordinated decisions are causing friction
RECS.b: Focuses energy on the worst pain points rather than trying to fix everything at once.
RECS.i: Talk to staff and managers across the org. Identify the top three places where departmental autonomy is producing visible problems — duplicate vendors, integration breakage, security gaps. Use these as the case for the next maturity step.

RECS.r: Capture all departmental roadmaps in one shared location
RECS.b: Visibility is the precondition for coordination — even if ownership stays decentralised.
RECS.i: Ask each department to publish whatever roadmap they have to a shared location. Don't standardise format yet — visibility is the goal. Once everyone can see what everyone else is doing, alignment conversations become possible.

RECS.r: Designate a single accountable owner for the IT roadmap
RECS.b: Eliminates fragmented ownership that produces conflicting roadmaps.
RECS.i: Name one IT leader (typically the CIO or equivalent) as the accountable owner for the enterprise IT roadmap. Communicate the appointment broadly and route significant tech decisions through them. Explicit accountability is what enables the roadmap to be more than the sum of departmental wishlists.

RECS.r: Establish a structured input process from each business unit
RECS.b: Centralised ownership without business input becomes IT's plan, not the business's.
RECS.i: Build a defined process for each BU to submit input to the roadmap — quarterly business reviews, structured intake, captured in a shared template. The owner synthesises input but holds the pen. BUs feel heard; IT keeps coherence.

RECS.r: Document basic roles and responsibilities for the roadmap process
RECS.b: Removes ambiguity about who decides, who delivers, and who must be consulted.
RECS.i: Build a one-page RACI covering roadmap creation, prioritisation, and execution. Validate it with named individuals — not just job titles. Publish it in the governance pack. Review annually as the org evolves.

RECS.r: Run an annual roadmap-setting cycle aligned to business planning
RECS.b: Synchronises IT planning with business planning rather than running in parallel.
RECS.i: Build the annual roadmap as a derived artefact of business planning — IT leadership participates in business planning, then refreshes the roadmap to support it. Communicate the calendar so each BU knows when their input is needed.

RECS.r: Make the IT lead a regular contributor to executive discussions
RECS.b: Centralised IT ownership requires the owner to actually be at the table where strategy is shaped.
RECS.i: Negotiate a standing slot for IT leadership at executive forums where strategy and priorities are discussed. Read-only initially is fine; over time, contribution depth grows. Without a seat, centralised IT ownership becomes order-taking dressed as strategy.

RECS.r: Establish formal co-ownership with named business sponsors per major initiative
RECS.b: Distributes accountability for outcomes, not just delivery — IT alone can't make business KPIs move.
RECS.i: For every major initiative, name a business sponsor accountable for the outcome alongside IT delivery accountability. Sponsors are senior, not nominal. Make accountability visible in governance reports. Outcomes improve when both sides own them.

RECS.r: Stand up a steering committee with cross-functional decision rights
RECS.b: Provides a single forum where major roadmap decisions get made and recorded.
RECS.i: Charter a steering committee with representation from each major business function plus IT leadership. Define a clear remit, decision rights, and meeting cadence. Empower it to approve, reject, or send back proposals. Publish minutes and decisions to the broader organisation.

RECS.r: Document and publish a formal roadmap governance framework
RECS.b: Removes ambiguity about how decisions get made and protects the roadmap from political reopening.
RECS.i: Document the roadmap governance framework: who proposes, who reviews, who approves, who reports. Cover funding decisions, prioritisation changes, scope changes, and exception handling. Publish broadly. Review annually for fitness.

RECS.r: Run quarterly co-ownership reviews with business sponsors
RECS.b: Keeps business sponsors actively engaged rather than nominal owners.
RECS.i: Each quarter, run a structured review with every business sponsor covering their initiative's status, KPI movement, risks, and decisions needed. Make absence visible. Sponsors who can't engage at this cadence aren't real sponsors and the role needs reassigning.

RECS.r: Tie ownership to scorecards on both the IT and business side
RECS.b: Aligns personal incentives with delivery and outcome on both sides of the partnership.
RECS.i: Build roadmap delivery and outcome metrics into the scorecards of both IT and business sponsors. Use both delivery (on time, on budget) and outcome (KPI movement) measures so neither is gamed. Performance reviews become a forcing function for engagement.

RECS.r: Establish CIO and COO as joint roadmap owners with shared scorecards
RECS.b: Eliminates the IT-vs-business divide at the most senior level — they're accountable together or not at all.
RECS.i: Make the senior IT leader and senior operations leader (or equivalent) jointly accountable for the roadmap, with shared scorecard metrics. Compensation and tenure tied to joint success. The structural alignment makes business-tech disputes resolve faster and at a more strategic level.

RECS.r: Make roadmap performance a board-level standing agenda item
RECS.b: Embeds tech accountability at the highest governance level alongside financial performance.
RECS.i: Add roadmap performance — both delivery and outcome KPIs — to the standing board agenda. Quarterly is typical. The board's expectations and pushback shape how seriously the executive team treats the roadmap. Reporting at this level disciplines internal behaviour.

RECS.r: Provide real-time roadmap visibility to all senior leaders
RECS.b: Removes information asymmetry that creates friction and lets leaders self-direct.
RECS.i: Build a live executive dashboard showing roadmap status, KPI movement, and resource allocation. Senior leaders can drill in anytime without asking. Transparency removes the need for formal escalation in most cases — leaders see and act on issues themselves.

RECS.r: Tie executive compensation to long-term roadmap value realisation
RECS.b: Aligns executive incentives with the multi-year nature of strategic technology investment.
RECS.i: Build long-term incentive plan components that vest based on multi-year value realisation from the roadmap — not just annual delivery milestones. Encourages investment in capability that takes 2–3 years to compound. Reduces the bias toward short-term wins.

RECS.r: Embed external advisors or non-executive directors with deep tech expertise
RECS.b: Brings outside-in challenge and competitive context to roadmap decisions.
RECS.i: Recruit non-executive directors or advisors with operational tech leadership backgrounds. Use them to challenge roadmap assumptions, validate strategic direction, and bring competitive intelligence. The external perspective protects against insular thinking.

RECS.r: Send a short weekly written update on what IT is working on
RECS.b: Even a basic written rhythm beats silence and starts to set expectations.
RECS.i: Send a short weekly email or message to the leadership team listing what's in progress, what's blocked, and what's coming next. Keep it under 200 words. The discipline matters more than the format — it builds the habit of reporting.

RECS.r: Capture incidents and outages in a simple shared log
RECS.b: Creates a record so failures don't disappear after the immediate firefighting.
RECS.i: Maintain a shared log of every incident, what was affected, how long it took to resolve, and what was done. Even a spreadsheet works. The log becomes the basis for trend conversations and demonstrates that IT is paying attention.

RECS.r: Hold informal monthly catch-ups with key stakeholders
RECS.b: Surfaces concerns and updates early, before they become formal escalations.
RECS.i: Schedule 30-minute monthly catch-ups with each major stakeholder. No fixed agenda — let the conversation flow. Capture three takeaways per meeting and follow up on action items. Builds relationship capital that supports more formal reporting later.

RECS.r: Document the major projects in flight on a single page
RECS.b: Replaces verbal updates with something stakeholders can reference.
RECS.i: Maintain a one-page document listing the top 5–10 projects in flight with owner, status, and next milestone. Update monthly. Send to leadership with a one-paragraph commentary. Even basic structure beats the status of work being known only by IT.

RECS.r: Surface failures and near-misses, not just successes
RECS.b: Builds credibility with leadership by being honest about reality.
RECS.i: In every update, include at least one thing that went wrong or nearly did. The discipline of disclosure builds trust faster than presenting only wins. Leaders who feel they're getting the truth become better partners on hard decisions.

RECS.r: Establish a regular monthly executive update cycle with consistent template
RECS.b: Creates a predictable rhythm so issues surface before they become crises.
RECS.i: Set a fixed monthly executive update covering progress, risks, and decisions needed. Use a consistent template so trends are visible across months. Keep it short — under 10 minutes of reading — and follow up on every action item.

RECS.r: Standardise status indicators across all initiatives
RECS.b: Makes portfolio health understandable at a glance, not buried in narrative.
RECS.i: Define crisp criteria for green, amber, and red status. Train owners to apply them consistently — amber means action is needed, not just 'we're worried'. Audit status calls quarterly to catch optimism bias before it becomes red surprise.

RECS.r: Hold quarterly steering committee reviews with full executive attendance
RECS.b: Ensures top-of-house attention at predictable intervals — drives serious engagement.
RECS.i: Schedule quarterly steering committee meetings with full executive attendance and a published agenda covering portfolio status, risk register, decisions needed, and exceptions. Capture decisions in writing and circulate within 48 hours.

RECS.r: Build an issues and risks log reviewed at every meeting
RECS.b: Tracks the things that could derail the portfolio rather than just status of what's going well.
RECS.i: Maintain a live issues and risks log reviewed at every governance forum. Each entry has owner, mitigation, and target close date. Stale entries are red flags — risks that aren't moving need either action or formal acceptance.

RECS.r: Provide written meeting outputs within 48 hours
RECS.b: Decisions and actions only count if they're captured and circulated.
RECS.i: After every governance meeting, produce a 1-page summary of decisions, actions, and owners within 48 hours. Distribute to attendees and the broader leadership team. Without written outputs, decisions get reinterpreted later or quietly forgotten.

RECS.r: Build a real-time portfolio dashboard accessible to all stakeholders
RECS.b: Replaces monthly slide decks with always-current visibility.
RECS.i: Connect project tooling, finance data, and KPI sources into a single live dashboard. Stakeholders self-serve rather than waiting for the next monthly cycle. Eliminates the deck-prep tax on the IT team and significantly raises engagement quality.

RECS.r: Include business value realised, not just delivery progress
RECS.b: Stops 'delivered' projects masking the fact no value has been captured.
RECS.i: For every initiative past go-live, track and report business value actually realised against forecast. If value isn't measurable, that's a flag for the business case. Use the data to refine future cases — initiatives that consistently miss should change how they're sized.

RECS.r: Embed risk and dependency views in the dashboard
RECS.b: Surfaces the cross-cutting issues that single-project status hides.
RECS.i: Add views to the dashboard showing top risks across the portfolio, cross-initiative dependencies, and resource conflicts. Single-project status reports miss systemic issues; portfolio views catch them. Review at every governance forum.

RECS.r: Run a monthly executive review against the dashboard, not against decks
RECS.b: Forces conversations to be data-driven and exposes inconsistencies in real time.
RECS.i: Replace the monthly slide-deck review with a working session against the live dashboard. Drill in on flagged items together. Concerns get resolved or escalated in the room. Significantly faster cycle time than slide-mediated discussion.

RECS.r: Track decision cycle time as a governance KPI
RECS.b: Surfaces whether governance is producing timely decisions or becoming a blocker.
RECS.i: Measure how long decisions take from raised to resolved. Report quarterly. Long cycle times indicate governance bottlenecks that need addressing. Fast governance is a competitive advantage; slow governance hides as 'thoroughness'.

RECS.r: Provide automated, real-time portfolio reporting to the entire executive team
RECS.b: Eliminates the latency between status changing and leaders knowing.
RECS.i: Build automated reporting that updates in real time as project, finance, and KPI data changes. Push notifications for material status changes. Senior leaders see issues as they emerge, not in a monthly review. Allows action at the speed of opportunity.

RECS.r: Link IT investment reporting directly to business P&L
RECS.b: Closes the loop between technology spend and business outcome at the financial reporting level.
RECS.i: Build reporting that connects IT investment categories directly to P&L lines they support — revenue, customer acquisition cost, operational cost, risk reduction. Use it in financial reviews. Forces tech investment conversations to happen in the same language as the rest of the business.

RECS.r: Make ROI and value tracking visible to the board
RECS.b: Embeds tech value at the highest level of organisational accountability.
RECS.i: Include IT portfolio ROI and value realisation as a standing board metric — same visibility as financial KPIs. The board sees tech as a value lever, not a cost line. Drives different decision-making at the executive layer over time.

RECS.r: Use predictive analytics to flag at-risk initiatives before status changes
RECS.b: Catches issues earlier than human judgement alone reliably can.
RECS.i: Apply analytics to project history, sentiment data, and operational signals to predict initiatives at risk of slip or value miss. Surface as early warnings to sponsors. Pair with human judgement — the analytics flag, the humans decide. Earlier intervention means cheaper correction.

RECS.r: Provide self-service drill-down for any leader at any time
RECS.b: Removes the gatekeeping pattern where stakeholders depend on IT for visibility.
RECS.i: Let any senior leader drill into any initiative, vendor relationship, or risk in real time without asking. Train once, then trust the data. Builds the cultural expectation that information flows openly — central to a tech-led organisation.

RECS.r: Establish an annual IT budget with a clear total figure
RECS.b: Replaces emergency requests with a known boundary that focuses prioritisation.
RECS.i: Work with finance to set an annual IT budget — even if rough — covering projected run cost and known major projects. Get it signed off. Even imperfect budgeting beats reactive spending and forces explicit conversations about what's affordable.

RECS.r: Distinguish between run-the-business and change-the-business spend
RECS.b: Visibility on this split is the foundation for every later funding conversation.
RECS.i: Categorise every line of spend as 'run' (keep operations going) or 'change' (transformation, new capability). Don't try to perfect the split — produce a first cut and refine. The ratio itself is informative and starts to drive better conversations.

RECS.r: Identify and document major lifecycle replacements due in 24 months
RECS.b: Surfaces the upcoming bow-wave so it doesn't become an emergency.
RECS.i: List every major asset (hardware, licence renewals, contracts) due for replacement or renewal in the next 24 months with rough cost. Share with finance. Surfaces upcoming spend before it becomes a crisis ask and lets it be planned into the budget cycle.

RECS.r: Set up a basic approval threshold for unplanned spend
RECS.b: Stops every minor request becoming an executive escalation.
RECS.i: Agree with finance a threshold below which IT can authorise spend without escalation. Document and communicate. Reduces friction on small-but-necessary spend while preserving control on larger items.

RECS.r: Track actual spend against budget monthly
RECS.b: Even basic tracking starts the discipline of variance management.
RECS.i: Set up a simple monthly comparison of actual spend vs budget at category level. Flag significant variances. Don't aim for sophistication; aim for visibility. The cadence of monthly checks builds the muscle that supports more sophisticated FinOps later.

RECS.r: Set the annual IT budget centrally based on a documented plan
RECS.b: Removes the pattern of departments competing for IT spend without a shared framework.
RECS.i: Build a centrally-set annual IT budget driven by the roadmap and current run cost. Negotiate with each BU on their share. Get executive sign-off. Even imperfect central control beats fragmented departmental tech budgets that compound over time.

RECS.r: Distinguish maintenance, project, and lifecycle spend explicitly
RECS.b: Lets each category be governed differently — they have different drivers and risks.
RECS.i: In the budget, separate maintenance (BAU operating cost), project (new initiatives), and lifecycle (replacement of existing assets). Each gets its own forecast logic and governance. Treats them as different problems because they are.

RECS.r: Document the funding approval process from request to commitment
RECS.b: Removes ambiguity about how money flows and what's required to access it.
RECS.i: Document the approval process — request, business case, review, decision, funding release — including thresholds and timeframes. Publish broadly. Eliminates the pattern of insiders knowing how to get things funded while others wait in queue.

RECS.r: Run a monthly variance review with finance
RECS.b: Builds the discipline of structured spend management.
RECS.i: Schedule a monthly review with finance covering actual vs forecast at category and initiative level. Investigate variances above threshold. Use trend data to refine forecasting and challenge consistently optimistic owners. Builds the habit before it's mandatory.

RECS.r: Build a 12-month forecast that's refreshed quarterly
RECS.b: Allows for course-correction without waiting for annual cycles.
RECS.i: Maintain a rolling 12-month spend forecast refreshed quarterly. Reflects latest commitments, performance, and reprioritisations. Annual budgets become out of date within months; rolling forecasts stay useful all year.

RECS.r: Build a multi-year IT funding model with transformation/run/lifecycle visibility
RECS.b: Avoids reactive year-by-year scrambling and surprise lifecycle bow-waves.
RECS.i: Build a 3-year funding model showing transformation (capex), run (opex), and lifecycle replacement, anchored to the business strategy. Refresh annually as part of corporate planning. Use it to set realistic boundaries on what's committable in any given year.

RECS.r: Tie funding requests to business cases with quantified ROI
RECS.b: Forces every spend ask to defend itself on value, not just cost.
RECS.i: Require a standardised business case for any investment above a threshold (e.g. $100k). Include quantified benefits, ROI, and payback period. Reject incomplete cases. Run post-implementation reviews to validate whether forecasts were realistic.

RECS.r: Reserve 10–15% capacity for unplanned strategic opportunities
RECS.b: Lets you respond to market shifts without raiding committed work.
RECS.i: In the annual plan, ringfence 10–15% of IT budget as a strategic reserve. Govern it through a fast-track approval path so opportunities don't sit in limbo. Roll unspent reserve into the following year — don't let finance claw it back.

RECS.r: Implement portfolio governance to reallocate funds dynamically
RECS.b: Keeps money flowing to the highest-value work as conditions change.
RECS.i: Run a quarterly portfolio review with authority to pause, redirect, or accelerate funds. Use real performance data — not advocacy — to drive decisions. Communicate reallocations transparently so teams trust the process and don't hoard or pad.

RECS.r: Track post-implementation value realisation against business cases
