RECS.i: Build a self-service portal for standard hardware and software requests. Automate fulfilment where possible. For approved standard requests, the portal becomes the primary path; ad-hoc tickets become the exception that need human intervention.

RECS.r: Automate provisioning to reduce manual handling time
RECS.b: Frees support staff for higher-value work and cuts cycle time.
RECS.i: Automate the most common provisioning workflows — accounts, devices, software — end-to-end. Each manual workflow you eliminate compounds: less queue time, fewer errors, faster onboarding. Reinvest the freed capacity in higher-value support work.

RECS.r: Use predictive analytics to identify and resolve issues proactively
RECS.b: Stops issues escalating to user-impacting before they're caught.
RECS.i: Use telemetry from endpoints and applications to spot patterns predicting failure — disk health, app crashes, login latency. Trigger preemptive remediation. Users notice less when issues never surface; the data quietly does the work.

RECS.r: Build problem management discipline alongside incident management
RECS.b: Eliminates recurring incidents at root cause rather than re-resolving them.
RECS.i: Establish problem management — root cause analysis, permanent fixes, problem records. Incident management resolves; problem management prevents recurrence. The discipline is what stops support load from growing year on year as the environment grows.

RECS.r: Use AI assistants to handle common queries 24/7
RECS.b: Provides immediate response for routine queries and frees humans for complex work.
RECS.i: Deploy AI assistants for common queries (password reset, software requests, basic FAQs). Track deflection rate and accuracy. Used carefully, AI assistants are a force multiplier; used badly they push frustration to users. Start narrow and expand based on data.

RECS.r: Build AI-driven proactive support that prevents user-visible issues
RECS.b: At highest maturity, support is invisible — issues are resolved before users notice.
RECS.i: Build proactive support driven by behavioural and environmental telemetry — predicting issues, pre-staging fixes, communicating proactively. Mature companies' support load is fundamentally lower because most issues never become incidents. Users notice the absence of problems.

RECS.r: Tie support to product engineering for systematic root cause elimination
RECS.b: Eliminates whole classes of issues at the source.
RECS.i: Build feedback loops from support into product engineering. Support data drives product changes that eliminate issue classes. Mature companies treat support volume as a product quality metric; immature ones treat it as a support capacity issue.

RECS.r: Use NLP-driven service desk that handles complex queries naturally
RECS.b: Provides natural language interaction quality matching the best human agents.
RECS.i: Deploy advanced NLP-driven service desk capability that handles complex queries naturally — context-aware, multi-turn, escalating gracefully when needed. The technology has matured significantly; the user experience can match or exceed human agents for routine and moderate complexity.

RECS.r: Position support as part of customer/employee experience strategy
RECS.b: Reframes support from cost to experience driver.
RECS.i: Communicate support as part of customer or employee experience strategy — the ability to resolve issues quickly is part of what makes the experience excellent. The framing drives investment in capability rather than treating support as cost containment.

RECS.r: Treat support capability as recruiting and retention advantage
RECS.b: Excellent support is a tangible part of employee experience.
RECS.i: Use excellent IT support as part of employee value proposition — productivity uninterrupted, fast response, self-service that actually works. Particularly powerful for technology-dependent roles. The story attracts talent that's been frustrated elsewhere.

RECS.r: Identify a named owner for each application
RECS.b: Eliminates the orphan-app problem where no one is accountable.
RECS.i: For every application, identify a named technical owner. Document in a central register. The exercise often surfaces apps no one owns — these are the ones that fail spectacularly. Assigned ownership is the baseline before any other support practice can work.

RECS.r: Build a basic application inventory
RECS.b: Provides the foundation for any application management practice.
RECS.i: Build an inventory of every application in use — name, owner, business purpose, vendor, support contract status. Most organisations are surprised by the count. The inventory drives prioritisation, support coverage, and licence management. Without it, everything else is guesswork.

RECS.r: Document basic support contacts for each application
RECS.b: Reduces the panic of figuring out who to call when something breaks.
RECS.i: For each significant application, document the support contacts — internal owner, vendor support, key user contacts. Make accessible to support staff. Even basic contact lists save hours during incidents and reduce the dependency on one person remembering.

RECS.r: Triage application incidents through the main support channel
RECS.b: Provides a structured path even before specialised teams exist.
RECS.i: Route application incidents through the main support channel for triage and routing to the named owner. Document the routing rules. Even basic structured routing improves response over the 'whoever picks up the phone' pattern that plagues immature support.

RECS.r: Identify which apps have vendor support contracts and which don't
RECS.b: Surfaces the unsupported apps that pose the greatest risk.
RECS.i: Cross-reference applications with active vendor support contracts. Surface apps without support — these depend entirely on internal capability when issues arise. Prioritise either getting support contracts or building internal capability for the most critical of these.

RECS.r: Establish a dedicated application support team for critical apps
RECS.b: Builds deep expertise that generalist support can't match.
RECS.i: Stand up a dedicated application support function for critical apps with deep platform expertise. Define ownership boundaries with infrastructure and end-user support. Critical apps deserve named, accountable owners — generalist support produces generalist outcomes.

RECS.r: Document troubleshooting guides for top issues per application
RECS.b: Shortens MTTR and reduces dependency on individual experts.
RECS.i: For each critical app, document the top 10 issues with diagnostic and resolution steps. Update after every significant incident. Reduces MTTR and means new staff become productive faster. Knowledge that lives only in heads becomes a single point of failure.

RECS.r: Run regular reviews of application support metrics
RECS.b: Surfaces emerging hotspots and underperforming applications.
RECS.i: Each quarter, review application support metrics — ticket volume, MTTR, recurring issues, user satisfaction. Identify hotspots and address at root cause (vendor escalation, retraining, retiring an app). Reactive support without analytics never improves.

RECS.r: Establish change management discipline for applications
RECS.b: Reduces incidents caused by uncontrolled changes.
RECS.i: Implement change management for applications — change requests, peer review, scheduled windows, communications. Even basic discipline reduces change-induced incidents materially. Without it, changes happen in production with predictable results.

RECS.r: Build SLAs and response targets per application tier
RECS.b: Aligns response capacity with business criticality.
RECS.i: Tier applications (1, 2, 3) and define SLAs and response targets per tier. Communicate to business stakeholders. Tiered SLAs let you concentrate support investment where it matters and set realistic expectations for less critical apps.

RECS.r: Implement self-service software centres for standard apps
RECS.b: Lets users get common software immediately without raising a ticket.
RECS.i: Deploy a self-service software centre (e.g. Intune Company Portal) for approved standard applications. Users install on demand without IT intervention. Reduces support load and gives users immediate access. Reserve approval workflows for non-standard requests.

RECS.r: Use AI assistants to deflect common questions
RECS.b: Handles routine queries 24/7 and frees humans for complex work.
RECS.i: Deploy an AI assistant trained on your knowledge base for common support queries. Start narrow — password resets, software requests, common FAQs — and expand. Track deflection rate and customer satisfaction. Don't try to boil the ocean on day one.

RECS.r: Embed application support inside product teams (you build it, you run it)
RECS.b: Eliminates the build-run handoff that produces poor-quality apps.
RECS.i: Move to product teams that own both build and run for their applications. The team that builds also fixes — closes the feedback loop and improves quality dramatically. The cultural shift takes time but transforms application reliability.

RECS.r: Build observability into applications during development
RECS.b: Catches issues before users report them.
RECS.i: Make application observability a build-time concern — instrumentation, logging standards, health checks, error reporting. Done at build time, observability is excellent and free. Bolted on later, it's expensive and incomplete. The shift requires engineering investment but pays back continuously.

RECS.r: Adopt SRE practices for tier-1 applications
RECS.b: Builds the engineering discipline that scales operational excellence.
RECS.i: For tier-1 applications, adopt SRE practices — service level objectives, error budgets, blameless postmortems, capacity engineering. Operations becomes engineering rather than reactive support. The discipline transforms application reliability over time.

RECS.r: Build self-healing applications that recover automatically
RECS.b: Eliminates the human in the loop for routine application incidents.
RECS.i: For tier-1 applications, build self-healing capability — auto-detection, auto-diagnosis, auto-remediation. The pattern handles most operational events without human action; humans focus on novel issues. Mature companies' application incidents are dominated by the genuinely novel; routine issues self-resolve.

RECS.r: Use AI for predictive application health management
RECS.b: Catches issues before they impact users.
RECS.i: Apply ML to application telemetry to predict issues — performance degradation, error rate trends, capacity issues. Combine with auto-remediation where safe. Mature predictive operations means most issues never become user-impacting; users notice the absence of problems.

RECS.r: Tie application engineering to business outcome metrics
RECS.b: Aligns engineering investment with business value.
RECS.i: For each application, define business outcome metrics — adoption, user satisfaction, business KPIs supported. Hold engineering teams accountable. Outcome accountability drives different engineering decisions from delivery accountability. The pattern produces applications that actually create value.

RECS.r: Open-source application platforms or contribute to OSS
RECS.b: Builds technical brand and engineering capability through external contribution.
RECS.i: Where strategically appropriate, open-source application platforms or contribute to existing OSS. Builds external recognition, recruiting advantage, and engineering capability. Mature companies treat OSS contribution as strategic; immature ones see it as distraction.

RECS.r: Position application excellence as competitive differentiator
RECS.b: Mature application engineering becomes a sustained competitive advantage.
RECS.i: Communicate application excellence in customer narratives — reliability, performance, innovation velocity. Particularly powerful for software companies and digital businesses. The story drives investment in maintaining the capability and attracts customers who value it.

RECS.r: Deploy basic remote support tooling for off-site users
RECS.b: Provides minimum viable support for remote workers.
RECS.i: Deploy basic remote support tooling — screen sharing (TeamViewer, AnyDesk, or built-in OS tools), basic VPN if needed for resources. Even basic capability is dramatically better than none for remote workers. The sophistication can grow over time; presence matters first.

RECS.r: Document basic remote support procedures
RECS.b: Provides the structure that ad-hoc remote support lacks.
RECS.i: Document basic remote support procedures — how to initiate, what tools to use, what information to gather, how to verify identity. Without procedures, remote support quality varies wildly with whoever picks up the call.

RECS.r: Establish remote support as a supported channel, not exception
RECS.b: Sets the expectation that remote support is a normal mode of working.
RECS.i: Communicate that remote support is a supported, normal channel — not an exception or workaround. Train staff and support team. Adjust SLAs to reflect remote support realities. The cultural shift is as important as the tooling shift; both must happen.

RECS.r: Provide basic VPN access for remote users
RECS.b: Gives remote users access to the resources they need.
RECS.i: Deploy basic VPN access for remote users to reach internal resources. Document setup procedures and provide self-service where possible. VPN isn't ideal long-term (see ZTNA later) but is the starting point for most organisations moving from in-office to hybrid.

RECS.r: Train support staff on basic remote troubleshooting
RECS.b: Builds the minimum capability needed for remote support.
RECS.i: Train support staff on basic remote troubleshooting — phone diagnosis, guided self-help, screen sharing. The skills are different from in-person support; without training, remote support quality is poor. Even basic training transforms outcomes.

RECS.r: Deploy modern endpoint management (e.g., Intune)
RECS.b: Provides secure, manageable devices regardless of location.
RECS.i: Standardise on modern endpoint management (Intune or equivalent) for all corporate devices. Configure compliance, conditional access, and remote actions. Replaces legacy AD-joined fleet management and supports a workforce that's no longer in the office every day.

RECS.r: Train support staff on remote-first troubleshooting
RECS.b: Builds the skills needed for a workforce that no longer comes to a support desk.
RECS.i: Train support staff on remote-first techniques — guided self-help, screen sharing, phone troubleshooting. Walk-up support is a small fraction of what they do now. Without training, frustration on both sides grows; with it, remote support feels seamless.

RECS.r: Build self-service capability for common remote issues
RECS.b: Reduces the support load that scales linearly with remote workforce size.
RECS.i: Build self-service for common remote issues — password reset, software install, basic troubleshooting. Make it accessible from any device and location. Self-service is essential when the support team can't physically visit; without it, support volume grows unsustainably.

RECS.r: Implement secure remote shipping and provisioning processes
RECS.b: Eliminates the in-office bottleneck for new hire setup.
RECS.i: Build processes for secure shipping of pre-configured devices to remote workers, including identity verification at delivery. Automate as much as possible. Without this, every new remote hire becomes a logistical event; with it, remote onboarding scales smoothly.

RECS.r: Provide remote-friendly hardware standards
RECS.b: Ensures remote workers have the tools they need to be productive.
RECS.i: Standardise on hardware suitable for remote work — laptops with good battery, webcams, headsets, external monitors, peripherals. Remote workers face different needs than office workers; hardware standards should reflect this. The investment compounds in productivity.

RECS.r: Implement a zero-trust support model with identity verification
RECS.b: Stops support being a vector for social engineering attacks.
RECS.i: Verify identity strongly before privileged support actions — MFA, callback to known number, ticket validation. Train staff to resist social engineering. Document the process so verification feels routine, not intrusive. Recent breaches show this is a priority.

RECS.r: Use secure remote access tools — not legacy VPN
RECS.b: Provides better security and user experience than full-tunnel VPNs.
RECS.i: Move from legacy VPN to modern zero-trust access (e.g. ZTNA, conditional access, secure web gateway). Per-app access reduces blast radius vs full tunnel. Improves user experience and reduces the attack surface significantly.

RECS.r: Automate device health checks and remediation
RECS.b: Keeps the fleet healthy without depending on user action or manual visits.
RECS.i: Build automated checks for device compliance — patch status, encryption, security tools — with auto-remediation where possible. Alert support for cases needing attention. Drastically reduces compliance drift compared to user-dependent or scheduled scan models.

RECS.r: Build proactive endpoint analytics for fleet health
RECS.b: Surfaces fleet-wide issues that individual support can't see.
RECS.i: Deploy endpoint analytics platforms that surface fleet-wide patterns — boot times, app crashes, login experience, hardware health. Address patterns before users complain. Mature fleet management catches issues affecting many users at once; reactive support catches them one ticket at a time.

RECS.r: Establish remote-first as the default, not the exception
RECS.b: Aligns the operating model with modern workforce reality.
RECS.i: Make remote-first the default operating model — meetings designed for remote, processes that work from anywhere, support model that doesn't depend on in-office presence. Even hybrid organisations benefit from remote-first defaults. The cultural shift transforms outcomes.

RECS.r: Build truly borderless support that's identical regardless of location
RECS.b: Eliminates the in-office vs remote support quality gap.
RECS.i: Build support capability that delivers identical experience and quality regardless of user location. Same tools, same response, same SLAs. Mature companies that have done this well attract talent that wants location flexibility. Make borderless support a deliberate strategic capability.

RECS.r: Use AI to surface and resolve issues before users notice
RECS.b: Eliminates the friction of remote workers needing to ask for help.
RECS.i: Apply AI to endpoint telemetry to predict and resolve issues before users notice — performance degradation, software issues, hardware failure. Particularly valuable for remote workers who often suffer in silence. The pattern transforms remote work experience.

RECS.r: Build support analytics that match in-office observation
RECS.b: Provides the equivalent of walking the floor for remote workforces.
RECS.i: Build behavioural and experience analytics that surface remote worker needs the way in-office observation traditionally has — engagement signals, productivity patterns, frustration indicators. Combine with respect for privacy. The pattern lets support teams see the remote workforce, not just respond to its tickets.

RECS.r: Use remote support capability as recruiting advantage
RECS.b: Top talent increasingly demands location flexibility.
RECS.i: Communicate borderless support and remote-first operations in recruiting materials. Top talent increasingly evaluates remote work quality as a key factor. Mature remote support is a tangible advantage that competitors can't fake — it shows in the experience candidates have during the recruiting process itself.

RECS.r: Position remote support capability as competitive operational advantage
RECS.b: At highest maturity, remote support is competitive infrastructure.
RECS.i: Communicate remote support capability as competitive operational advantage — the ability to operate effectively at any scale, geography, or workforce model. Mature companies that built this during the pandemic now have lasting advantage; immature ones still struggle with the transition.

RECS.r: Set basic response targets for support tickets
RECS.b: Provides the minimum expectation that more advanced practices build on.
RECS.i: Set basic response targets — for example, tier-1 incidents responded within 1 hour, normal tickets within 4 hours. Communicate to users. Even basic targets are dramatically better than no targets; they create accountability that informal support lacks.

RECS.r: Acknowledge every ticket within an agreed time
RECS.b: Reduces the user anxiety of wondering if anyone's seen the ticket.
RECS.i: Set up automated acknowledgements for every ticket and a target for human acknowledgement. Even just 'we've seen this' within 30 minutes transforms user experience. Without acknowledgement, users assume they're not being heard and escalate via other channels.

RECS.r: Track basic response time metrics
RECS.b: Provides the data to manage and improve.
RECS.i: Track first response time and resolution time at a basic level. Even rough metrics tell you whether you're getting better or worse. Without metrics, support quality assertions are anecdotal; with them, conversations become evidence-based.

RECS.r: Establish basic priority levels for incidents
RECS.b: Lets you concentrate response capacity where it matters most.
RECS.i: Establish basic priority levels (e.g. critical, high, medium, low) with criteria for each. Train support staff. Without priority levels, every ticket competes for the same attention; with them, critical issues get the response they need.

RECS.r: Communicate response expectations to users
RECS.b: Aligns expectations with capacity to reduce frustration.
RECS.i: Communicate clearly to users what response they should expect for different ticket types. Most user frustration comes from mismatched expectations, not actual response time. Even setting expectations honestly transforms perceived support quality.

RECS.r: Define and publish SLAs for response and resolution
RECS.b: Sets clear expectations and provides accountability on both sides.
RECS.i: Define realistic SLAs for response and resolution by ticket type. Publish them and track compliance. Communicate breaches with root cause, not excuses. Without published SLAs, perceived support quality varies wildly across users and teams.

RECS.r: Standardise support tier processes and escalation paths
RECS.b: Eliminates inconsistent quality between agents and shifts.
RECS.i: Document standard processes for tier 1, 2, 3 — what each tier handles, escalation criteria, expected response. Train new agents against the standards. Quality control depends on a clear process; without it, quality tracks individual experience.

RECS.r: Implement queue prioritisation based on business impact
RECS.b: Ensures critical issues don't get stuck behind low-impact tickets.
RECS.i: Build queue prioritisation logic that factors business impact, not just submission time. Tier 1 systems and VIP users get faster routing. Make the rules transparent so users understand why their ticket is or isn't urgent.

RECS.r: Track first-call resolution rate as a key KPI
RECS.b: Drives investment in agent capability and knowledge management.
RECS.i: Track first-call resolution as a key KPI alongside time-to-resolve and customer satisfaction. Investigate dips. FCR improvement comes from training, knowledge management, and tooling — not from pressure. Use it as a diagnostic, not a stick.

RECS.r: Run regular SLA performance reviews with leadership
RECS.b: Builds the rhythm of accountability that prevents drift.
RECS.i: Run regular reviews of SLA performance with IT leadership — monthly or quarterly. Discuss patterns, root causes, investment needs. Without leadership review, SLA performance drifts; with review, it gets the attention and investment needed to maintain or improve.

RECS.r: Use AI triage to route and resolve common issues instantly
RECS.b: Combines speed and consistency for common requests.
RECS.i: Deploy AI-driven triage to classify, route, and (for simple issues) auto-resolve incoming tickets. Combine with human escalation for complex cases. Track auto-resolution accuracy continuously. Used carefully it's a force multiplier; used badly it pushes work back to users.

RECS.r: Track customer experience metrics, not just operational metrics
RECS.b: Aligns response with what users actually value.
RECS.i: Beyond operational metrics (response time, resolution time), track customer experience metrics — CSAT, effort score, NPS. The metrics often diverge in informative ways: technically successful responses can produce poor user experience. Track both to optimise both.

RECS.r: Drive root cause analysis to eliminate recurring issues
RECS.b: Reduces support volume systematically rather than just responding faster.
RECS.i: For recurring issues, conduct formal root cause analysis and implement permanent fixes. Track the impact on volume. Mature support reduces total volume year on year through RCA; immature support just gets faster at responding to growing volume.

RECS.r: Build problem management capability alongside incident management
RECS.b: Eliminates the issues that cause incidents at the source.
RECS.i: Establish problem management — the discipline of preventing recurrence rather than just resolving incidents. Build problem records, root cause analysis, permanent fixes. Problem management is what stops support load from growing year on year.

RECS.r: Use predictive analytics to anticipate and prevent issues
RECS.b: Stops issues escalating to user-impacting before they're caught.
RECS.i: Apply predictive analytics to telemetry to spot patterns predicting failure or user impact. Trigger preemptive remediation. Mature companies' support volume is dominated by genuinely novel issues; routine ones never become tickets.

RECS.r: Build AI-driven support that resolves issues before users report them
RECS.b: At highest maturity, the best support is invisible.
RECS.i: Build proactive support driven by behavioural and environmental telemetry — predicting issues, pre-staging fixes, communicating proactively when needed. Mature companies' support load is fundamentally lower because most issues never become incidents. Users notice the absence of problems, not the presence of support.

RECS.r: Tie support to engineering for systematic root cause elimination
RECS.b: Eliminates whole classes of issues at the source.
RECS.i: Build feedback loops from support into product engineering. Support data drives product changes that eliminate issue classes. Mature companies treat support volume as a quality metric; immature ones treat it as a capacity issue. The framing produces fundamentally different investment.

RECS.r: Position support speed as competitive customer experience advantage
RECS.b: Mature support becomes a sustained competitive advantage.
RECS.i: Communicate support responsiveness as competitive advantage — particularly in B2B contexts where customer support quality affects retention and expansion materially. The narrative drives investment in maintaining the capability rather than treating it as cost.

RECS.r: Embed support metrics into business outcome KPIs
RECS.b: Aligns support investment with business value.
RECS.i: Track support metrics alongside business outcomes — does fast support response correlate with retention, NPS, expansion. Mature companies measure this systematically. The data often shows support quality has outsized business impact, supporting investment in the capability.

RECS.r: Make support responsiveness part of recruiting and retention narrative
RECS.b: Top employees expect responsive support that doesn't waste their time.
RECS.i: Use excellent IT support as part of employee value proposition — productivity uninterrupted, fast response, problems resolved before they become problems. Particularly powerful for technology-dependent roles. The story attracts and retains talent that's been frustrated by mediocre IT elsewhere.

RECS.r: Identify which apps are business-critical and document their owners
RECS.b: You can't manage what you haven't named — even a basic list shifts the conversation.
RECS.i: List every app the business depends on, who owns it on the IT side, and who owns it on the business side. Distribute to leadership. The list itself often surfaces orphaned apps and unclear ownership that have been quietly causing pain.

RECS.r: Schedule regular maintenance windows for critical applications
RECS.b: Replaces reactive break-fix with at least a baseline of preventive care.
RECS.i: Schedule monthly or quarterly maintenance windows for critical apps — patching, log rotation, certificate renewal, performance tuning. Communicate them to the business. Even basic preventive maintenance dramatically reduces unplanned outages.

RECS.r: Stand up a basic incident response process for app outages
RECS.b: Reduces chaos when critical apps fail by providing a known path.
RECS.i: Document who gets called, in what order, when a critical app goes down. Distribute the on-call list. Run a tabletop walkthrough. Reactive response is fine; reactive response without a process is what produces extended outages.

RECS.r: Capture downtime and patch outcomes for each critical app
RECS.b: Builds the evidence base for prioritising further investment.
RECS.i: Track every outage and major maintenance event for critical apps — duration, cause, business impact. Even rough tracking changes the conversation about where to invest. Anecdotes don't move budgets; data does.

RECS.r: Open a regular conversation between IT and key app owners
RECS.b: Reduces the siloing that produces blame during outages.
RECS.i: Set up a monthly check-in between IT and the business owners of the top 5 critical apps. Cover health, upcoming changes, and concerns. The relationship is what surfaces issues early; the meeting is just the forcing function.

RECS.r: Define service levels for every business-critical application
RECS.b: Turns vague expectations into measurable commitments on both sides.
RECS.i: For each critical app, define availability targets, RTO, RPO, and support SLAs. Validate with business owners. Publish them. SLAs that aren't agreed with the business produce friction in every outage; SLAs that are agreed produce shared accountability.

RECS.r: Establish a regular health-check cadence for critical apps
RECS.b: Catches degradation before it becomes user-visible failure.
RECS.i: Schedule recurring health checks for each critical app — performance, error rates, capacity, certificate expiry, dependency health. Track findings and remediate. Health checks are cheap; the outages they prevent are expensive.

RECS.r: Build a shared runbook for each tier-1 app
RECS.b: Standardises response and reduces dependence on heroes.
RECS.i: Document how each tier-1 app works, common failure modes, and step-by-step recovery procedures. Validate by having someone who didn't write it follow it. Heroes leave; runbooks stay. Your support quality should not depend on who's awake.

RECS.r: Implement structured change management for critical apps
RECS.b: Reduces the change-induced outages that dominate most incident logs.
RECS.i: Adopt a formal change management process for critical apps — change advisory board, risk assessment, rollback plan, post-change validation. Most outages are change-induced; structured change management cuts incident volume meaningfully.

RECS.r: Report critical app health and SLA performance to the business
RECS.b: Builds the business relationship that supports further investment.
RECS.i: Produce a monthly report on critical app health and SLA performance for business stakeholders. Discuss in business reviews. Visibility creates accountability and informs investment conversations; without it, app reliability stays an IT-internal concern.

RECS.r: Deploy proactive application monitoring across all critical apps
RECS.b: Catches issues before users do — the threshold for true reliability.
RECS.i: For tier-1 apps, deploy deep monitoring — synthetic transactions, real user monitoring, error tracking, dependency health. Alert on leading indicators, not just outages. Reactive monitoring guarantees your users find issues first; proactive monitoring catches them while you can still fix them.

RECS.r: Implement auto-scaling for variable-load applications
RECS.b: Absorbs demand spikes without manual intervention or over-provisioning.
RECS.i: For apps with variable load, implement auto-scaling — horizontal scaling for stateless tiers, read replicas for databases, CDN for static content. Test under load. Auto-scaling done right cuts both outages and infrastructure cost.

RECS.r: Adopt blue-green or rolling deployments for critical apps
RECS.b: Removes change windows for the most important systems.
RECS.i: Adopt blue-green or rolling deployment patterns for tier-1 apps. Combine with feature flags to decouple deployment from release. Allows continuous improvement without user-visible disruption — a major shift from quarterly release cycles.

RECS.r: Run regular DR exercises for all tier-1 applications
RECS.b: Validates that documented recovery actually works.
RECS.i: Schedule full DR exercises for tier-1 apps at least annually. Document outcomes and remediate gaps. Untested DR plans almost always have hidden assumptions that break under real conditions. The discipline of testing is what produces reliable recovery.

RECS.r: Track customer-experience impact of app health, not just operational metrics
RECS.b: Aligns reliability investment with what users and the business actually feel.
RECS.i: Beyond uptime and error rates, track CX-impact metrics — transaction success rates, page load times, user-perceived availability. The metrics often diverge from operational health in informative ways. Investment decisions based on CX impact land better with the business.

RECS.r: Treat critical applications as continuously-evolving digital products
RECS.b: Changes the operating model from project-funded maintenance to product-funded evolution.
RECS.i: Restructure critical apps under a product model — persistent product team, product manager, product roadmap, ongoing funding. Funding follows the product, not projects. The shift produces fundamentally faster evolution and better customer outcomes.

RECS.r: Adopt full CI/CD with zero-downtime deployments
RECS.b: Removes deployment as a constraint on improvement velocity.
RECS.i: Build full CI/CD pipelines with automated testing, canary deployment, automated rollback, and zero-downtime release patterns. Mature companies deploy critical apps multiple times daily without user-visible disruption — and the velocity translates to competitive advantage.

RECS.r: Embed chaos engineering and continuous resilience validation
RECS.b: Catches resilience gaps before incidents do.
RECS.i: Run chaos engineering exercises against tier-1 apps continuously — randomly killing instances, degrading dependencies, introducing latency. Validates that resilience patterns work under real stress. Chaos engineering is what separates 'designed to be resilient' from 'proven to be resilient'.

RECS.r: Use AI and predictive analytics to anticipate and prevent issues
RECS.b: Stops most issues from ever becoming user-visible.
RECS.i: Apply predictive analytics to telemetry from critical apps — anomaly detection, capacity forecasting, failure prediction. Trigger automated remediation. Mature companies' critical-app incident volume is dominated by genuinely novel issues; routine ones never reach users.

RECS.r: Position application reliability as competitive advantage to customers
RECS.b: Reframes reliability investment as growth investment, not cost.
RECS.i: Communicate critical-app reliability externally — to customers, partners, and the market — as a competitive advantage. The narrative drives investment and attracts customers who value reliability, particularly in B2B and regulated sectors where it materially affects retention and pricing power.

RECS.r: Draft a basic RACI for infrastructure and application changes
RECS.b: Replaces blame with at least a starting point for accountability.
RECS.i: Spend a week drafting a RACI for major change types — who's responsible, accountable, consulted, informed for infrastructure changes, app deployments, incident response. Distribute it. Even an imperfect RACI is dramatically better than none; it gives outages an audit trail other than blame.

RECS.r: Stand up a regular sync between infrastructure and application leads
RECS.b: Reduces the surprise factor when changes go wrong.
RECS.i: Schedule a weekly 30-minute sync between the heads of infrastructure and applications. Cover upcoming changes, current incidents, capacity concerns. Even minimal regular contact reduces the 'we didn't know' pattern that drives most cross-team outages.

RECS.r: Document escalation paths between infra and app teams
RECS.b: Removes the delay of figuring out who to call when something breaks.
RECS.i: Document who escalates to whom, and when, between infrastructure and application teams during incidents. Distribute. Test under a tabletop. Escalation paths designed during a real outage are inevitably worse than ones designed in advance.

RECS.r: Run blameless post-incident reviews after every cross-team outage
RECS.b: Replaces blame culture with learning culture.
RECS.i: After every cross-team incident, run a blameless review focused on system and process issues, not individual fault. Capture actions and assign owners. Blameless reviews are what break the blame cycle and produce the trust needed for genuine collaboration.

RECS.r: Inventory the handoffs between infra and app teams
RECS.b: Surfaces the friction points that drive most coordination failures.
RECS.i: Map every handoff between infrastructure and application teams — code-to-deploy, ticket-to-resolution, change-to-validation. List where each handoff currently breaks. The map itself often surfaces obvious fixes, and frames the longer journey toward shared ownership.

RECS.r: Validate the RACI with the people doing the work
RECS.b: RACI on paper that breaks in real escalations is worse than no RACI.
RECS.i: Sit down with infrastructure and application leads and walk through the RACI with real scenarios. Update where reality differs from the doc. Validate with people doing the work, not just managers. The RACI becomes useful only when it matches actual practice.

RECS.r: Establish joint release planning ceremonies
RECS.b: Surfaces dependencies and conflicts before they cause delivery problems.
RECS.i: Run joint release planning involving infrastructure, application, security, and operations. Capture dependencies and risks. Resolve conflicts before commitment. Joint planning is where DevOps culture starts; it's also where most teams underinvest.

RECS.r: Run joint incident response drills
RECS.b: Builds the muscle memory needed during real incidents.
RECS.i: Schedule quarterly joint incident response drills involving infrastructure and application teams. Cover communication, escalation, decision-making under time pressure. Drills are cheap; the gap between documented and actual response capability is what they expose.

RECS.r: Build shared service catalogues with clear ownership
RECS.b: Removes ambiguity about who owns what.
RECS.i: Maintain shared service catalogues listing every service, its owners, dependencies, SLAs, and runbooks. Validate quarterly. The catalogue becomes the single source of truth for ownership questions; without one, ownership questions consume disproportionate time during incidents.

RECS.r: Track joint KPIs across infrastructure and application teams
RECS.b: Aligns incentives so cross-team success is what matters, not local wins.
RECS.i: Define KPIs that require both teams to succeed — deployment frequency, MTTR, change failure rate. Report jointly. Local KPIs produce local optimisation; joint KPIs produce joint outcomes. The shift matters more than the specific metrics chosen.

RECS.r: Standardise on a shared CI/CD platform across infra and app teams
RECS.b: Removes the friction that comes from different tools and different mental models.
RECS.i: Standardise on a shared CI/CD platform — infrastructure-as-code in the same pipelines as application code, same testing approach, same approval gates. Different tools per team produces blind spots and translation overhead. Shared tools build shared understanding.

RECS.r: Implement infrastructure-as-code reviewed and managed like app code
RECS.b: Brings infrastructure under the same engineering rigour as applications.
RECS.i: Manage infrastructure as code — Terraform, Bicep, Pulumi — with PRs, reviews, automated testing, and the same delivery pipeline as application code. The discipline transforms infra from artisanal toil to engineered systems, and removes a major source of cross-team friction.

RECS.r: Build shared observability across infrastructure and applications
RECS.b: Gives both teams the same picture of what's actually happening.
RECS.i: Deploy a unified observability platform covering infrastructure, applications, and dependencies. Same traces, same logs, same dashboards across teams. Shared observability is what enables shared root-cause analysis instead of finger-pointing.

RECS.r: Automate environment provisioning and deployment self-service
RECS.b: Removes infra teams from the critical path for routine app needs.
RECS.i: Build self-service environment provisioning and deployment for application teams — paved roads with guardrails. Automate the routine 80% so infra engineers focus on platform improvement, not ticket queues. Self-service is what turns infra from bottleneck into enabler.

RECS.r: Track and continuously improve handoff times and failure rates
RECS.b: Surfaces friction so it can be addressed rather than tolerated.
RECS.i: Instrument handoffs between teams — time, errors, satisfaction. Run quarterly retrospectives focused on the worst friction points. Improvement here often delivers more value than tooling investments because it removes the dominant slowdown — friction in the seams between teams.

RECS.r: Reorganise into product teams owning the full application stack
RECS.b: Removes handoffs entirely by aligning ownership with outcomes.
RECS.i: Migrate from technology-silo teams to product teams that own the full stack — application, infrastructure, data, security — for their domain. Faster delivery, fewer handoffs, clearer ownership. Requires cultural change as much as structural; sponsor it from the top.

RECS.r: Build a platform team enabling product teams' autonomy
RECS.b: Resolves the central tension between autonomy and standardisation.
RECS.i: Stand up a platform team whose customer is product teams — providing paved roads, shared services, golden paths. Product teams retain autonomy within the guardrails. This pattern (Team Topologies) is how mature DevOps organisations scale without descending into chaos.

RECS.r: Embed full-lifecycle ownership: you build it, you run it
RECS.b: Aligns incentives so quality is built in, not bolted on.
RECS.i: Make product teams accountable for production — on-call, incident response, performance, customer experience. Engineers build different software when they wear the pager. Full-lifecycle ownership is what produces the resilience and quality that mature DevOps delivers.

RECS.r: Drive change through SPACE-style developer experience metrics
RECS.b: Optimises for the practices that actually produce outcomes.
RECS.i: Adopt SPACE or DORA metrics — deployment frequency, lead time, change failure rate, MTTR, developer satisfaction. Use them to drive continuous improvement. Mature companies measure their delivery system as carefully as they measure their products.

RECS.r: Position DevOps maturity as competitive advantage
RECS.b: Frames the investment correctly: it's about speed-to-market, not cost.
RECS.i: Communicate DevOps maturity as competitive advantage — faster experimentation, shorter feedback loops, higher quality. The narrative drives executive support for the cultural and structural changes required. Mature DevOps organisations can deliver new value to customers in days; immature ones take quarters.

RECS.r: Hold a BCP scoping conversation with leadership
RECS.b: Forces the topic onto the agenda before an incident does it for you.
RECS.i: Run a half-day workshop with executives covering: which functions must continue, for how long, under what scenarios. Output a one-page summary. The conversation itself is most of the value; it converts vague awareness into shared understanding of what's at stake.

RECS.r: Identify the top 5 most critical business functions
RECS.b: Concentrates early effort on what matters most when capacity is limited.
RECS.i: Identify the top 5 functions where extended outage is unacceptable — finance, customer service, fulfilment, etc. List the systems they depend on. Even this short list informs every subsequent BCP decision. Trying to plan everything at once produces nothing.

RECS.r: Capture key personnel contact details and authorities
RECS.b: Removes the most basic operational obstacle in a real event.
RECS.i: Build and maintain a contact list for key personnel — IT leadership, business owners, executive decision-makers, key vendors. Include after-hours and personal numbers. In real incidents, the simplest things (who to call, who has authority) are often the most overlooked.

RECS.r: Document the dependencies for the top critical functions
RECS.b: Builds the visibility needed before plans can be drafted.
RECS.i: For each top critical function, document the systems, vendors, locations, and key personnel it depends on. Even rough mapping reveals dependencies that catch most teams by surprise during real events. Use it to prioritise where to harden first.

RECS.r: Walk through one realistic scenario informally with leadership
RECS.b: Generates initial momentum and surfaces obvious gaps.
RECS.i: Take one realistic scenario (extended power outage, ransomware, key system down for 48h) and walk through it informally with leadership. Capture what would happen, what's missing, what would break first. The conversation produces the brief for a more formal plan.

RECS.r: Build and document a comprehensive BCP for all critical functions
RECS.b: Ensures the business can keep operating when major systems fail.
RECS.i: Map critical business functions, the systems they depend on, and manual workarounds for each. Document who does what, with what authority. Validate with each business owner. The doc is only the starting point — it needs to live in the operating model, not the shelf.

RECS.r: Identify manual workarounds for major system failures
RECS.b: Provides a fallback when technology recovery takes longer than expected.
RECS.i: For each critical business function, identify what staff can do manually if the supporting system is down for hours or days. Document the workarounds and pre-position any required artefacts (forms, contact lists). Tested manual workarounds are the difference between reduced and stopped operations.

RECS.r: Establish a basic crisis communication plan
RECS.b: Reduces the chaos and rumour that compound real incidents.
RECS.i: Document who communicates what, to whom, when — including staff, customers, suppliers, regulators. Pre-draft templated messages. Define decision rights. Comms failure is a major contributor to incident impact; pre-thinking removes most of the friction.

RECS.r: Distribute the BCP and validate it with key stakeholders
RECS.b: Plans nobody knows about don't help when needed.
RECS.i: Distribute the BCP to all key stakeholders. Walk through it with each business owner. Capture feedback and incorporate. A BCP locked in a senior leader's drawer is worse than no BCP — it produces false confidence without the practical benefit.

RECS.r: Schedule the first formal tabletop exercise
RECS.b: Converts the document from theory to muscle memory.
RECS.i: Schedule a first formal tabletop exercise within three months of the BCP being signed off. Include business and IT leadership. Document gaps. The exercise transforms the BCP from compliance artefact into living operational tool.

RECS.r: Run annual tabletop simulations across multiple scenarios
RECS.b: Surfaces gaps before a real event, when the cost of learning is high.
RECS.i: Run tabletop simulations covering different scenarios — cyber, natural disaster, key person loss, vendor failure. Include business and IT leadership. Document gaps and remediate. The exercises themselves build the muscle memory; the doc is just the artefact.

RECS.r: Train staff on BCP procedures and their roles
RECS.b: Removes the gap between documented plans and actual execution.
RECS.i: Run regular training so staff know their role and responsibilities in a BC event. Cover communication channels, decision rights, and escalation. Most BCP failures aren't process failures — they're staff who didn't know the process existed.

RECS.r: Integrate BCP triggers into operational monitoring
RECS.b: Reduces the time between event and response.
RECS.i: Wire BCP activation triggers into operational monitoring — major outage indicators, security alerts, infrastructure failure signals. Pre-define thresholds. The faster the activation, the lower the impact. Manual activation often delays response by hours that matter.

RECS.r: Update the BCP after every major incident or organisational change
RECS.b: Keeps the plan aligned with the current operating model.
RECS.i: After every major incident, run a structured review and update the BCP based on what was learned. Same after major reorgs, system replacements, or vendor changes. Stale BCPs are worse than no BCP because they create false confidence.

RECS.r: Report BCP readiness as a board-level KPI
RECS.b: Maintains executive attention on resilience as a strategic priority.
RECS.i: Report BCP readiness — exercise completion, training coverage, plan currency — to the board quarterly. Without leadership visibility, BCP investment loses out to delivery in every budget cycle. Visibility is what protects the program from atrophy.

RECS.r: Embed resilience principles into business model and architecture
RECS.b: Removes the boundary between BAU and continuity, building in resilience by design.
RECS.i: Architect business and technology so the operating model itself absorbs disruption — multi-site staffing, redundant supply chains, multi-region tech. Design choices flow from continuity requirements. The result is resilience that doesn't depend on plan activation; it just is.

RECS.r: Build seamless transitions to alternative operating modes
RECS.b: Means staff barely notice the shift when something fails.
RECS.i: Design alternative operating modes — manual-mode, degraded-mode, alternative-site — so transitions are seamless. Test continuously. The maturity benchmark is staff carrying on with minor adjustments; the immature pattern is escalation to crisis mode.

RECS.r: Run continuous resilience validation through chaos engineering
RECS.b: Validates that resilience claims survive real conditions.
RECS.i: Continuously validate resilience by inducing controlled failures — power, network, system, vendor — and observing the response. Chaos engineering for the business model, not just the tech. The investment delivers confidence in claims that paper plans never can.

RECS.r: Make resilience a competitive market position
RECS.b: Reframes resilience investment as growth investment, not insurance.
RECS.i: Communicate resilience as competitive advantage to customers and partners — supply chain reliability, customer service continuity, data protection. Particularly valuable in regulated and B2B contexts where customers value continuity directly. The narrative drives investment and attracts customers who prize it.

RECS.r: Use AI to anticipate disruption and pre-position responses
RECS.b: Catches disruption signals earlier than human awareness.
RECS.i: Apply AI to weather data, supply chain telemetry, threat intelligence, and operational signals to predict disruption. Pre-position responses — alert staff, activate alternative supply, shift workloads. Mature companies' BC events look planned; immature ones look chaotic.

RECS.r: Have an initial conversation with business owners about acceptable downtime
RECS.b: Even a rough conversation produces clarity that the absence of one cannot.
RECS.i: Run a 60-minute conversation per business owner: how long can the function operate without each major system, and how much data loss is tolerable. Capture rough answers — hours, days, megabytes. Even imprecise targets dramatically beat no targets at all.

RECS.r: Define rough RTO/RPO targets for the top 5 critical systems
RECS.b: Concentrates effort where downtime impact is greatest.
RECS.i: For the top 5 critical systems, document a rough RTO (how quickly to restore) and RPO (how much data loss is acceptable). Use existing architecture as the starting point. Refine over time. Targets, even rough, drive better recovery investment than no targets.

RECS.r: Document current recovery capabilities for critical systems
RECS.b: Surfaces the gap between aspiration and reality.
RECS.i: Document what current recovery capability actually delivers for each critical system — based on past restores, vendor SLAs, or theoretical analysis. Compare to rough targets. The gap is your remediation roadmap.

RECS.r: Identify the highest-risk gap and start closing it
RECS.b: Concrete progress beats comprehensive plans that don't get acted on.
RECS.i: Pick the worst gap between actual and target capability and start remediation — backup improvements, replication, faster restore tooling. Demonstrating progress on one critical system builds momentum for the broader programme.

RECS.r: Bring recovery targets into management reporting
RECS.b: Forces ongoing executive attention rather than one-off agreement.
RECS.i: Add recovery target status to monthly IT management reporting — current targets, current capability, biggest gap. Even brief reporting maintains attention and supports investment cases. Without it, recovery targets agreed once tend to fade from view.

RECS.r: Define RTO/RPO targets per system tier with business alignment
RECS.b: Anchors recovery investment in what the business can actually tolerate.
RECS.i: Work with business owners to define RTO and RPO targets for each system tier based on impact tolerance. Validate the targets are realistic given current architecture. Targets without alignment to business impact lead to either over-investment or unacceptable exposure.

RECS.r: Document and publish targets to all stakeholders
RECS.b: Shared visibility prevents disagreement during real recovery events.
RECS.i: Document RTO/RPO targets per system in a single accessible location. Distribute to business and IT stakeholders. Reference in recovery plans. Targets agreed but not documented are remembered differently by different people; written ones provide a single source of truth.

RECS.r: Validate technical capability to meet the targets via testing
RECS.b: Confirms targets are achievable, not just aspirational.
RECS.i: Run periodic recovery tests against documented RTO and RPO. Capture actual achieved values. Where reality misses target, either invest to close the gap or formally accept the risk. Targets that no one validates become meaningless promises.

RECS.r: Tier systems explicitly so recovery investment focuses correctly
RECS.b: Concentrates investment where downtime is most costly.
RECS.i: Tier systems explicitly — tier 1 (mission-critical), tier 2 (business-important), tier 3 (operational). Different recovery investments per tier. Without tiering, every system is critical, every system is treated the same, and recovery investment is spread too thin.

RECS.r: Build remediation roadmap for systems not meeting their targets
RECS.b: Converts agreed targets into actual capability over time.
RECS.i: For systems where current capability misses target, build a remediation roadmap with prioritised actions, costs, and timelines. Present to leadership for funding. Targets without a roadmap become commitments without delivery.

RECS.r: Run quarterly recovery tests for tier-1 systems
RECS.b: Catches degradation before it matters.
RECS.i: Schedule quarterly recovery tests for tier-1 systems and at least annually for the rest. Vary scenarios (component failure, region failure, data corruption). Test results drive remediation prioritisation and provide audit-grade evidence.

RECS.r: Update objectives as systems and dependencies change
RECS.b: Keeps targets aligned with the evolving environment.
RECS.i: Refresh RTO/RPO targets when major systems or dependencies change. New cloud platforms, new vendors, new business processes can all shift achievable performance. Stale targets either over-promise or fail to capture improvements that have been made.

RECS.r: Track achieved RTO/RPO against target as a KPI
RECS.b: Makes recovery performance visible and managed.
RECS.i: Build dashboards showing target vs achieved RTO/RPO per system. Track trends. Investigate misses. Visibility is what drives sustained capability; without it, recovery performance drifts when other priorities crowd in.

RECS.r: Report achievement to executive sponsors quarterly
RECS.b: Maintains accountability and informs investment decisions.
RECS.i: Report recovery test results and target achievement to executive sponsors quarterly. Highlight gaps and proposed remediation. Without leadership visibility, recovery investment loses out to feature delivery in every budget cycle.

RECS.r: Use test failures as input to architecture roadmap
RECS.b: Connects recovery findings to the systems that produce them.
RECS.i: Capture systemic patterns from recovery test failures — slow restore from particular backup tier, dependency on a flaky vendor, manual steps in supposedly automated processes. Feed into architecture roadmap. Recovery testing produces a structured stream of architecture-improvement input.

RECS.r: Implement continuous replication for tier-1 systems
RECS.b: Brings RPO toward zero for the most critical workloads.
RECS.i: For tier-1 systems, implement continuous data replication to a recovery site or region. Sync replication where the workload supports it; async otherwise. Modern cloud platforms make this readily achievable. Brings RPO down to seconds for the systems where every minute of data matters.

RECS.r: Build automated failover with health-check triggers
RECS.b: Eliminates the human delay between failure detection and recovery initiation.
RECS.i: For tier-1 systems, build automated failover triggered by health checks. Define automated failover rules with human override available. Manual failover under incident pressure is error-prone; automated failover removes most of the variability and reduces RTO from hours to minutes.

RECS.r: Run continuous recovery validation, not periodic tests
RECS.b: Validates resilience continuously rather than at scheduled snapshots.
RECS.i: Move from periodic recovery tests to continuous validation — each replication continuously verified, periodic automatic failovers in test, chaos engineering. Mature recovery is validated continuously, not on a calendar; failure modes change too fast for annual testing to keep up.

RECS.r: Position low RTO/RPO as competitive customer commitment
RECS.b: Reframes recovery investment as growth investment.
RECS.i: Communicate near-zero RTO/RPO commitments to customers as part of the value proposition. Particularly valuable in B2B contexts where downtime affects customer operations directly. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment in maintaining the capability.

RECS.r: Use AI to predict recovery readiness and surface drift
RECS.b: Catches degradation in recovery capability before incidents do.
RECS.i: Apply AI to recovery telemetry — replication lag, failover latency, restore success patterns — to predict recovery readiness drift. Catches deterioration weeks before it would matter. Mature companies surface recovery degradation as proactively as they surface operational degradation.

RECS.r: Automate daily backups for all critical systems
RECS.b: Removes the most basic source of backup failure: humans forgetting.
RECS.i: Implement automated daily backups for all critical systems — full or incremental on appropriate cadence. Eliminate manual backup scripts. Automation is a one-time investment that pays back forever; manual backups fail predictably whenever the responsible human is busy.

RECS.r: Standardise backup tooling across the estate
RECS.b: Reduces operational complexity and skill requirements.
RECS.i: Standardise on a single backup platform (or small set) across the estate. Different tools per system multiplies operational overhead and skill requirements. A single platform produces consistent operations and easier monitoring.

RECS.r: Build a basic backup catalogue and retention policy
RECS.b: Establishes governance over what's backed up and for how long.
RECS.i: Document what's backed up, retention period, and storage location. Define retention by data type and regulatory requirement. The catalogue is the foundation for both restore confidence and compliance evidence; without it, both are guesswork.

RECS.r: Verify backup completion daily
RECS.b: Catches the most common silent failure — backups that didn't actually happen.
RECS.i: Implement daily verification that scheduled backups actually completed successfully. Alert on missed or failed backups. The most common backup failure isn't corruption — it's never running in the first place. Verify completion before worrying about restore.

RECS.r: Perform a sample restore at least monthly
RECS.b: Confirms the most basic claim — that backups are usable.
RECS.i: Pick one backup per month and restore it. Validate the data is usable. Untested backups have a high probability of failing under real recovery; even a basic monthly sample restore catches most issues before they matter.

RECS.r: Run automated daily backups to off-site/cloud locations
RECS.b: Ensures continuity if the primary site is destroyed or compromised.
RECS.i: Automate daily backups to a geographically separate location — typically cloud-based. Eliminate manual steps. Monitor for failures continuously. Backup discipline is one of the highest-ROI operational investments because the cost of failure is so high.

RECS.r: Test backup restoration monthly via sample recovery
RECS.b: Confirms backups are usable, not just present.
RECS.i: Restore a sample of backups monthly and validate data integrity. Schedule full system recovery tests at least annually. Untested backups have a high probability of failing under real recovery conditions; testing catches the failures before they matter.

RECS.r: Implement 3-2-1 backup pattern (3 copies, 2 media, 1 off-site)
RECS.b: Industry-standard pattern that addresses the common failure modes.
RECS.i: Implement 3-2-1: three copies of data, on two different media types, with at least one off-site. This pattern survives most realistic disaster scenarios — site loss, media failure, accidental deletion. Cloud-as-off-site has made 3-2-1 dramatically cheaper to implement.

RECS.r: Track backup success rates and remediate failures fast
RECS.b: Catches silent backup failures before they leave you exposed.
RECS.i: Monitor backup success rates with alerting on failure. Investigate every failure within 24 hours. Track trends — increasing failure rate is a leading indicator. Silent backup failures are a common pattern in incident post-mortems.

RECS.r: Document and rehearse end-to-end recovery procedures
RECS.b: Builds the muscle memory to execute under stress.
RECS.i: Document the full recovery sequence — from backup retrieval through application bring-up to validation. Rehearse with the team that would actually execute. Recovery under pressure with an undocumented process is where most recovery failures originate.

RECS.r: Implement immutable, air-gapped backups protected from ransomware
RECS.b: Provides the last line of defence when everything else has been compromised.
RECS.i: Implement immutable backups using technologies that prevent encryption or deletion by ransomware (object lock, separate identity domain). Maintain air-gapped or logically isolated copies. Ransomware response strategy hinges on whether you can recover; backups are the foundation.

RECS.r: Encrypt backups at rest and in transit
RECS.b: Protects backup data from compromise even if the storage is breached.
RECS.i: Encrypt backups in transit and at rest with keys managed separately from production. Manage key rotation. Backups are an attractive target for attackers because they contain everything; treating them with the same security rigour as production is essential.

RECS.r: Build automated success-rate reporting
RECS.b: Provides quantitative evidence of backup hygiene.
RECS.i: Build reporting on backup success rates, restore validation results, and capacity. Report monthly to IT leadership. The reporting cadence drives sustained discipline; without metrics, backup hygiene degrades silently between audits.

RECS.r: Use a separate identity domain for backup infrastructure
RECS.b: Prevents an identity compromise from cascading to backups.
RECS.i: Manage backup infrastructure under a separate identity domain from production. Ransomware attacks routinely use compromised credentials to delete backups before encrypting production. Identity isolation is what prevents that pattern.

RECS.r: Run quarterly full-restore tests of tier-1 systems
RECS.b: Validates more than sample restores can.
RECS.i: Quarterly, restore a complete tier-1 system end-to-end into an isolated environment. Validate functionality, not just data. Sample restores catch corruption; full restores catch the operational issues that only show up at full scale.

RECS.r: Implement continuous multi-region data protection
RECS.b: Removes single-region risk from the recovery story.
RECS.i: For tier-1 systems, implement continuous data replication across multiple regions. Combine with automated failover. The recovery model shifts from restore-from-backup to spin-up-replica — orders of magnitude faster and removes whole-region failure modes.

RECS.r: Build instant recovery capability through replicated systems
RECS.b: Brings recovery time toward zero for the most critical workloads.
RECS.i: Stand up replicated systems in standby mode capable of instant activation. Point-in-time recovery via continuous data protection. Mature companies recover from major disruption in minutes, not hours; the architecture is what makes the timing achievable.

RECS.r: Run continuous recovery validation, not periodic tests
RECS.b: Validates resilience continuously rather than at scheduled snapshots.
RECS.i: Move from periodic recovery tests to continuous validation — each backup tested, each replica health-checked, periodic automated failover. Mature recovery is validated continuously, not on a calendar; failure modes change too fast for periodic testing to keep up.

RECS.r: Use AI to detect data integrity issues across backup copies
RECS.b: Catches subtle corruption that periodic tests would miss.
RECS.i: Apply AI/ML to detect anomalies in backup data — silent corruption, ransomware-style encryption patterns, unusual change rates. Trigger automated investigation. Catches integrity issues that pure success-rate monitoring would miss entirely.

RECS.r: Position rapid recovery as competitive customer commitment
RECS.b: Reframes recovery investment as growth investment.
RECS.i: Communicate rapid recovery commitments externally — to customers, partners, and the market. Particularly valuable in B2B and regulated contexts. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment in maintaining the capability and aligns recovery with business strategy.

RECS.r: Draft a basic DR plan for the top 3 critical systems
RECS.b: Anything beats the absence of a plan when an event hits.
RECS.i: Draft a basic DR plan for the top 3 critical systems — what fails over, where, who decides, in what order. Even a one-page plan dramatically improves outcomes versus no plan. Comprehensive can come later; existence has to come first.

RECS.r: Identify obvious single points of failure
RECS.b: Surfaces the easiest wins for resilience improvement.
RECS.i: Walk the architecture and identify obvious single points of failure — single power feed, single internet connection, single database server, single key person. List them. Some can be addressed cheaply; the list informs both immediate fixes and longer-term roadmap.

RECS.r: Set up basic redundancy for the most exposed components
RECS.b: Cheap improvements that materially reduce risk.
RECS.i: For the worst single points of failure, implement basic redundancy — redundant power, dual ISPs, secondary database, named backup person. Each fix is small; cumulatively they shift the risk profile materially.

RECS.r: Establish on-call coverage for tier-1 systems
RECS.b: Ensures someone responds when something fails outside business hours.
RECS.i: Establish basic on-call rotation for tier-1 systems with clear escalation paths and documented contact details. The maturity gap from 'whoever happens to be online' to 'we have a rota' is large and largely free.

RECS.r: Walk through one DR scenario informally with the team
RECS.b: Builds basic muscle memory and surfaces obvious gaps.
RECS.i: Take one realistic scenario (database failure, primary site outage) and walk through it with the team. Capture what they would do, what they don't know, what's missing. The walkthrough is the cheapest possible test and produces immediate improvements.

RECS.r: Document HA/DR plans with clear ownership
RECS.b: Ensures someone is accountable for each component of the recovery story.
RECS.i: Document HA/DR plans for each tier-1 system with named owners for each component. Capture dependencies, runbooks, decision authorities, and escalation. Without named ownership, plans drift; with it, regular review becomes routine.

RECS.r: Configure HA for critical systems
RECS.b: Eliminates many failure modes by removing single-instance risk.
RECS.i: For critical systems, configure HA — clusters, load-balanced pairs, replication groups. Designed to survive instance failure without service interruption. HA is the foundation; DR is for events HA can't absorb.

RECS.r: Run annual full DR exercises to validate plans
RECS.b: Provides the only real evidence that DR will work when needed.
RECS.i: At least annually, run full DR exercises that fail over tier-1 systems and validate functionality end-to-end. Capture timings, gaps, and remediation actions. The annual cadence keeps the muscle memory and catches drift between exercises.

RECS.r: Build clear runbooks for the most likely failure scenarios
RECS.b: Removes ambiguity from response when minutes matter.
RECS.i: Build runbooks for the most likely failure scenarios — primary site loss, database corruption, key dependency failure. Step-by-step instructions, not just goals. Validated under exercise conditions. Runbook quality is what separates fast recovery from slow.

RECS.r: Track HA/DR test results and remediate gaps systematically
RECS.b: Closes the loop from testing to improvement.
RECS.i: After each DR exercise, capture timing, what worked, what failed, what's needed. Build into a remediation backlog. Test results that don't drive systematic improvement are wasted; the backlog is what makes the exercise pay back.

RECS.r: Configure automated failover for tier-1 systems
RECS.b: Reduces RTO from hours of human-driven work to minutes.
RECS.i: Implement automated failover for tier-1 systems where the technology supports it. Health checks trigger failover; runbooks document the human approvals required. Manual failover under incident pressure is error-prone; automation removes most of the variability.

RECS.r: Use chaos engineering to test resilience continuously
RECS.b: Surfaces brittleness in production conditions, not just in test.
RECS.i: Inject controlled failures continuously — instance termination, dependency latency, network partition. Start in test; mature teams run controlled chaos in production. Reveals brittle patterns and hidden assumptions traditional testing never catches.

RECS.r: Implement multi-region or multi-AZ architecture for tier-1
RECS.b: Survives whole-region failures, not just instance failures.
RECS.i: Architect tier-1 systems across multiple availability zones or regions. Active-active where workload supports it; active-passive otherwise. Modern cloud platforms make multi-AZ readily achievable; multi-region is achievable for systems that justify the investment.

RECS.r: Build auto-scaling and self-healing into the platform
RECS.b: Removes humans from the routine path.
RECS.i: Implement auto-scaling for variable load, automatic instance replacement on failure, automatic restart on crash. Mature platforms handle most failure modes without human involvement; humans intervene only for genuinely novel issues.

RECS.r: Refine plans continuously based on test results and real incidents
RECS.b: Captures lessons before they're forgotten and turns events into improvement.
RECS.i: After every test or real incident, update HA/DR plans based on what was learned. Track improvements in metrics — reduced RTO, fewer manual steps, faster detection. Without this feedback loop, plans stagnate while the environment evolves.

RECS.r: Build self-healing infrastructure across all regions
RECS.b: Removes the boundary between resilience and BAU operation.
RECS.i: Architect self-healing infrastructure that detects and remediates most failures without human involvement — auto-scaling, auto-replacement, multi-region redundancy. Mature platforms run for months at high availability without human resilience work; humans focus on novel issues only.

RECS.r: Run continuous chaos engineering in production
RECS.b: Validates resilience under real conditions, not just test.
RECS.i: Run controlled chaos engineering exercises continuously, including in production. Random instance termination, dependency degradation, latency injection. Validates that resilience claims are real, not theoretical. The discipline is what produces production-grade resilience.

RECS.r: Implement automated recovery validation across regions
RECS.b: Catches drift in recovery capability before incidents do.
RECS.i: Continuously validate recovery capability — replica health, failover speed, RTO/RPO achievement — across all regions. Surface drift as it happens. Mature companies treat recovery capability as a continuously-monitored property, not a periodically-tested one.

RECS.r: Use AI to predict and prevent infrastructure failures
RECS.b: Stops most failures from ever requiring recovery.
RECS.i: Apply AI to operational telemetry to predict failures — disk health, network anomalies, resource exhaustion. Trigger preemptive remediation. Mature companies' incident volume is dominated by truly novel issues; predictable failure modes are caught early enough to never become incidents.

RECS.r: Position resilience as competitive differentiator
RECS.b: Reframes resilience investment as growth investment.
RECS.i: Communicate resilience as competitive advantage — uptime SLAs, regional resilience, recovery commitments. Particularly valuable in B2B and regulated contexts where customers value continuity directly. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment.

RECS.r: Build a basic asset inventory of servers, endpoints, and cloud resources
RECS.b: You cannot protect what you cannot see.
RECS.i: Build a basic inventory: servers, endpoints, cloud resources, key SaaS apps. Capture owner, criticality, and exposure. Even an imperfect inventory is dramatically better than none — most breach incidents trace back to assets that fell off the inventory entirely.

RECS.r: Identify the top 10 cyber risks the organisation faces
RECS.b: Forces explicit identification of risks rather than vague awareness.
RECS.i: Spend a workshop identifying the top 10 cyber risks — based on industry threats, organisational exposure, and known issues. Capture them in a simple spreadsheet. Even rough listing makes risks discussable and actionable rather than vaguely felt.

RECS.r: Identify the security owners for each major system
RECS.b: Removes ambiguity about who's accountable for security on each asset.
RECS.i: For each major system, identify the security owner — IT or business. Validate accountability. Ownership ambiguity is one of the most common causes of unaddressed security issues; clarifying ownership is a fast, cheap improvement.

RECS.r: Run a basic external vulnerability scan
RECS.b: Reveals the most easily-exploited issues.
RECS.i: Run a basic external vulnerability scan — even with free tools — to identify the most exposed issues from an attacker's perspective. Address the highest-severity issues. The scan often reveals issues nobody knew about, providing immediate remediation targets.

RECS.r: Establish basic incident reporting and response
RECS.b: Ensures security events get reported rather than hidden.
RECS.i: Document who reports security events, to whom, in what timeframe. Distribute to all staff. The biggest single improvement for early-stage organisations is converting unreported events into reported ones; the rest follows from there.

RECS.r: Maintain a comprehensive asset inventory with discovery automation
RECS.b: You can't protect what you don't know exists.
RECS.i: Implement automated discovery for all asset classes — servers, endpoints, cloud resources, SaaS, IoT. Reconcile against expected baseline weekly. Most breach incidents trace back to assets that fell off the inventory; automated discovery prevents the gap.

RECS.r: Build a risk register linked to assets and business impact
RECS.b: Frames cyber risk in business terms, not just technical.
RECS.i: Build a risk register where each entry maps to specific assets, threats, and business impact. Score using a consistent framework (likelihood × impact). Validate with business owners. Drives investment to where the real risk lives, not where the loudest voice points.

RECS.r: Run quarterly risk assessments with security and business input
RECS.b: Keeps the risk picture current as threats and the business evolve.
RECS.i: Run quarterly assessments combining technical scans, threat intelligence, and business context. Update the risk register based on findings. Annual-only assessments miss too much; quarterly is the right cadence for most organisations.

RECS.r: Adopt a recognised cyber framework (NIST CSF, ISO 27001, CIS)
RECS.b: Provides structured guidance rather than reinventing the wheel.
RECS.i: Adopt a recognised framework as the basis for security programme structure. NIST CSF for breadth; CIS Controls for prioritised actionability; ISO 27001 for certification path. Frameworks compress years of industry learning into structured guidance.

RECS.r: Report risk register status to executive leadership quarterly
RECS.b: Maintains executive visibility on cyber risk.
RECS.i: Report the risk register and major changes to executive leadership quarterly. Discuss material risks, mitigation progress, and emerging concerns. Without leadership visibility, cyber risk competes against tangible delivery and loses; with it, sustained investment becomes possible.

RECS.r: Implement continuous risk assessment integrated into operations
RECS.b: Replaces periodic snapshots with always-current risk visibility.
