RECS.i: Move from quarterly assessment to continuous — automated scans, integrated threat intelligence, real-time risk scoring. Quarterly snapshots miss too much; continuous assessment catches changes as they happen. Modern tooling makes this readily achievable.

RECS.r: Use threat modelling for new systems and major changes
RECS.b: Catches design-level security flaws before they're built in.
RECS.i: Apply structured threat modelling (e.g. STRIDE) to new systems and significant changes. Capture and address findings before deployment. Building security in is dramatically cheaper than retrofitting; threat modelling is the gateway to that discipline.

RECS.r: Drive risk-based investment in mitigation controls
RECS.b: Ensures security spend goes to the highest-impact controls.
RECS.i: Prioritise security investment based on risk register findings — not vendor pitches or industry trends. Document the rationale for each major spend. Risk-based investment beats fashion-driven investment for both efficacy and audit defensibility.

RECS.r: Build security posture dashboards visible to leadership
RECS.b: Provides ongoing visibility rather than periodic reports.
RECS.i: Build security posture dashboards covering vulnerabilities, exposures, control coverage, threat indicators. Make them visible to security and IT leadership continuously. Dashboards drive sustained attention better than periodic reports — issues become visible as they emerge.

RECS.r: Track risk reduction over time as a KPI
RECS.b: Demonstrates programme value rather than just activity.
RECS.i: Track quantitative risk reduction over time — risk register movements, exposure reduction, control coverage. Report as a KPI. Activity metrics (scans run, tickets closed) flatter the program; outcome metrics (risk reduced) prove value.

RECS.r: Implement real-time asset and risk intelligence
RECS.b: Catches threats as they emerge rather than at scheduled scan times.
RECS.i: Build real-time asset intelligence integrating discovery, vulnerability data, threat intelligence, and behavioural telemetry. Risk picture updates continuously. Mature companies see threats develop in real time; immature ones see them at next-scan-cycle.

RECS.r: Use AI for risk-scoring and prioritisation
RECS.b: Manages risk volume that human teams cannot keep up with.
RECS.i: Apply AI to risk scoring and prioritisation — combining vulnerability severity, exploitability, exposure, asset criticality, threat intelligence. AI makes triage tractable at the volume modern environments produce; without it, signal gets lost in noise.

RECS.r: Drive proactive investment based on predictive risk modelling
RECS.b: Stays ahead of threats rather than responding to them.
RECS.i: Use predictive risk modelling to anticipate emerging risks — based on threat trends, environmental changes, vulnerability disclosures. Pre-position investment. Mature companies act on emerging risk before it materialises; immature ones respond to incidents.

RECS.r: Position cyber maturity as competitive advantage to customers
RECS.b: Reframes cyber investment as growth investment.
RECS.i: Communicate cyber maturity to customers, partners, and regulators as competitive advantage. Particularly valuable in B2B contexts where customer cyber due diligence is rigorous. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment.

RECS.r: Embed cyber risk into business strategy at board level
RECS.b: Treats cyber as a strategic risk class rather than an IT issue.
RECS.i: Embed cyber risk into board-level strategy discussions — alongside financial, operational, market risk. Cyber that's discussed only with the CIO loses to cyber discussed with the CEO. Mature companies treat it as a fundamental business risk, not a technical one.

RECS.r: Enforce MFA on the most critical systems immediately
RECS.b: Closes the most exploited attack vector with minimum effort.
RECS.i: Enforce MFA on email, VPN, admin consoles, and the top financial systems immediately. Don't wait for full coverage. Most breach incidents involve credential compromise; even partial MFA coverage on critical systems delivers outsized risk reduction.

RECS.r: Build and enforce a basic password policy
RECS.b: Establishes a minimum bar against the most trivial credential attacks.
RECS.i: Implement basic password requirements — length, complexity, no reuse, regular rotation only on suspected compromise (per modern guidance). Block common passwords. Even basic policy beats the chaos of unmanaged passwords.

RECS.r: Run an offboarding audit and remove orphaned access
RECS.b: Eliminates the easiest-to-exploit standing access.
RECS.i: Audit all systems for accounts of former employees and remove them. Build a basic offboarding checklist. Orphaned former-employee accounts are a frequent breach pattern; removing them is high-impact and free.

RECS.r: Centralise identity for the most-used systems
RECS.b: Reduces the number of identity stores to manage.
RECS.i: For the top 5 most-used systems, federate identity through a central IdP. Even partial centralisation reduces the operational burden and tightens control. Multiple disconnected identity stores produce gaps that are hard to see and easy to exploit.

RECS.r: Disable inactive accounts automatically
RECS.b: Reduces standing exposure from accounts no one is using.
RECS.i: Implement automatic disablement of accounts inactive for 30-60 days. Review before disabling. Removes a steady source of standing exposure that nobody is monitoring. Easy to implement; meaningful risk reduction.

RECS.r: Enforce MFA for all users and admin access
RECS.b: Eliminates the largest single source of breach risk.
RECS.i: Enforce phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2, certificate-based) for all users — not just admins. Eliminate password-only fallback. Most successful breach attempts depend on credential compromise; strong MFA closes the door on the dominant attack pattern.

RECS.r: Implement single sign-on (SSO) across all critical applications
RECS.b: Reduces password fatigue and centralises identity controls.
RECS.i: Implement SSO across all critical apps. Federate identity through your central IdP (Entra ID, Okta). Reduces password-related risk and simplifies access management. Holdout apps that resist SSO need a clear remediation plan.

RECS.r: Implement role-based access control (RBAC)
RECS.b: Replaces ad-hoc permissions with managed roles.
RECS.i: Define roles based on job functions and map permissions to roles. Assign users to roles rather than granting individual permissions. RBAC scales; per-user permissions don't. Auditable, manageable, and dramatically reduces the over-privilege common in informal access models.

RECS.r: Run quarterly access reviews
RECS.b: Catches privilege creep before it becomes systemic.
RECS.i: Quarterly, have managers review their team's access. Remove anything that's no longer needed. Without regular review, access only ever accumulates; with review, it stays roughly aligned with role needs. The discipline matters more than the tooling.

RECS.r: Implement automated provisioning and deprovisioning
RECS.b: Removes the human delay between role change and access change.
RECS.i: Build joiner-mover-leaver automation — HR system events trigger access changes automatically. Eliminates the gap between someone leaving and their access being removed. Manual processes always lag; automation closes the gap to minutes.

RECS.r: Apply Conditional Access policies for risk-based decisions
RECS.b: Adapts authentication strength to actual risk in the moment.
RECS.i: Implement risk-based conditional access — device compliance, location, sign-in risk score, app sensitivity. Tighter controls for sensitive operations; lighter friction for low-risk. The dynamic model balances security and usability better than static rules.

RECS.r: Use Just-In-Time admin elevation for privileged access
RECS.b: Eliminates standing privileged access — a top breach vector.
RECS.i: Move privileged access to JIT model: standing access removed, elevation requested with justification, time-limited, audited. Reduces blast radius of credential compromise materially. Implement via PIM (Microsoft) or equivalent.

RECS.r: Implement device compliance as access criterion
RECS.b: Ensures access only from managed, healthy devices.
RECS.i: Tie access to device health and compliance — managed device, encryption on, patched, EDR running. Block non-compliant devices. The model raises the bar for attacker access and uses identity as the policy enforcement point.

RECS.r: Build privileged access workstations for admin work
RECS.b: Isolates admin work from the general attack surface.
RECS.i: Provide dedicated, hardened workstations for privileged access to critical systems. No general browsing or email on these devices. Prevents many of the lateral-movement patterns attackers use to escalate from user compromise to admin compromise.

RECS.r: Continuously monitor identity for anomalies
RECS.b: Catches credential compromise as it happens.
RECS.i: Implement identity-focused monitoring — impossible travel, unusual access patterns, privilege escalation, unusual app access. Alert on anomalies. Identity is the new perimeter; monitoring it is what catches attacks that bypass network controls entirely.

RECS.r: Implement full Zero-Trust architecture
RECS.b: Replaces network-perimeter assumptions with explicit, continuous identity verification.
RECS.i: Adopt zero-trust principles: verify explicitly, use least privilege, assume breach. Apply per-app access, micro-segmentation, continuous evaluation. Multi-year journey but the destination is the only credible answer to the modern threat landscape.

RECS.r: Use continuous access evaluation, not session-time auth
RECS.b: Revokes access in real time when risk changes.
RECS.i: Implement continuous access evaluation — real-time policy evaluation throughout the session, not just at login. Risk changes (location, device state, behavioural anomaly) trigger immediate re-auth or revocation. Closes the window where stolen sessions can be used.

RECS.r: Build full device context into every access decision
RECS.b: Maximises signal quality in the access decision.
RECS.i: Combine identity, device, location, behavioural, and context signals in every access decision. Dynamic risk scoring drives policy. The richer the signal, the more accurate the decision — and the less friction for legitimate users.

RECS.r: Implement passwordless authentication where possible
RECS.b: Eliminates the dominant attack vector entirely.
RECS.i: Move toward passwordless authentication — FIDO2 keys, Windows Hello, mobile authenticators. Most breach attempts target passwords; eliminating them eliminates the attack vector. Deploy progressively, starting with high-risk roles.

RECS.r: Position identity maturity as customer trust commitment
RECS.b: Reframes identity investment as growth investment.
RECS.i: Communicate identity security maturity externally — to customers, partners, and regulators. Particularly valuable in B2B contexts where customer due diligence is rigorous. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment in maintaining the capability.

RECS.r: Implement basic automated OS patching for endpoints and servers
RECS.b: Removes the most common source of unpatched systems: humans forgetting.
RECS.i: Deploy basic patching tools — Windows Update for Business, Intune, WSUS, or equivalent — to automate OS patching on a known schedule. Even basic automation dramatically improves on irregular manual patching. Most exploited vulnerabilities have patches available; deployment is what's missing.

RECS.r: Establish a basic monthly patch window
RECS.b: Replaces ad-hoc patching with predictable cadence.
RECS.i: Establish a monthly patch window when patches are deployed. Communicate to users. Even basic predictability reduces operational disruption and ensures patches happen. Without a window, patches are perpetually deferred.

RECS.r: Track which systems are unpatched at month-end
RECS.b: Provides visibility on the gap between policy and reality.
RECS.i: Generate a monthly report listing unpatched systems and their owners. Distribute to IT leadership. Without measurement, unpatched systems accumulate silently; with measurement, the gap becomes visible and actionable.

RECS.r: Maintain a list of critical CVEs requiring out-of-cycle patching
RECS.b: Ensures urgent issues don't wait for the next monthly window.
RECS.i: Define criteria for out-of-cycle patching — actively exploited CVEs, critical-severity issues on exposed systems. Empower the security team to trigger emergency patching. Mature patching balances cadence with the ability to respond fast when needed.

RECS.r: Identify the top 10 highest-risk unpatched systems
RECS.b: Concentrates remediation on what matters most.
RECS.i: Run a prioritised assessment of unpatched systems based on exposure and criticality. Pick the worst 10 and remediate. Demonstrates progress and builds momentum for the broader programme. Leaves systems uncovered — but they were uncovered before, and now you have a roadmap.

RECS.r: Establish a formal patch management policy with monthly windows
RECS.b: Provides predictable, governed patch cadence.
RECS.i: Define and publish a patch management policy with monthly windows by tier. Document exception process. Ensures patches happen on a known cadence rather than heroic effort. Balance patching aggression against business disruption explicitly.

RECS.r: Extend patching to third-party applications
RECS.b: Closes the gap that OS-only patching leaves.
RECS.i: Extend patching coverage to third-party applications — browsers, runtimes, common business apps. Most exploited vulnerabilities are in apps, not OS. Tools like Patch My PC, Ninite Pro, or modern endpoint management bring third-party patches under management.

RECS.r: Use automated vulnerability scanning across all assets
RECS.b: Surfaces vulnerabilities continuously, not just at audit time.
RECS.i: Deploy automated vulnerability scanning across all environments — internal, external, cloud, container. Pair with continuous monitoring for new CVEs. Scanning is now table stakes; the discipline is acting on the findings, not just generating reports.

RECS.r: Build a patch exception process with required documentation
RECS.b: Ensures unpatched systems are deliberate, not forgotten.
RECS.i: Build a formal exception process — business reason, compensating controls, review date, owner. Track exceptions actively. Every unpatched system should be a deliberate documented choice, not an oversight.

RECS.r: Track time-to-patch and remediation rates
RECS.b: Provides quantitative evidence of vulnerability hygiene.
RECS.i: Track mean time to patch by severity, remediation rate over time, and exceptions. Report monthly. Metrics make hygiene visible and let you spot trends. Audit and regulator scrutiny increasingly focuses on time-to-patch — having the numbers ready matters.

RECS.r: Prioritise patches by exploitability and asset criticality
RECS.b: Focuses limited patching capacity on what actually matters.
RECS.i: Don't patch in CVE order. Prioritise based on exploitability (KEV, EPSS), asset criticality, and exposure. Most CVEs don't need urgent action; a small subset need immediate response. Risk-based prioritisation makes the team's effort impactful.

RECS.r: Automate patch deployment via CI/CD where possible
RECS.b: Reduces patch latency and removes human error from routine work.
RECS.i: Build patch deployment pipelines for OS, middleware, and apps. Stage through environments with automated verification and rollback. Move from human-driven to automated by default; reserve human judgement for exceptions.

RECS.r: Integrate vulnerability management into CI/CD pipelines
RECS.b: Catches vulnerabilities before they reach production.
RECS.i: Embed vulnerability scanning in CI/CD pipelines — container image scanning, dependency analysis, code scanning. Block deployments with critical vulnerabilities. Shift-left vulnerability management is dramatically cheaper than catching issues post-deploy.

RECS.r: Build risk-based dashboards covering vulnerabilities and patches
RECS.b: Provides ongoing visibility on the risk picture.
RECS.i: Build dashboards combining vulnerability scan results, asset criticality, exploitability, and patch status. Visible to security and IT leadership. The integrated view drives better decisions than separate vulnerability and patch dashboards.

RECS.r: Set aggressive but achievable time-to-patch SLAs
RECS.b: Drives sustained capability rather than periodic effort.
RECS.i: Set SLAs for time-to-patch by severity — e.g. critical exploited within 72 hours, critical unexploited within 30 days, others within 90. Track and report. Mature companies meet aggressive SLAs consistently; immature ones miss generous ones.

RECS.r: Implement real-time vulnerability orchestration
RECS.b: Reduces time from vulnerability disclosure to patched to minutes or hours.
RECS.i: Build orchestration that automatically detects new vulnerabilities, identifies affected assets, prioritises by risk, and triggers remediation. Mature companies close critical vulnerabilities within hours of disclosure; immature ones take weeks or months. The gap is automated orchestration.

RECS.r: Use AI to predict and prevent vulnerability exposure
RECS.b: Stays ahead of attackers rather than chasing them.
RECS.i: Apply AI to vulnerability and threat intelligence to predict which vulnerabilities will be exploited and which assets need priority remediation. Pre-position patches and compensating controls. Mature companies act on emerging risk before exploitation; immature ones respond to incidents.

RECS.r: Implement immutable infrastructure where possible
RECS.b: Replaces patching with rebuilding from a known-good state.
RECS.i: For appropriate workloads, adopt immutable infrastructure — no patching of running systems; replace them entirely from current images. Removes whole classes of patch failure modes and configuration drift. The pattern increasingly fits cloud-native workloads.

RECS.r: Build self-healing platforms that auto-remediate
RECS.b: Removes humans from the routine path.
RECS.i: Build platforms that detect and auto-remediate common vulnerabilities — auto-patch, auto-replace, auto-isolate. Humans intervene only for exceptions. The maturity benchmark is platforms that maintain their own security posture; humans focus on novel issues.

RECS.r: Position vulnerability management as competitive advantage
RECS.b: Reframes patching investment as growth investment.
RECS.i: Communicate vulnerability management maturity externally — to customers, partners, and regulators. Particularly valuable in B2B contexts where customer cyber due diligence is rigorous. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment.

RECS.r: Deploy modern endpoint detection and response (EDR)
RECS.b: Replaces signature-only AV with behavioural detection.
RECS.i: Deploy a modern EDR platform across endpoints. EDR catches behavioural patterns that signature-based AV misses — fileless attacks, living-off-the-land techniques, lateral movement. Most modern attacks bypass traditional AV; EDR is the new minimum bar.

RECS.r: Centralise log collection for the most critical systems
RECS.b: Provides the foundation for any future detection capability.
RECS.i: Build basic centralised logging for the most critical systems — domain controllers, exposed services, security tools. Even basic centralisation enables retroactive investigation when something happens. Without it, post-incident investigation is largely impossible.

RECS.r: Establish basic alerting on the most obvious threat indicators
RECS.b: Catches obvious attacks before they progress.
RECS.i: Build basic alerting on obvious indicators — failed admin logins, account creation outside change windows, ransomware file extensions, AV detections. Even basic alerting catches attacks early. Sophistication can come later; existence has to come first.

RECS.r: Subscribe to threat intelligence relevant to your industry
RECS.b: Provides context the team can't generate internally.
RECS.i: Subscribe to industry-relevant threat intelligence — sector-specific feeds, government alerts (NCSC, CISA), vendor advisories. Use to inform detection and response. Threat intelligence shouldn't be exotic; even basic subscriptions raise the team's awareness materially.

RECS.r: Run a basic compromise assessment of the environment
RECS.b: Establishes a baseline of whether you're already compromised.
RECS.i: Run a one-off compromise assessment — internally if skills allow, otherwise via a partner. Provides the baseline you cannot establish from new tools alone. Many organisations discover existing compromise during this assessment; better to find it deliberately than be told later.

RECS.r: Deploy SIEM for centralised monitoring
RECS.b: Provides one place to correlate signals across the entire estate.
RECS.i: Deploy a SIEM platform with broad telemetry coverage — endpoints, identity, network, cloud, email. Tune detections continuously. Without centralised correlation, signals from multiple sources never connect; with it, complex attack patterns become visible.

RECS.r: Establish 24/7 security operations (in-house or via partner)
RECS.b: Provides continuous coverage when most attacks happen outside business hours.
RECS.i: Stand up a 24/7 SOC capability — in-house if scale supports it, or via a managed partner (MDR/MSSP). Most attacks unfold outside business hours; without round-the-clock coverage, dwell time stretches into days. Document handover protocols carefully.

RECS.r: Build a basic detection engineering practice
RECS.b: Tunes detections to the actual environment rather than relying on defaults.
RECS.i: Establish a practice of building, tuning, and reviewing detections continuously — not just relying on vendor defaults. Map detections to MITRE ATT&CK. The discipline produces detection coverage that vendor defaults can't match.

RECS.r: Define and rehearse alert triage procedures
RECS.b: Ensures alerts get acted on rather than buried.
RECS.i: Document procedures for alert triage — what an analyst checks, escalates, dismisses. Train analysts. Run through realistic alerts to validate. Without process, alert volume produces fatigue; with process, even high volume becomes manageable.

RECS.r: Track key metrics — MTTD, MTTR, alert fidelity
RECS.b: Provides quantitative evidence of capability maturity.
RECS.i: Track mean time to detect, mean time to respond, alert fidelity (true positive rate). Report monthly to leadership. Metrics make the security operations capability visible and defensible during budget discussions.

RECS.r: Upgrade to XDR for cross-domain detection
RECS.b: Catches attacks that span multiple domains in a way SIEM struggles with.
RECS.i: Adopt XDR capabilities — extending beyond endpoint to identity, network, cloud, email — with built-in cross-domain correlation. Modern attacks span domains; detection that looks at domains separately misses them. XDR is the natural evolution.

RECS.r: Implement automated threat hunting and response playbooks
RECS.b: Closes the gap between detection and containment.
RECS.i: Build automated playbooks for common detection types — credential anomalies, lateral movement, data exfiltration. SOAR platforms accelerate response from hours to minutes. Pair automation with human review for edge cases and tuning.

RECS.r: Run regular proactive threat hunts
RECS.b: Catches threats that haven't triggered detections yet.
RECS.i: Schedule regular proactive threat hunts based on threat intelligence and hypotheses about what an attacker might do. Document findings. Threat hunting catches the subtle adversaries that defeat detection-based approaches; the discipline produces capability detection alone cannot.

RECS.r: Integrate threat intelligence into automated detection
RECS.b: Operationalises intelligence rather than just consuming reports.
RECS.i: Build automation that ingests threat intelligence indicators and applies them as detections. New IoCs become detection within minutes, not days. Operationalised intelligence is dramatically more valuable than reports nobody acts on.

RECS.r: Continuously assess posture against benchmarks (CIS, NIST)
RECS.b: Provides external reference points to validate maturity.
RECS.i: Run continuous posture assessment against industry benchmarks (CIS Controls, NIST CSF, sector frameworks). Track gap closure over time. External frameworks provide structured improvement paths and audit-friendly evidence of progress.

RECS.r: Use AI/ML for detection at scale across the estate
RECS.b: Catches subtle attack patterns that rule-based detection misses.
RECS.i: Layer AI-driven anomaly detection onto telemetry to spot unusual patterns — login anomalies, data movement spikes, unusual binary execution. Tune to your environment to manage false positives. Catches threats that don't match known indicators.

RECS.r: Implement autonomous response for routine threats
RECS.b: Closes the time-to-contain window to seconds.
RECS.i: For routine, high-confidence detections, implement autonomous response — auto-isolate, auto-revoke session, auto-block. Reserve human judgement for novel or high-impact decisions. Mature SecOps closes routine threats in seconds; immature operations take hours.

RECS.r: Build continuous attack surface management
RECS.b: Treats the attack surface as a dynamically managed property.
RECS.i: Implement continuous attack surface management — discovery of internet-exposed assets, configuration drift, shadow IT. Combined with prioritised remediation. The attack surface changes constantly; managing it requires continuous capability, not periodic audits.

RECS.r: Run red team / purple team exercises continuously
RECS.b: Validates capability against realistic adversaries.
RECS.i: Run regular red team or purple team exercises against your environment. Use findings to drive detection engineering and response improvement. Continuous adversarial validation is what produces detection capability that real attackers can't easily defeat.

RECS.r: Position security operations as competitive trust commitment
RECS.b: Reframes investment as growth investment.
RECS.i: Communicate security operations maturity externally — to customers, partners, and regulators. Particularly valuable in B2B and regulated contexts. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment in maintaining the capability.

RECS.r: Draft a basic IR plan covering detection through recovery
RECS.b: Even an imperfect plan beats the absence of one when something happens.
RECS.i: Draft a basic IR plan — who responds, what steps, what authorities. Include external contacts (legal, PR, vendors, cyber insurance). Even a one-page plan dramatically improves outcomes versus ad-hoc response. Comprehensive can come later.

RECS.r: Establish a basic IR contact list with after-hours numbers
RECS.b: Removes the basic operational obstacle of not knowing who to call.
RECS.i: Build and maintain a contact list — IT leadership, security team, executives, legal, PR, key vendors, cyber insurer. Include after-hours and personal numbers. Distribute. In real incidents, finding the right person is often the first major delay.

RECS.r: Identify a cyber insurance partner if not already engaged
RECS.b: Provides expertise and financial backstop you cannot stand up internally fast enough.
RECS.i: Engage a cyber insurance partner if you don't have one. Their incident response panel and breach coach are invaluable in a real event. Most organisations cannot stand up the legal, forensic, and PR capability needed during a major incident; insurers can.

RECS.r: Pre-engage a forensic and IR firm for retainer access
RECS.b: Gets you to the front of the queue when capacity is tight.
RECS.i: Pre-engage a forensic and IR firm with a retainer. During major incidents, capacity is constrained; retainer customers get attention first. The retainer is cheap insurance and enables fast access to expertise you don't have internally.

RECS.r: Document the basic decision framework for incident escalation
RECS.b: Reduces decision delay when minutes matter.
RECS.i: Document criteria for escalating to executives, engaging legal, contacting insurers, notifying regulators. Even rough criteria reduce decision paralysis during real events. The middle of an incident is the worst time to figure out who can make which call.

RECS.r: Document an IR plan with clear roles and escalation paths
RECS.b: Removes confusion at the moment when clarity matters most.
RECS.i: Document the IR plan covering detection, triage, containment, eradication, recovery, lessons learned. Define roles, decision authorities, and escalation. Include external contacts (legal, PR, regulators, vendors). Test under simulated conditions, not just at audit time.

RECS.r: Run annual tabletop exercises with executives and IT
RECS.b: Builds the muscle memory and surfaces decision-level gaps.
RECS.i: Run annual tabletop exercises with realistic scenarios, including executive participation. Document gaps, decisions delayed, and assumptions broken. Most IR failures aren't technical; they're decisions delayed because no one knew who could make them.

RECS.r: Build runbooks for the most likely incident types
RECS.b: Removes ambiguity from response when minutes matter.
RECS.i: Build runbooks for the most likely incident types — phishing, account compromise, ransomware indicators, data exfiltration. Step-by-step instructions, not just goals. Validated under exercise conditions. Runbook quality is what separates fast response from slow.

RECS.r: Establish a war room (physical and virtual) for incident response
RECS.b: Reduces coordination overhead during real events.
RECS.i: Establish a designated war room — physical and virtual — for incident response. Pre-equip with required tooling, comms, contact lists. The space itself reduces coordination friction during fast-moving events; ad-hoc setup wastes the first hour of every incident.

RECS.r: Update the IR plan after every exercise and real incident
RECS.b: Closes the loop from event to improvement.
RECS.i: After every exercise and real incident, run a structured review and update the plan. Track what changed and why. Stale IR plans tend to be worse than no plan because they create false confidence; living plans deliver real value.

RECS.r: Establish 24/7 response capability (in-house or partner)
RECS.b: Cuts dwell time when every hour matters.
RECS.i: Establish 24/7 incident response capability — in-house team, retainer with an IR firm, or managed service. Major incidents don't wait for business hours. The investment pays for itself the first time you need it.

RECS.r: Build automated playbooks for common incident types
RECS.b: Standardises response and reduces variability under pressure.
RECS.i: Build playbook automation for common incident types — phishing, ransomware indicators, account compromise. Automation handles routine steps consistently; humans focus on judgement calls. Speeds containment significantly.

RECS.r: Implement SOAR for orchestrated response
RECS.b: Coordinates response across tools and teams at machine speed.
RECS.i: Deploy SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response) to orchestrate response across security tools, IT systems, and communication channels. Reduces response time from hours to minutes for known incident types. Frees the team to focus on novel issues.

RECS.r: Track MTTR and continuously improve via retrospectives
RECS.b: Closes the loop from incident to improvement.
RECS.i: Track mean time to detect, contain, and remediate. Run a structured retrospective after every significant incident. Surface and address root causes — not just immediate fixes. The metrics make improvement visible and defensible.

RECS.r: Build threat-informed defence improvement loops
RECS.b: Treats every incident as input to systemic improvement.
RECS.i: After incidents, capture what the attacker did and use to improve defence — new detections, new compensating controls, training. Mature companies' security capability compounds over time; each incident makes the next one harder. The discipline is what produces that compounding.

RECS.r: Use AI to predict and pre-empt attack patterns
RECS.b: Stops most attacks before they require active response.
RECS.i: Apply AI to behavioural telemetry, threat intelligence, and environmental signals to predict attack patterns. Pre-position controls and detections. Mature companies' incident volume drops over time as predictive capability matures; immature companies' incident volume tracks attacker activity.

RECS.r: Build continuous improvement cycles into the IR programme
RECS.b: Treats IR maturity as a continuous engineering property.
RECS.i: Establish continuous improvement cycles — purple team exercises, red team simulations, attack path analysis. Drive systematic capability uplift. Mature IR programmes mature continuously; immature ones plateau after the initial buildout.

RECS.r: Run continuous attack simulation against the environment
RECS.b: Validates IR capability against realistic threats.
RECS.i: Run continuous breach and attack simulation (BAS) against the environment. Validate detection and response against realistic attack patterns. The discipline catches capability degradation as it happens, rather than during the next real incident.

RECS.r: Position IR maturity as competitive customer trust commitment
RECS.b: Reframes IR investment as growth investment.
RECS.i: Communicate IR maturity externally — to customers, partners, and regulators. Particularly valuable in B2B and regulated contexts where customer cyber due diligence is rigorous. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment.

RECS.r: Build threat intelligence sharing into industry communities
RECS.b: Multiplies defensive capability through industry collaboration.
RECS.i: Participate in industry threat intelligence sharing — ISACs, sector groups, peer networks. Share what you've seen; benefit from what others see. Mature companies treat security as a community challenge; immature ones treat it as private. Sharing produces compounding returns.

RECS.r: Mandate annual security awareness training for all staff
RECS.b: Establishes a baseline that more sophisticated programmes build on.
RECS.i: Mandate annual security awareness training for all staff. Track completion. Use a quality vendor (KnowBe4, Hoxhunt, etc.) — DIY content typically underperforms. Even basic training meaningfully reduces the most common errors users make.

RECS.r: Cover the high-impact basics: phishing, passwords, data handling
RECS.b: Concentrates effort on the topics that produce the most incidents.
RECS.i: Build training around the topics that produce most incidents — phishing recognition, password safety, data handling, suspicious activity reporting. Other topics matter but these dominate; getting them right matters disproportionately.

RECS.r: Run a basic one-time phishing simulation as baseline
RECS.b: Establishes how much risk the human factor currently represents.
RECS.i: Run a baseline phishing simulation to measure current click rates. Don't punish clickers; use the data as motivation for the broader programme. The baseline number is also useful as a comparison point as the programme matures.

RECS.r: Communicate security responsibilities clearly at onboarding
RECS.b: Sets expectations from day one rather than retrofitting.
RECS.i: Build basic security expectations into onboarding — what's expected, what to report, how to do so. Distribute basic guidance documents. New starters arrive with clean slates; setting expectations correctly at onboarding is dramatically more effective than retrofitting habits later.

RECS.r: Establish a clear channel for staff to report suspicious activity
RECS.b: Removes the friction between awareness and reporting.
RECS.i: Build a clear, easy reporting channel — phish-report button in email, dedicated phone line, dedicated Teams channel. Promote it. Most incidents are first detected by users; reducing friction in reporting is one of the highest-ROI security investments.

RECS.r: Run regular phishing simulations with role-based difficulty
RECS.b: Builds the human first line of defence against the most common attack vector.
RECS.i: Run regular phishing simulations tuned to role-specific risk profiles. Track click rates and report rates. Provide immediate feedback to those who click. Sustained programs reduce successful phishing rates dramatically over time.

RECS.r: Make security part of onboarding and annual training
RECS.b: Establishes the baseline expectation from day one.
RECS.i: Embed security training in onboarding, with annual refreshers. Cover the actual threats your staff face — phishing, credential safety, data handling. Generic compliance training underperforms; relevant, scenario-based training drives real behaviour change.

RECS.r: Run monthly awareness updates and topical communications
RECS.b: Keeps security top-of-mind without overloading.
RECS.i: Run short monthly awareness updates — current threats, recent industry incidents, practical tips. Keep them brief and relevant. Annual training alone fades quickly; monthly touchpoints maintain awareness without producing fatigue.

RECS.r: Track and report click rates, report rates, and training completion
RECS.b: Provides quantitative evidence the programme is working.
RECS.i: Track key metrics — phishing click rate, phishing report rate, training completion. Report monthly to leadership. Improvements are slow but real; visible metrics defend the programme during budget discussions and surface emerging issues early.

RECS.r: Provide just-in-time micro-training when users click sims
RECS.b: Highest-impact teachable moment is immediately after the failure.
RECS.i: When users click phishing simulations, deliver immediate brief micro-training — what they should have noticed, how to spot similar in future. Most teachable moment is the moment of failure; the embarrassment is also the motivation. Modern platforms automate this entirely.

RECS.r: Establish security champions in each business unit
RECS.b: Builds peer-to-peer security culture beyond what central training delivers.
RECS.i: Identify security champions in each BU — interested, respected staff who advocate for security. Provide training, recognition, and a direct line to the security team. Champions embed security thinking in places central training never reaches.

RECS.r: Build role-specific security training paths
RECS.b: Acknowledges that security risk profiles vary materially by role.
RECS.i: Build role-specific training paths — finance staff get more on BEC and wire fraud, developers get more on secure coding, executives get more on whaling and travel security. Targeted training is dramatically more effective than generic content.

RECS.r: Communicate security incidents transparently as learning opportunities
RECS.b: Builds trust and uses incidents to improve culture, not punish.
RECS.i: When incidents occur, communicate transparently within the organisation — what happened, what was learned, what's changing. Avoid scapegoating individuals. Transparent communication builds reporting culture; opacity drives it underground.

RECS.r: Recognise and reward strong security behaviour
RECS.b: Reinforces the behaviour you want to see more of.
RECS.i: Recognise users who report phishing, raise legitimate security concerns, or model good practice. Public recognition (where appropriate) and small rewards. Punishment-only cultures produce hidden behaviour; recognition cultures produce visibility and engagement.

RECS.r: Measure cultural indicators alongside operational metrics
RECS.b: Captures whether attitudes are actually shifting.
RECS.i: Beyond click rates and completion, measure cultural indicators — staff confidence reporting issues, perceived support from leadership, willingness to challenge insecure practices. Survey periodically. Cultural metrics often lead operational metrics; capturing them produces earlier signal of programme maturity.

RECS.r: Embed security into business processes by default
RECS.b: Removes the dependence on user vigilance for routine security.
RECS.i: Build security into the way work happens — secure-by-default tools, automatic data classification, friction in dangerous actions, easy paths for safe ones. Mature companies don't depend on users making security decisions repeatedly; the systems make most decisions for them.

RECS.r: Tie security KPIs to executive scorecards
RECS.b: Embeds security accountability at the leadership level.
RECS.i: Build security metrics into executive scorecards — phishing report rates, training completion, incident response readiness. What gets measured gets attention. Without leadership accountability, security competes against more visible metrics and loses.

RECS.r: Position security as integral to customer trust and brand
RECS.b: Reframes security investment as growth investment.
RECS.i: Communicate security culture externally — to customers, partners, regulators. Particularly valuable in B2B and regulated contexts. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment in maintaining the culture.

RECS.r: Use AI to deliver personalised security guidance to users
RECS.b: Provides relevant guidance at the moment of decision.
RECS.i: Apply AI to deliver personalised security guidance based on individual risk profile and behaviour. Just-in-time prompts during risky actions. Adaptive training based on weak areas. Mature companies' security awareness scales with the workforce; immature programmes hit content-delivery limits.

RECS.r: Build security into leadership messaging and culture
RECS.b: Top-down signal does what bottom-up alone cannot.
RECS.i: Have senior executives consistently model and message security as core to how the company operates. Leadership tone is what either reinforces or undermines the entire programme. Mature companies' security culture is recognisably leadership-driven; immature ones depend on the security team alone.

RECS.r: Stand up a basic library of training videos for core tools
RECS.b: Gives staff somewhere to start beyond hallway questions.
RECS.i: Build a simple library of short videos covering core platforms — Microsoft 365 essentials, collaboration tools, line-of-business apps. Use vendor content where good content exists. Even unstructured content beats no content; the goal at this stage is making something available to those who go looking.

RECS.r: Make the training library easy to find from a single landing page
RECS.b: Removes the most basic barrier — staff not knowing help exists.
RECS.i: Publish a single staff-facing portal that links to all training resources. Promote it during onboarding. Without a discoverable entry point, even good content goes unused. Discoverability is the cheapest improvement available at this stage.

RECS.r: Mandate basic digital induction during onboarding
RECS.b: Establishes a minimum baseline as people join.
RECS.i: Require new starters to complete a short digital induction covering core tools. Capture completion. Sets a baseline that prevents new starters from inheriting the legacy of self-taught gaps. Cheap to implement, lasting value.

RECS.r: Identify the highest-pain skill gaps via support ticket analysis
RECS.b: Targets initial content where it solves real problems staff already raise.
RECS.i: Analyse the helpdesk ticket backlog — what skill gaps are driving the most tickets? Build content addressing those gaps first. Helps the helpdesk and helps users simultaneously. Far more impactful than building generic content speculatively.

RECS.r: Run brief lunch-and-learn sessions on key tools
RECS.b: Creates low-cost touchpoints that supplement self-serve content.
RECS.i: Run regular lunch-and-learn sessions on common tools — short, practical, recorded. Builds awareness that the organisation cares about digital capability without requiring a full programme. Recordings extend the reach beyond attendees.

RECS.r: Build a learning management system to track training
RECS.b: Provides the evidence base needed to manage capability deliberately.
RECS.i: Deploy an LMS (Microsoft Viva Learning, LinkedIn Learning, dedicated platform) to track training enrollment, completion, and assessment. Tracking is the difference between hoping people learn and managing capability. Most platforms integrate with M365 directly.

RECS.r: Run formal training during every major technology rollout
RECS.b: Lifts adoption from accidental to engineered.
RECS.i: For every major rollout (M365 features, new LOB systems, security tools), pair the deployment with formal training — pre-rollout, at launch, and reinforcement after. Adoption rates differ dramatically between rollouts that train and rollouts that don't.

RECS.r: Map training paths to roles and seniority
RECS.b: Avoids the one-size-fits-all content that wastes everyone's time.
RECS.i: Build training paths by role family — frontline, knowledge workers, managers, executives. Different roles need different content at different depths. Generic training underperforms because it's too shallow for the engaged and too deep for everyone else.

RECS.r: Set completion targets and report them to leadership
RECS.b: Creates accountability that drives steady improvement.
RECS.i: Set completion targets per training module and report monthly to leadership. Targets drive completion; reporting drives priority. Without both, training programmes drift to whoever volunteers and miss the people who need it most.

RECS.r: Tie training to manager conversations and onboarding milestones
RECS.b: Embeds training into routine workforce processes rather than a side activity.
RECS.i: Embed training expectations into manager check-ins and onboarding milestones. Managers are the most reliable mechanism for ensuring training actually happens. Detached training programmes underperform; embedded ones become routine.

RECS.r: Build digital literacy programs targeted by role
RECS.b: Lifts productivity by ensuring staff can actually use the tools they have.
RECS.i: Define digital literacy expectations by role — what every user should know, plus role-specific skills (analytics for finance, collaboration for project leads). Build learning paths that match. Generic 'digital skills' training underperforms targeted programs.

RECS.r: Use telemetry to identify users struggling with tools
RECS.b: Targets help where it actually moves the needle.
RECS.i: Use platform telemetry (Microsoft 365 usage, app analytics) to identify cohorts struggling with adoption. Reach out proactively with targeted help. Far more effective than waiting for users to raise tickets — most struggling users don't ask for help.

RECS.r: Establish digital champions in each business unit
RECS.b: Builds peer-to-peer capability beyond what central training delivers.
RECS.i: Identify and develop digital champions in every BU — interested, respected staff who model good digital practice. Provide them training, recognition, and direct support. Champions embed digital capability in places central programmes never reach.

RECS.r: Provide hands-on coaching, not just videos and docs
RECS.b: Drives behaviour change that passive content cannot.
RECS.i: Supplement video and documentation with hands-on coaching — drop-in sessions, peer learning, embedded power users. Behaviour change requires practice with feedback. Self-serve content alone reaches only the already-motivated.

RECS.r: Measure productivity outcomes, not just training completion
RECS.b: Distinguishes training that creates value from training that just consumes time.
RECS.i: Beyond completion rates, measure outcome metrics — time saved, tasks automated, support tickets reduced. Outcome data justifies continued investment and identifies under-performing programs. Pure completion tracking flatters the data without proving value.

RECS.r: Tailor training paths for evolving tech (AI, automation)
RECS.b: Keeps staff capability current with fast-moving capabilities.
RECS.i: For fast-evolving tech (AI assistants, automation, collaboration tools), build short modular learning paths refreshed quarterly. Year-long curricula are obsolete before they ship. Modular, just-in-time content matches the pace of change.

RECS.r: Use AI-driven adaptive learning platforms
RECS.b: Personalises learning at a depth no human-curated programme can match.
RECS.i: Deploy AI-driven adaptive learning platforms that personalise content, pace, and assessment to each learner. Modern platforms continuously adjust based on performance and behaviour. Mature companies' learning scales with the workforce; immature programmes hit content-delivery limits.

RECS.r: Embed learning into the flow of work via just-in-time guidance
RECS.b: Removes the friction of leaving the task to learn how to do it.
RECS.i: Embed contextual help and guidance directly into business tools — adoption tools (WalkMe, Pendo), in-app coaching, just-in-time prompts. Most learning happens at the point of need; on-demand contextual content outperforms scheduled training for skill transfer.

RECS.r: Position digital capability as a strategic differentiator
RECS.b: Reframes investment as competitive advantage rather than overhead.
RECS.i: Communicate digital capability publicly as a competitive commitment — to customers, partners, prospective hires. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment. Mature companies' digital capability shows up in customer experience and recruiting.

RECS.r: Build a continuous learning culture led from the top
RECS.b: Cultural reinforcement is what makes learning persistent rather than episodic.
RECS.i: Have leadership consistently model continuous learning — sharing what they're learning, allocating time, celebrating capability growth. Cultural reinforcement is what makes learning persistent. Mature companies' learning culture is leadership-driven; immature ones depend on programme momentum alone.

RECS.r: Run an annual employee technology experience survey
RECS.b: Establishes a baseline you don't have today.
RECS.i: Run a simple annual survey covering core tech experience — device performance, app reliability, support quality, friction points. Even basic data is dramatically better than none. Use the results to identify the worst pain points and trigger conversations with leadership.

RECS.r: Triage tickets for experience signal, not just incidents
RECS.b: Extracts insight from data already being collected.
RECS.i: Re-categorise helpdesk tickets to capture experience signal — slow performance, app crashes, repeat issues. Most tickets contain useful experience data that's lost in basic incident management. Costs nothing extra; surfaces patterns that drive investment.

RECS.r: Capture exit-interview signal on technology experience
RECS.b: Catches experience pain that costs you talent.
RECS.i: Add technology experience questions to exit interviews. Candid feedback at exit is often more honest than what gets reported in-role. Identifies experience gaps that directly cost the business in retention and recruitment.

RECS.r: Establish a quarterly leadership review of major experience pain points
RECS.b: Builds the habit of treating experience as a managed concern.
RECS.i: Run a short quarterly review with senior leaders covering top experience pain points — what's been raised, what's being done. Even unstructured, the cadence builds awareness that experience is something the business actively manages.

RECS.r: Measure basic device performance and login health
RECS.b: Provides early objective data alongside subjective survey signal.
RECS.i: Use Intune Endpoint Analytics or equivalent built-in reporting to measure boot time, login time, and app reliability. Free or low-cost data that establishes baseline performance metrics. The data is available; very few organisations actually look at it.

RECS.r: Deploy a Digital Employee Experience (DEX) platform
RECS.b: Surfaces friction users live with but never report.
RECS.i: Deploy a DEX platform (Nexthink, ControlUp, native Microsoft tools) to measure device performance, app load times, network quality, login experience. Most users tolerate friction silently; DEX surfaces it. Use the data to prioritise platform investment.

RECS.r: Capture sentiment via pulse surveys and feedback tools
RECS.b: Pairs telemetry with how people actually feel about their tools.
RECS.i: Run short pulse surveys quarterly covering tool effectiveness, support quality, and friction points. Combine with qualitative input from focus groups. Sentiment + telemetry together drive better investment decisions than either alone.

RECS.r: Define core experience metrics and set initial targets
RECS.b: Creates the framework that turns telemetry into a managed outcome.
RECS.i: Define a small set of core experience metrics — login time, app launch time, device crashes, helpdesk satisfaction. Set initial targets even if rough. Without targets the data is descriptive only; with targets it becomes actionable.

RECS.r: Report experience metrics monthly to IT leadership
RECS.b: Embeds experience into how IT manages itself.
RECS.i: Build a monthly experience dashboard reported to IT leadership covering core metrics and trends. Visibility drives priority. Experience metrics that aren't reported regularly drift; ones that are reported get attention.

RECS.r: Train support staff to use DEX data during ticket resolution
RECS.b: Multiplies the value of DEX investment by embedding it in daily work.
RECS.i: Train helpdesk and engineering to use DEX data during ticket resolution — root cause analysis, remediation validation, proactive identification. The platform investment pays back when it changes daily work, not when it just produces dashboards.

RECS.r: Track digital friction (login times, app load, errors)
RECS.b: Provides quantitative evidence of where investment should go.
RECS.i: Build dashboards showing login times, app launch times, application errors, and crash rates. Set targets and track against them. Friction metrics tend to deteriorate slowly without triggering tickets — explicit measurement catches the drift.

RECS.r: Build action plans for low-experience cohorts
RECS.b: Turns insight into action rather than dashboard decoration.
RECS.i: For cohorts (teams, locations, roles) showing poor experience metrics, build targeted action plans. Track improvements. Insight without action plans creates frustration; action without measurement creates random improvement. Both are needed.

RECS.r: Establish an experience champion network across the business
RECS.b: Provides on-the-ground signal that telemetry alone can't capture.
RECS.i: Identify experience champions across the business — power users who report friction, validate fixes, and champion experience improvements. Pair their qualitative signal with telemetry. Most valuable feedback comes from engaged users, not the silent majority.

RECS.r: Tie experience metrics to platform investment decisions
RECS.b: Ensures investment goes where it actually moves the experience needle.
RECS.i: Use experience data as a primary input to platform investment decisions — refresh cycles, app rationalisation, infrastructure spend. Metrics-driven investment outperforms vendor-driven investment. Closes the loop between measurement and money.

RECS.r: Run experience post-mortems on major incidents and outages
RECS.b: Captures lessons that improve experience over time.
RECS.i: After major incidents, run a post-mortem covering experience impact — who was affected, how badly, for how long, what they had to do. Use the findings to improve resilience and communication. Most post-mortems focus on the technical cause; the experience impact deserves equal weight.

RECS.r: Use AI to predict and resolve experience issues proactively
RECS.b: Catches issues before users notice them.
RECS.i: Apply AI to telemetry to predict failures — disk health, app crash patterns, login latency trends — and trigger preemptive action. Proactive remediation is invisible when it works; that's the point. Users notice less, support load drops.

RECS.r: Automate self-healing for common experience issues
RECS.b: Resolves friction without human involvement on either side.
RECS.i: Build self-healing automations for common experience issues — clearing problem profiles, resetting troubled services, auto-rotating credentials before expiry. Issues never reach the user, never reach the helpdesk. Mature companies' experience improves without correlated support load.

RECS.r: Tie experience scores to executive scorecards
RECS.b: Embeds experience as a managed business outcome rather than IT concern.
RECS.i: Build experience metrics into executive-level scorecards alongside customer NPS, employee engagement, financial KPIs. What gets reported to executives gets attention. Without that visibility, experience is always second priority to whatever is on the board agenda.

RECS.r: Position digital experience as a competitive advantage in talent
RECS.b: Reframes investment as growth and retention rather than overhead.
RECS.i: Communicate digital experience publicly as a commitment — to current staff, prospective hires, the market. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment. Mature companies' digital experience is recognisable to candidates evaluating offers.

RECS.r: Continuously benchmark experience against industry peers
RECS.b: Keeps standards rising rather than drifting to internal complacency.
RECS.i: Use vendor or independent benchmarks to compare experience metrics with industry peers. Benchmarks drive ambition; internal-only comparison rewards modest improvement against modest history. External comparison resets the standard.

RECS.r: Make adoption a named outcome on every project
RECS.b: Establishes that 'go-live' is not the success criterion.
RECS.i: Add adoption as a named outcome on every technology project, with a target measurable beyond go-live (active users, basic usage rate). Even without a formal practice, naming adoption changes how projects are scoped, funded, and reviewed. Cheapest possible improvement.

RECS.r: Capture basic post-rollout usage data for new tools
RECS.b: Reveals whether deployments actually deliver the promised value.
RECS.i: After every rollout, capture basic usage data at 30, 60, 90 days. License usage, key feature usage, user counts. Simple data; rarely captured. The pattern of low adoption is usually visible within 60 days but only if someone is looking.

RECS.r: Run a post-implementation review on completed projects
RECS.b: Surfaces lessons that compound across future rollouts.
RECS.i: Run a short post-implementation review on every project covering adoption outcomes — what worked, what didn't, what we'd do differently. The discipline builds an adoption practice naturally over time, even without formal methodology.

RECS.r: Identify the worst examples of failed adoption to date
RECS.b: Builds the case for investing in change management.
RECS.i: Audit recent rollouts for adoption failures — tools deployed but barely used, licenses paid but not consumed. Quantify the cost. Concrete waste numbers are the most reliable way to unlock investment in formal change management.

RECS.r: Assign someone (anyone) to own adoption as a remit
RECS.b: A unowned remit is a dead remit.
RECS.i: Even part-time, name a person responsible for adoption thinking on technology projects. Without a named owner, adoption defaults to 'everyone's responsibility' which means no one's. The named role drives the practice forward.

RECS.r: Run formal change management for all major rollouts
RECS.b: Lifts adoption from accidental to engineered.
RECS.i: Apply formal change management (Prosci ADKAR or similar) to major rollouts — communications, training, support, reinforcement. Tools fail to land most often because the change side was underinvested. The investment is small relative to the cost of failed adoption.

RECS.r: Build a standard adoption playbook covering communications and training
RECS.b: Removes the need to reinvent the approach for every rollout.
RECS.i: Create a standard adoption playbook covering communications, training, support, and reinforcement. Each project tailors but doesn't rebuild from scratch. Quality, consistency, and speed all improve compared to bespoke approaches per project.

RECS.r: Capture and report adoption metrics on every rollout
RECS.b: Establishes that adoption is the success metric, not deployment.
RECS.i: For every project, capture and report adoption metrics post-go-live — active users, key feature usage, helpdesk volume, user satisfaction. Report up to executive sponsors. Public metrics drive priority and accountability.

RECS.r: Resource adoption explicitly in project plans and budgets
RECS.b: Stops adoption being squeezed out by build pressure.
RECS.i: Build adoption resourcing into project plans and budgets from the outset — change manager, training development, communications support. When adoption is named in the budget, it survives scope pressure. When it's not, it gets cut first.

RECS.r: Conduct stakeholder analysis at project start
RECS.b: Surfaces resistance and support patterns early when they're cheaper to address.
RECS.i: At project initiation, conduct stakeholder analysis — who is affected, what's their disposition, what do they need. Build engagement plans accordingly. Late-stage stakeholder discovery is expensive; early discovery is cheap.

RECS.r: Identify adoption barriers via surveys and analytics
RECS.b: Reveals why adoption stalls so the right barriers can be removed.
RECS.i: Combine usage analytics (who's actually using the tool?) with surveys (why or why not?). Diagnose specific barriers — training, capability, fit, leadership signal. Generic 'we need more training' rarely captures the real obstacle.

RECS.r: Use champions and peer-coaching to drive adoption
RECS.b: Trusted peers move the needle more than central training.
RECS.i: Identify and invest in champions — power users in each team. Provide them advanced training, recognition, and a direct line to the platform team. Peer coaching delivers adoption uplift that top-down rollouts struggle to achieve.

RECS.r: Tailor communications and training to specific user segments
RECS.b: Generic comms get filtered out; targeted comms land.
RECS.i: Segment users by role and tailor communications and training to what's relevant for them. Specific examples, role-relevant scenarios, named local champions. Generic mass communications get tuned out; targeted communications land.

RECS.r: Measure adoption metrics and adjust the plan accordingly
RECS.b: Treats adoption as a managed outcome rather than hope.
RECS.i: Set adoption targets (e.g. 80% active usage by month 6), measure progress, and adjust the plan when targets are missed. Treats adoption as an engineered outcome. Most rollouts measure deployment, not adoption — the latter is what matters.

RECS.r: Build an adoption Centre of Excellence
RECS.b: Concentrates expertise that would otherwise scatter across projects.
RECS.i: Stand up an adoption CoE that supports all major rollouts with proven methodology, materials, and capability. CoE delivers consistency and quality that project-by-project change management can't. Mature companies invest in CoEs because the value compounds across the portfolio.

RECS.r: Build continuous feedback loops into every digital tool
RECS.b: Replaces episodic surveys with ongoing signal at the point of use.
RECS.i: Embed lightweight feedback mechanisms directly into business tools — thumbs-up/down, friction reporting, contextual surveys. Continuous, low-friction signal at the point of use beats periodic surveys. Mature companies' feedback flows are continuous rather than scheduled.

RECS.r: Use AI to personalise adoption interventions per user
RECS.b: Right intervention to right user at right time.
RECS.i: Apply AI to behavioural data to personalise adoption interventions — targeted prompts, role-relevant tips, content matched to user behaviour. Personalised at scale outperforms cohort-based interventions. Mature companies' adoption scales with the workforce.

RECS.r: Tie adoption to executive sponsorship and accountability
RECS.b: Top-down signal is what makes adoption a strategic outcome.
RECS.i: Build adoption metrics into executive scorecards. Hold sponsors accountable for adoption, not just deployment. The executive accountability is what shifts adoption from a programme concern to a business outcome. Without it, programmes return to defaults under pressure.

RECS.r: Position adoption capability as a competitive advantage
RECS.b: Reframes investment as growth rather than overhead.
RECS.i: Communicate adoption capability publicly — to vendors evaluating you as a customer, to staff evaluating you as an employer. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment. Mature companies make adoption capability a recognised differentiator.

RECS.r: Use predictive analytics to forecast adoption risk early
RECS.b: Lets you intervene before adoption stalls visibly.
RECS.i: Apply predictive analytics to early adoption signal — usage curves, sentiment, training completion — to forecast adoption risk in the first 30 days. Early intervention is dramatically cheaper than late recovery. The mature pattern is preventive, not reactive.

RECS.r: Identify obvious manual repetitive work across the business
RECS.b: Surfaces the universe of opportunity that is currently invisible.
RECS.i: Run a basic discovery to identify manual repetitive work — finance reconciliations, HR onboarding tasks, IT user requests. Even a basic inventory exposes scale. Most organisations dramatically underestimate how much manual repetitive work exists until they look.

RECS.r: Pilot a low-code automation on a single high-pain process
RECS.b: Builds proof of value with low investment.
RECS.i: Pick one process with high manual burden and obvious automation potential. Build a pilot using low-code tools (Power Automate, Zapier). Measure the saved hours. The proof point unlocks broader investment far more reliably than abstract proposals.

RECS.r: Build basic awareness of automation capabilities across teams
RECS.b: Teams need to know what's possible before they can ask for it.
RECS.i: Run awareness sessions across the business showcasing what automation can do — examples, simple case studies, demos. Most teams don't ask for automation because they don't know it's available. Awareness drives demand.

RECS.r: Provide simple self-serve automation tools for individual productivity
RECS.b: Lets individuals automate their own work without waiting for central capacity.
RECS.i: Make individual automation tools available — Power Automate, Outlook rules, Excel macros, simple integrations. Train people to use them. Individual automation saves time at scale even before any enterprise programme exists.

RECS.r: Capture automation requests and ideas in a simple list
RECS.b: Without capture, opportunity is lost when ideas surface and decay.
RECS.i: Set up a simple intake list (Forms, ServiceNow, even a spreadsheet) for automation ideas. Track them. Even unprioritised, the list becomes the seed of a real backlog. Lost ideas can't be prioritised; captured ones can.

RECS.r: Establish IT as the lead for enterprise automation strategy
RECS.b: Provides governance and scale that ad-hoc team-by-team automation can't.
RECS.i: Position IT as the strategic owner of enterprise automation — platforms, governance, capability. Doesn't mean IT does all the building, but IT sets the rules and provides the platforms. Without strategic leadership, automation fragments and creates new debt.

RECS.r: Build a citizen developer program with IT-governed platforms
RECS.b: Scales automation capacity beyond IT's capacity.
RECS.i: Build a citizen developer program providing governed platforms (Power Platform, Zapier, low-code tools), training, and support. IT provides guardrails; business builds. Multiplies automation throughput by an order of magnitude when done right.

RECS.r: Identify high-volume, high-cost manual processes for automation
RECS.b: Concentrates automation effort on the highest-ROI opportunities.
RECS.i: Run a structured discovery to identify manual processes — process mining, time studies, cost analysis. Prioritise high-volume and high-cost. Avoid the trap of automating low-volume processes for visibility; the boring high-volume ones generate real value.

RECS.r: Define enterprise automation platforms and standards
RECS.b: Stops the proliferation of incompatible tools that creates future debt.
RECS.i: Standardise on enterprise automation platforms — typically Power Platform plus a process automation tool. Define when to use each. Without standards, every team picks differently and integration costs explode within two years.

RECS.r: Establish governance over citizen-built automations
RECS.b: Manages risk while enabling distributed building.
RECS.i: Stand up basic governance — security review for automations touching sensitive data, support model for business-critical automations, retirement process for unused ones. Light-touch where risk is low. Governance is what separates citizen development from shadow IT.

RECS.r: Set up an automation Centre of Excellence
RECS.b: Provides expertise and reusable capability that scales beyond first delivery.
RECS.i: Stand up an automation CoE responsible for platforms, standards, reusable components, and capability building. The CoE accelerates delivery teams and ensures quality consistency. Without it, each automation is built from scratch with variable quality.

RECS.r: Measure automation ROI per initiative
RECS.b: Validates investment and informs prioritisation of future work.
RECS.i: For each automation, capture baseline metrics, measure post-implementation results, and report ROI. Track portfolio-level value. Without ROI data, automation programs lose budget in the first cost-cutting cycle. Visible value defends the program.

RECS.r: Build reusable components and integration patterns library
RECS.b: Compounds delivery velocity over time.
RECS.i: Build a library of reusable components — connectors, common workflows, UI patterns, governance modules. Subsequent automations build on the library rather than from scratch. Velocity compounds; mature programmes deliver new value 5-10× faster than starting projects.

RECS.r: Run an automation skills programme tied to certifications
RECS.b: Builds a deep capability bench rather than relying on a few experts.
RECS.i: Build a skills programme — Power Platform certifications, RPA training, integration design — across IT and citizen developer cohorts. Mature programmes have a deep capability bench. Skills concentration in a few experts creates fragility and delivery bottlenecks.

RECS.r: Run a quarterly portfolio review with executive sponsors
RECS.b: Embeds the programme in business governance rather than IT-only.
RECS.i: Each quarter, review the automation portfolio with executive sponsors — value delivered, pipeline, risks, investments needed. Builds executive ownership of automation as a business outcome. Without that ownership, automation programmes plateau or contract under cost pressure.

RECS.r: Apply AI to identify automation opportunities continuously
RECS.b: Replaces periodic discovery with continuous opportunity surfacing.
RECS.i: Apply process mining and AI to operational data continuously to identify automation opportunities — bottlenecks, repetitive patterns, variance hot-spots. Mature companies don't run discovery exercises; opportunities surface continuously from operational data.

RECS.r: Use AI agents for autonomous decision-making in workflows
RECS.b: Moves beyond rule-based automation to genuine intelligent automation.
RECS.i: Build AI agents into automation workflows — handling exceptions, making contextual decisions, learning from feedback. Mature platforms blend deterministic automation with AI judgement. Pure rule-based automation hits a ceiling; intelligent automation extends the addressable scope.

RECS.r: Position automation as a board-level strategic capability
RECS.b: Reframes investment as competitive advantage at the executive level.
RECS.i: Communicate automation maturity publicly — at investor briefings, customer pitches, talent recruitment. Mature companies' automation is a recognised differentiator, not just an internal efficiency programme. The external visibility drives sustained investment.

RECS.r: Embed automation thinking into business strategy and operating model
RECS.b: Treats automation as core to how the business operates rather than a tool.
RECS.i: Embed automation in business strategy — assumed in operating models, factored into M&A integration, designed into new product launches. Mature companies don't bolt automation on; they assume it as a baseline. The pattern unlocks investment that point-projects can't justify.

RECS.r: Continuously benchmark automation maturity against industry leaders
RECS.b: Keeps the standard rising rather than drifting to internal complacency.
RECS.i: Use independent benchmarks (vendor analyses, industry surveys) to compare automation maturity with leading companies. The external benchmark resets ambition. Internal-only comparison rewards modest progress against modest history; external benchmarks drive continuous capability uplift.

RECS.r: Capture automation ideas anywhere — start with a list
RECS.b: Stops good ideas from disappearing into emails and conversations.
RECS.i: Set up a basic capture mechanism — a SharePoint list, a Forms intake, even a shared spreadsheet. Communicate where to send ideas. Most early opportunities come from staff who notice repetitive work; capturing the ideas is the prerequisite to acting on them.

RECS.r: Run a quick discovery workshop in one or two functions
RECS.b: Generates the seed of a backlog with low investment.
RECS.i: Run a 90-minute workshop with a function (finance, ops, HR) listing manual repetitive work. Walk away with 20-50 candidate items. The exercise generates a working backlog quickly and demonstrates that the problem space is large.

RECS.r: Build a simple intake form for automation requests
RECS.b: Standardises the information needed to evaluate ideas.
RECS.i: Build a basic Forms-based intake covering process description, volume, current effort, expected benefit. Reduces back-and-forth on ideas as they arrive. Even at this stage, structured intake makes triage and prioritisation viable.

RECS.r: Tag captured ideas with rough effort and value estimates
RECS.b: Lets even an early backlog be triaged sensibly.
RECS.i: Apply rough T-shirt sizing (S/M/L) to effort and value on captured ideas. The estimates don't need to be precise; they enable basic prioritisation. Good-enough estimates beat no estimates for backlog hygiene.

RECS.r: Communicate the backlog and what gets built
RECS.b: Visibility encourages contribution and manages expectations.
RECS.i: Publish a simple view of what's in the backlog and what's been built. Acknowledges that ideas are captured and processed. Without visibility, contributors stop submitting because nothing visible happens with their suggestions.

RECS.r: Build an automation backlog with intake from across the business
RECS.b: Consolidates demand signal and lets prioritisation work properly.
RECS.i: Build a single automation backlog with intake from across the business. Standard intake template covering process, volume, current pain, expected benefit. Centralised demand signal lets you prioritise across the whole portfolio rather than first-come.

RECS.r: Score opportunities by complexity, ROI, and business impact
RECS.b: Filters the backlog to focus on what matters most.
RECS.i: Score every backlog item on complexity (build effort), ROI (financial benefit), and impact (process criticality). Prioritise high-ROI low-complexity. Use the score as a guide; the investment board makes the call. Drives portfolio thinking rather than first-come fulfilment.

RECS.r: Set transparent prioritisation criteria and publish them
RECS.b: Manages stakeholder expectations and reduces escalation pressure.
RECS.i: Document the prioritisation criteria and publish them. Stakeholders accept 'yours wasn't picked because…' more readily when criteria are explicit. Hidden prioritisation invites politics; transparent prioritisation invites engagement.

RECS.r: Triage new requests within a defined SLA
RECS.b: Builds confidence that submissions are taken seriously.
RECS.i: Triage new ideas within a defined SLA — say, 10 business days. Acknowledge, classify, score, and respond to the submitter. Slow triage erodes trust and reduces future submissions. Predictable triage builds the funnel.

RECS.r: Build basic backlog reporting to leadership
RECS.b: Surfaces backlog health for management attention.
RECS.i: Build simple reporting on the backlog — items in/out, age distribution, top items by score. Report monthly. Visibility helps leadership understand whether automation capacity matches demand. Without reporting, capacity decisions are made blind.

RECS.r: Run an automation roadmap review quarterly
RECS.b: Keeps the portfolio aligned with current priorities.
RECS.i: Each quarter, review the backlog and active work with stakeholders. Reprioritise, add, retire. The cadence keeps the portfolio fresh and gives stakeholders predictable input opportunities. Static backlogs accumulate stale items and lose credibility.

RECS.r: Establish a CoE to deliver and govern automation
RECS.b: Provides delivery capacity, standards, and governance in one place.
RECS.i: Stand up a CoE responsible for automation delivery, standards, support, and capability building. Combines build capacity with governance. Without it, automation either bottlenecks on a small team or fragments into inconsistent local builds.

RECS.r: Embed automation into business process improvement work
RECS.b: Links automation to broader process work rather than treating it as a tool.
RECS.i: Embed automation thinking into BPI work from the start — what could be automated as we redesign? Often the bigger value is process simplification with selective automation, not automating broken processes. The two disciplines should work together.

RECS.r: Build value tracking through delivery and post-go-live
RECS.b: Closes the loop from forecast to realised value across the portfolio.
RECS.i: Track each automation through delivery and 90-day post-go-live with realised value compared to business case. Aggregate to portfolio level. Closing the loop on value disciplines future business cases and defends portfolio investment.

RECS.r: Establish portfolio capacity planning
RECS.b: Matches funded capacity to demand rather than reacting to it.
RECS.i: Run quarterly capacity planning — how many delivery FTEs, how many citizen developers, what platform capacity is needed for the next quarter's backlog. Mature programmes plan capacity ahead of demand; immature ones perpetually queue.

RECS.r: Use process mining to surface opportunities continuously
RECS.b: Replaces episodic workshops with continuous discovery from operational data.
RECS.i: Deploy process mining (Celonis, Microsoft Process Mining, similar) to identify automation opportunities continuously from system logs. Surfaces patterns no workshop would catch. Mature companies' backlog is data-generated, not interview-generated.

RECS.r: Apply AI to prioritise the backlog dynamically
RECS.b: Reprioritises automatically as new data and operational context arrive.
