RECS.i: Apply AI to backlog prioritisation, using realised value, evolving cost-of-delay, and changing business context. Backlog priority shifts as data shifts. Mature programmes don't need quarterly reviews to reprioritise — the backlog stays current continuously.

RECS.r: Embed automation discovery into every business change
RECS.b: Treats automation as part of how change happens, not a separate stream.
RECS.i: Build automation discovery into every business change — new market entry, M&A, restructuring, new product launches. Mature companies don't run separate automation initiatives; they assume automation thinking in every change. Compounds value across the change portfolio.

RECS.r: Position the automation portfolio as competitive capability
RECS.b: Reframes investment as growth rather than overhead.
RECS.i: Communicate the automation portfolio's value publicly — to investors, partners, talent. Mature companies make automation a recognised differentiator. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment in keeping the portfolio fresh and ambitious.

RECS.r: Continuously retire low-value automations as the portfolio evolves
RECS.b: Stops the portfolio from accumulating low-value bloat.
RECS.i: Build a retirement discipline — automations that no longer deliver value are retired with the same rigour as new ones are built. Mature portfolios are pruned regularly; immature ones accumulate dead weight. The discipline keeps maintenance load proportional to value.

RECS.r: Capture before/after stories on early automations
RECS.b: Builds basic value evidence before formal measurement is possible.
RECS.i: For each early automation, capture a simple before/after story — what was the manual process, what is it now, what's the qualitative improvement. Even uncalibrated stories build a value narrative that supports investment in formal measurement later.

RECS.r: Estimate hours saved using rough self-reported figures
RECS.b: Gives directional value data when nothing else exists.
RECS.i: Ask process owners and users to estimate hours saved post-automation. Rough, self-reported, but useful for directional comparison. Not defensible against rigorous scrutiny but better than no estimate. Move toward measured data over time.

RECS.r: Capture user feedback on each automation
RECS.b: Adds qualitative dimension that pure numbers miss.
RECS.i: After each automation, capture user feedback — what's better, what's worse, what would they want changed. Qualitative signal often surfaces issues numbers don't catch. Combine with quantitative data for fuller picture.

RECS.r: Document business impact stories for executive communications
RECS.b: Builds the language needed to defend automation investment.
RECS.i: Build a small library of business impact stories — what the automation enabled the business to do, not just the hours saved. Stories communicate value in ways pure metrics don't. Useful for executive updates and broader awareness.

RECS.r: Identify metrics worth measuring on future automations
RECS.b: Prepares the ground for formal ROI tracking.
RECS.i: For each new automation, identify the 2-3 metrics that should be measured. Even if you don't measure them yet, naming them creates the expectation. Sets up the transition to formal ROI tracking on subsequent work.

RECS.r: Define ROI metrics (time saved, cost reduced, errors avoided)
RECS.b: Provides the basis for measuring whether automation pays back.
RECS.i: Define ROI metrics with units — hours saved per month, dollars per year, error rate reduction. Be specific. Vague metrics ('improved productivity') can't be defended. Concrete metrics let you compare investments and justify continuation.

RECS.r: Capture baseline metrics before automation goes live
RECS.b: Without baseline you can't prove improvement.
RECS.i: Before deploying any automation, capture baseline metrics — current cycle time, error rate, manual hours. Many programs skip this step and then can't credibly claim benefits. The baseline is small effort with massive payoff later.

RECS.r: Measure realised value 3–6 months post-implementation
RECS.b: Validates that promised value actually shows up in practice.
RECS.i: At 3–6 months post-go-live, measure actual value against business case. Document gaps. Use the data to refine future business case rigour. Closes the loop between forecast and reality — a discipline that makes the program more credible over time.

RECS.r: Build standardised business case templates
RECS.b: Drives consistency and rigour across the portfolio.
RECS.i: Build a standard business case template for automation requests — costs, benefits, assumptions, sensitivities, payback. Use it consistently. Standardisation makes business cases comparable and trains stakeholders on the disciplines that matter.

RECS.r: Independently validate value claims via finance
RECS.b: Adds credibility that programme self-reporting can't.
RECS.i: Have finance validate value claims on completed automations. Builds credibility — claims signed off by finance survive scrutiny in ways self-reported claims don't. Mature programmes build value-tracking partnerships with finance early.

RECS.r: Build a portfolio dashboard showing total automation value
RECS.b: Aggregates value across initiatives to defend the program.
RECS.i: Build a single dashboard showing total realised value across the automation portfolio. Update quarterly. Aggregate value tends to be substantial even when individual initiatives are modest — visibility defends program investment in budget cycles.

RECS.r: Use ROI data to justify continued investment
RECS.b: Turns the program into an investment-grade story rather than a cost.
RECS.i: Use the ROI data in budget submissions, executive updates, and customer stories. Programs with documented value continue to grow; programs that can't quantify value get cut at the first budget pressure. The data is the program's defence.

RECS.r: Build cost-of-delay analysis into prioritisation
RECS.b: Sharpens prioritisation by quantifying the cost of waiting.
RECS.i: For high-value backlog items, calculate cost-of-delay — the value foregone for each month not built. Use this in prioritisation. Mature portfolios prioritise on cost-of-delay alongside ROI; less mature ones prioritise purely on ROI and miss timing-sensitive opportunities.

RECS.r: Tie automation outcomes to business unit P&L
RECS.b: Builds executive ownership by linking automation to financial results.
RECS.i: Map automation outcomes to specific business unit P&L lines — cost saved, revenue enabled, capacity freed. Mature programmes have executive sponsors who can articulate automation impact in their own financial language. Without that mapping, executives stay disengaged.

RECS.r: Run an annual portfolio value review with the executive team
RECS.b: Embeds automation value in the strategic conversation, not just operational.
RECS.i: Each year, present the automation portfolio's value, capacity, and trajectory to the executive team. Treat it like any other strategic capability investment review. Mature programmes have this annual moment; immature ones survive on operational visibility only.

RECS.r: Use AI to predict automation value at intake
RECS.b: Improves prioritisation by replacing rough estimates with data-driven forecasts.
RECS.i: Apply AI to historical automation data to predict realised value of new candidates at intake. Reduces estimation error and improves prioritisation. Mature programmes have models with hundreds of past data points; their forecasts are dramatically more accurate than expert judgement alone.

RECS.r: Run continuous value re-evaluation across the portfolio
RECS.b: Catches value erosion as business context shifts.
RECS.i: Continuously re-evaluate value across automations as business volumes, processes, and context change. Some automations grow in value, others shrink. Mature portfolios manage this actively; immature ones assume value is static.

RECS.r: Position automation value as a competitive metric
RECS.b: Reframes investment as a competitive advantage signal.
RECS.i: Communicate automation value publicly — at investor briefings, partner events, talent recruitment. Mature companies make automation a recognised competitive metric. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment and ambition.

RECS.r: Embed value optimisation into automation operating model
RECS.b: Treats value as continuously managed, not measured at points-in-time.
RECS.i: Build value optimisation into the automation operating model — every automation has an owner accountable for ongoing value, not just delivery. Routine value reviews. Mature programmes treat value as a continuously managed property; immature ones measure it once and move on.

RECS.r: Benchmark automation value-per-FTE against industry leaders
RECS.b: Resets the standard against external evidence rather than internal history.
RECS.i: Use independent benchmarks (vendor analyses, industry surveys) to compare automation value-per-FTE with industry leaders. The external comparison drives continuous capability uplift. Internal-only comparison rewards modest progress against modest history.

RECS.r: Run an executive briefing on AI risks and opportunities
RECS.b: Builds shared executive understanding before strategy can land.
RECS.i: Run a focused executive briefing on AI — current capabilities, business opportunities, risks, regulatory exposure. Build shared understanding among the leadership team. Without baseline literacy, AI strategy conversations stall on definitions rather than getting to decisions.

RECS.r: Audit current AI usage across the organisation
RECS.b: Surfaces shadow AI before formalising strategy.
RECS.i: Audit where AI is currently being used — sanctioned tools, shadow tools, consumer accounts with company data. Likely surfaces concerning usage you didn't know about. The audit findings drive both the urgency and the shape of the formal strategy.

RECS.r: Identify 2-3 priority use cases worth exploring
RECS.b: Anchors strategy on concrete opportunities rather than generic ambition.
RECS.i: Identify 2-3 specific use cases where AI could deliver clear value — customer service, document automation, sales enablement. Concrete use cases are far easier to fund and govern than abstract AI strategy. Use them to prototype the strategic approach.

RECS.r: Issue interim guidance on safe AI use to staff
RECS.b: Closes the worst risks while formal strategy develops.
RECS.i: Communicate basic interim guidance — what consumer AI tools are not to be used, what data is not to be entered, what enterprise tools are available. Mature companies issued this guidance years ago. Even basic interim guidance reduces shadow AI risk meaningfully.

RECS.r: Assign an executive sponsor for AI
RECS.b: Without a sponsor, AI strategy remains aspirational.
RECS.i: Name an executive sponsor accountable for AI strategy — typically COO, CIO, or CDO depending on focus. The sponsor drives the work forward, removes blockers, and makes the case at executive level. Without a sponsor, AI strategy work tends to stall in committees.

RECS.r: Document an AI strategy aligned to business goals
RECS.b: Replaces ad-hoc AI experimentation with directed investment.
RECS.i: Document an AI strategy covering use cases, platforms, capability building, and governance. Anchor it to business goals — productivity, customer experience, decision quality. Without strategy, AI investment fragments and value gets diluted across disconnected pilots.

RECS.r: Establish AI governance covering ethics, privacy, and security
RECS.b: Manages risk while enabling responsible adoption.
RECS.i: Stand up AI governance covering ethics review, data privacy, security, and acceptable use. Empower it to approve and block. Light-touch where risk is low, rigorous where it's high. Governance is what separates safe innovation from regulatory exposure.

RECS.r: Define approved AI tools and disallowed practices
RECS.b: Provides clarity on what's safe to use and what isn't.
RECS.i: Maintain a clear list of approved AI tools and disallowed practices (consumer accounts with company data, unmonitored copying of sensitive content). Communicate broadly. Without explicit guidance, staff use whatever they find — sometimes safely, sometimes not.

RECS.r: Define an AI risk framework with clear thresholds
RECS.b: Stops governance turning into either rubber-stamp or blanket no.
RECS.i: Build a tiered risk framework — low-risk use cases (general productivity), medium-risk (customer-affecting), high-risk (decision-making about people). Apply governance proportional to risk. Without thresholds, governance either approves everything or nothing.

RECS.r: Set up an AI investment review board
RECS.b: Coordinates investment so AI spend builds compounding capability.
RECS.i: Stand up a small AI investment review board to evaluate proposed AI projects, ensure alignment with strategy, and avoid duplicate spend. Mature programmes invest deliberately; immature ones fund whatever surfaces from individual teams.

RECS.r: Build secure platforms for AI experimentation
RECS.b: Lets teams innovate without creating shadow IT risk.
RECS.i: Provide secure sandboxes where teams can experiment with AI on company data — Azure OpenAI, Microsoft Copilot, governed LLM access. Removes the shadow IT pressure that drives staff to consumer tools. Innovation happens within guardrails.

RECS.r: Communicate the strategy clearly across the organisation
RECS.b: Turns strategy from a document into shared understanding.
RECS.i: Run communications campaigns, town halls, and team sessions to explain the AI strategy and what it means for staff. Without active communication, strategy stays in PowerPoint and behaviour reverts to whatever was happening before.

RECS.r: Build an AI capability programme across the workforce
RECS.b: Capability is the rate-limiter on AI value realisation.
RECS.i: Build a structured AI capability programme — basic literacy for everyone, role-specific skills, advanced training for power users. The capability gap is what limits AI value far more than tool availability. Mature programmes invest in capability ahead of broad rollout.

RECS.r: Establish data foundations needed for AI
RECS.b: Data quality and access shape what AI can deliver.
RECS.i: Invest in the data foundations AI needs — data classification, access controls, integration, quality. Many AI initiatives stall on data readiness rather than AI capability itself. Mature programmes treat data investment as inseparable from AI strategy.

RECS.r: Run regular strategy reviews with executive ownership
RECS.b: Keeps the strategy current as AI evolves rapidly.
RECS.i: Review the AI strategy quarterly with the executive team. Update for emerging capabilities, regulatory shifts, and lessons from delivery. Mature companies revise their AI strategy more often than any other strategy because the technology shifts so fast.

RECS.r: Embed AI in business strategy at the board level
RECS.b: Treats AI as core to how the business competes, not a discrete capability.
RECS.i: Build AI explicitly into business strategy — products, customer experience, operating model, M&A, talent. Discussed at board level. Mature companies don't have a separate AI strategy; AI is assumed in business strategy. The pattern unlocks investment that point-projects can't justify.

RECS.r: Position AI capability as a competitive differentiator
RECS.b: Reframes AI investment as growth rather than productivity tool.
RECS.i: Communicate AI capability publicly — to customers, investors, talent. Mature companies make AI a recognised differentiator in their market. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment and disciplines short-term cost-cutting impulses.

RECS.r: Build AI-native products and customer experiences
RECS.b: Moves from internal use to customer-facing competitive value.
RECS.i: Build new products and experiences that are only possible because of AI — not just AI-augmented versions of existing products. Mature companies use AI to do new things; immature ones use AI to do existing things slightly faster. The strategic value is much larger in the first.

RECS.r: Run continuous AI strategy refresh as the technology evolves
RECS.b: Treats AI strategy as a continuously managed asset rather than a periodic exercise.
RECS.i: Build continuous monitoring of AI capabilities, regulations, and competitive moves into the strategy function. Refresh strategy as inputs change, not on a fixed cadence. Mature companies' AI strategy is continuously current; immature ones rely on annual rewrites that are obsolete on release.

RECS.r: Lead industry collaboration on responsible AI standards
RECS.b: Builds market-shaping capability that compounds beyond direct investment returns.
RECS.i: Participate visibly in industry collaboration on responsible AI — standards bodies, sector consortia, policy submissions. Mature companies shape the environment they operate in. The participation builds market leadership and informs strategy with insights peers don't have.

RECS.r: Run a small enterprise AI pilot for a high-value use case
RECS.b: Builds proof of value with bounded investment.
RECS.i: Run a focused AI pilot for one high-value use case — sales document drafting, customer service summarisation, code generation. 20-50 users, 90 days. The proof point unlocks broader investment and surfaces real-world considerations no analysis catches.

RECS.r: Block consumer AI tools from accessing company data
RECS.b: Reduces shadow AI risk while building enterprise capability.
RECS.i: Block consumer AI tools (free ChatGPT accounts with company logins, untrusted browser plugins) from accessing company data via DLP, browser controls, network policy. Pair with messaging about why. Mature companies treat consumer AI like any other unmanaged SaaS — blocked or sanctioned.

RECS.r: Stand up a single approved enterprise AI platform
RECS.b: Establishes a sanctioned alternative as you block unsanctioned ones.
RECS.i: Stand up a single approved enterprise AI option — Microsoft Copilot, ChatGPT Enterprise, Azure OpenAI playground. Make it easy to access. Without a sanctioned alternative, blocking consumer AI just drives use further underground.

RECS.r: Set up data and content protection before broad deployment
RECS.b: Avoids exposing sensitive content the moment AI gets indexing access.
RECS.i: Before scaling AI, set up at least basic DLP, sensitivity labelling, and access controls on the most sensitive content. AI scales access; pre-existing oversharing becomes newly visible. Address the underlying access model before turning on AI indexing.

RECS.r: Capture pilot results for the business case to scale
RECS.b: Builds the evidence needed to fund broader deployment.
RECS.i: Document pilot results carefully — usage, qualitative feedback, time saved, issues encountered. The data carries the conversation about scaling far better than vendor case studies. Mature companies' AI scaling decisions are evidence-based, not faith-based.

RECS.r: Deploy enterprise AI tools (Copilot, internal LLMs) broadly
RECS.b: Provides AI capability at scale rather than for a privileged few.
RECS.i: Roll out enterprise AI tools — Microsoft Copilot, governed LLM access, role-specific assistants — to a broad user base. Usage data shows where value emerges. Locking AI to a small group misses the productivity dividend that comes with scale.

RECS.r: Train users to use AI productively and responsibly
RECS.b: Closes the gap between license deployment and real productivity.
RECS.i: Provide structured AI training covering effective prompting, recognising hallucinations, data handling. Most users plateau at superficial usage without training. The license cost is wasted if users don't know how to extract value from it.

RECS.r: Build adoption support for AI tools
RECS.b: Lifts adoption from accidental to engineered.
RECS.i: Treat AI rollout as a major adoption programme — communications, champions, role-specific examples, ongoing support. AI tools have lower passive adoption than most software because users don't know what they're for. Active adoption support is what unlocks value at scale.

RECS.r: Capture and share AI usage patterns and prompt libraries
RECS.b: Compounds capability building across the organisation.
RECS.i: Build internal libraries of prompts, examples, and use cases that work. Surface success stories. Most users discover AI value via examples from peers, not training. The knowledge-sharing infrastructure is what accelerates capability uplift across the workforce.

RECS.r: Track AI usage telemetry for adoption insight
RECS.b: Reveals where adoption is sticking and where it's not.
RECS.i: Use platform telemetry (Microsoft 365 admin, Copilot reports) to track adoption — active users, feature usage, intensity. Identify cohorts not adopting and intervene. Without telemetry, adoption is opaque; with it, it's manageable.

RECS.r: Integrate AI into core business workflows, not just chat
RECS.b: Moves AI from novelty to embedded productivity.
RECS.i: Integrate AI directly into business workflows — ticketing, contract review, customer service, analytics. Embedded AI delivers value without requiring users to learn a new interface. The chat-bot pattern only goes so far; embedded patterns scale.

RECS.r: Provide secure sandboxes for user experimentation
RECS.b: Encourages innovation while containing risk.
RECS.i: Maintain governed sandboxes where users can build their own AI experiments safely — Power Platform with Copilot, Azure OpenAI playgrounds. Surface successful patterns for broader rollout. Bottom-up innovation often beats top-down on creativity.

RECS.r: Measure AI value delivered, not just license usage
RECS.b: Distinguishes adoption from value realisation.
RECS.i: Measure outcomes — time saved, document quality, response times — not just login counts. License usage is a leading indicator at best; outcome metrics are the real test. Build the measurement before scaling, not after.

RECS.r: Build role-specific AI assistants for high-value workflows
RECS.b: Targets AI investment where the highest impact is achievable.
RECS.i: Build role-specific AI assistants — sales assistant pre-loaded with sales process and product info, support assistant connected to KB and ticket history, analyst assistant with data context. Generic AI helps; role-specific AI delivers material productivity gains.

RECS.r: Establish responsible AI guardrails in production workflows
RECS.b: Manages risk as AI moves from individual tools to business processes.
RECS.i: Build responsible AI controls into production workflows — output review for high-stakes decisions, audit logs, hallucination detection, bias monitoring. Mature companies' AI in production has guardrails the user doesn't see; immature ones rely on user vigilance.

RECS.r: Build custom AI models and agents on enterprise data
RECS.b: Unlocks value general-purpose AI tools can't reach.
RECS.i: Build custom AI capability — fine-tuned models, multi-agent systems, retrieval-augmented generation on proprietary data. Where general-purpose AI plateaus, custom AI keeps creating value. Mature companies invest in custom capability; immature ones rely entirely on vendor-provided AI.

RECS.r: Run AI agents autonomously on multi-step business work
RECS.b: Moves AI from assistance to delegation.
RECS.i: Build AI agents capable of executing multi-step business work autonomously — research, analysis, drafting, simple decisions — with appropriate oversight. The autonomous pattern is the next step beyond AI as personal assistant. Mature companies are actively building agent capability; immature ones still see AI as a chat box.

RECS.r: Measure AI's contribution to revenue, customer NPS, and competitive position
RECS.b: Reframes AI investment as a competitive advantage.
RECS.i: Track AI's contribution to revenue (deals closed faster, better win rates), customer NPS (faster response, better personalisation), and competitive position (capabilities competitors don't have). Mature companies measure AI competitively, not just productively.

RECS.r: Embed AI across the customer experience, not just internal productivity
RECS.b: Extends AI value from cost reduction to revenue and growth.
RECS.i: Build AI into customer-facing experiences — personalised content, conversational support, predictive recommendations, automated services. Where internal AI saves cost, customer-facing AI grows revenue. Mature companies' AI investment is balanced across both; immature ones are internal-only.

RECS.r: Continuously evolve AI capability as the technology shifts
RECS.b: Mature programmes are continuously current rather than periodically refreshed.
RECS.i: Build a sustained capability function that monitors AI advances, evaluates new platforms, and reshapes the AI portfolio quarterly or faster. Technology shifts faster than annual planning cycles. Mature programmes have a permanent function that keeps AI capability at the frontier.

RECS.r: Audit current access permissions on key SharePoint sites and shares
RECS.b: Surfaces the worst over-sharing before AI amplifies it.
RECS.i: Audit access on critical SharePoint sites, OneDrive shares, and file servers — who has access, who shouldn't, what's shared with 'Everyone'. Most organisations are shocked by the audit results. The findings drive urgent remediation and inform the broader access strategy.

RECS.r: Lock down 'Everyone' and 'All Company' shares immediately
RECS.b: Removes the highest-risk default that AI most dramatically amplifies.
RECS.i: Identify and remediate content shared with 'Everyone except external' or 'All Company'. Replace with appropriate group access. AI indexing makes oversharing immediately and broadly visible. This is the cheapest, highest-impact remediation available.

RECS.r: Tighten guest and external sharing policies
RECS.b: Reduces external exposure that often persists from old projects.
RECS.i: Audit external sharing — who has access to what from outside the company. Set expiry on external links. Require approval for new external shares to sensitive content. External access tends to accumulate over time without active management.

RECS.r: Identify high-sensitivity content needing immediate restriction
RECS.b: Targets remediation effort where breach risk is highest.
RECS.i: Identify the highest-sensitivity content (finance, HR, M&A, legal) and restrict access tightly. Don't try to fix everything; prioritise where the harm of exposure is highest. Mature access models always tier sensitivity; immature ones treat all content the same.

RECS.r: Communicate basic safe-handling rules for sensitive data
RECS.b: Reinforces with users the responsibilities tools alone can't enforce.
RECS.i: Communicate clear rules — what data goes where, what shouldn't be shared externally, what shouldn't be entered into consumer AI tools. Even before formal classification, basic rules shift behaviour. Reinforce regularly until they're second nature.

RECS.r: Implement data classification and sensitivity labels
RECS.b: Provides the foundation for any access or AI control to work.
RECS.i: Implement data classification and sensitivity labelling (e.g. Microsoft Purview labels). Auto-labelling for common patterns; manual for unique. Without classification, downstream controls (DLP, AI access boundaries) operate blind. Classification is the prerequisite for everything else.

RECS.r: Apply least-privilege access controls before enabling AI indexing
RECS.b: Stops AI from amplifying existing over-sharing into a much bigger problem.
RECS.i: Audit and tighten access permissions before enabling AI indexing on enterprise content. AI exposes whatever the underlying access model allows. Pre-existing oversharing becomes newly visible at scale. Fix the access model first, then enable AI.

RECS.r: Roll out auto-classification on common sensitive data patterns
RECS.b: Scales classification beyond what manual processes can sustain.
RECS.i: Use auto-classification for common sensitive patterns — credit card numbers, IDs, financial data, PII. Tools like Microsoft Purview, Symantec, vendor-specific solutions. Auto-classification covers the volume that manual processes never catch up with.

RECS.r: Build access reviews into joiner/mover/leaver process
RECS.b: Stops privilege accumulation as people change roles.
RECS.i: Embed access reviews into JML processes — what does the new role need, what does the old role no longer need, what gets removed at exit. Privilege creep is the silent driver of overly-permissive access; JML hygiene prevents it.

RECS.r: Train users on classification and access expectations
RECS.b: Reinforces classification through user behaviour.
RECS.i: Train users on what to classify, when to classify, and why. Auto-classification covers volume; user-driven classification covers nuance. Without training, classification stays partial and downstream controls have gaps the user doesn't see.

RECS.r: Build a governed Power Platform for citizen development
RECS.b: Lets users build with AI safely under IT guardrails.
RECS.i: Stand up a governed Power Platform with environments, DLP policies, monitoring, and capability building. Lets users build AI-powered automation safely. The alternative — banning citizen development — drives shadow IT. Govern, don't prohibit.

RECS.r: Audit AI access patterns regularly
RECS.b: Catches over-permissive configurations and risky usage.
RECS.i: Run regular audits of AI access patterns — what data is being indexed, who's querying what, where outputs are flowing. Trigger investigation on anomalies. AI behaviour can drift; audits surface drift before it becomes incident.

RECS.r: Use DLP and information protection to prevent oversharing
RECS.b: Provides automatic guardrails on data movement.
RECS.i: Implement DLP policies that block or warn on sensitive data leaving controlled boundaries. Cover email, SharePoint, Teams, endpoints, AI prompts. Combine with sensitivity labels to make policies smarter. The system catches the human errors no training can prevent.

RECS.r: Run periodic access certification campaigns
RECS.b: Forces line managers to validate that access is still appropriate.
RECS.i: Run quarterly or biannual access certification — managers attest that their team's access is still appropriate. Standard practice in regulated industries; often missing elsewhere. The discipline catches the cumulative drift that JML processes miss.

RECS.r: Establish incident playbooks for AI-related access incidents
RECS.b: Prepares the response capability before incidents occur.
RECS.i: Build playbooks for AI-related access issues — over-permissive AI indexing, inappropriate exposure via AI, accidental external prompt sharing. Mature organisations have the playbooks before they need them. Building during the incident wastes the most expensive hours of an incident.

RECS.r: Apply zero-trust principles to data and AI access
RECS.b: Removes the assumption that authenticated users can access whatever they reach.
RECS.i: Apply zero-trust to data — every access request validated on user, device, location, sensitivity. AI tooling participates in the same controls as users. Mature companies' data access is verified continuously, not granted permanently.

RECS.r: Use AI-driven anomaly detection on access patterns
RECS.b: Spots access-pattern problems faster than periodic audits.
RECS.i: Apply AI to access logs to detect anomalous patterns — unusual data access, atypical AI queries, suspicious download patterns. Investigate or auto-respond. Mature programmes catch issues in minutes; immature ones catch them in audits months later.

RECS.r: Build automated access right-sizing based on usage
RECS.b: Removes the JML/manual labour of ongoing privilege management.
RECS.i: Apply automation to right-size access based on usage — privileges granted but never used over 90 days are flagged for review. Combined with automated revocation. Mature programmes maintain least-privilege automatically; immature ones rely on increasingly stale manual reviews.

RECS.r: Position data and AI governance as a customer trust signal
RECS.b: Reframes investment as growth rather than overhead.
RECS.i: Communicate access and AI governance maturity publicly — to customers, partners, regulators. Particularly valuable in B2B and regulated contexts. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment in keeping the controls strong.

RECS.r: Run continuous compliance against regulatory and AI standards
RECS.b: Keeps the access programme aligned with evolving regulation.
RECS.i: Build continuous compliance monitoring against relevant standards — ISO 27001, SOC 2, AI-specific frameworks (NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act). Mature companies have continuous compliance built in; immature ones scramble during audits and miss continuous regulatory drift.

RECS.r: Capture qualitative success stories from early AI adopters
RECS.b: Builds initial value narrative before formal metrics are possible.
RECS.i: Identify early AI adopters across the business and capture their stories — what they're doing differently, what they're producing faster or better. Stories communicate value in ways pure metrics don't, especially when metrics aren't yet in place.

RECS.r: Run brief surveys on AI tool impact
RECS.b: Provides directional data with low collection cost.
RECS.i: Run short pulse surveys covering AI tool perception — is it saving time, improving quality, worth continuing. Even rough self-reported data establishes a baseline view. Mature programmes pair survey signal with telemetry; immature ones have neither.

RECS.r: Capture estimated time savings from users
RECS.b: Gives early directional data on productivity impact.
RECS.i: Ask AI users to estimate time saved per week. Aggregate. Self-reported estimates are imperfect but defensible at this stage and give shape to a value case. Move toward measured data over time.

RECS.r: Identify metrics worth measuring on future AI deployments
RECS.b: Prepares the ground for formal value tracking.
RECS.i: For every new AI use case, identify the 2-3 metrics that should be measured. Even if you can't measure them yet, naming them creates the expectation. Mature companies don't introduce AI without naming the success metrics.

RECS.r: Build value-tracking expectations into AI vendor selection
RECS.b: Choose tools that support measurement, not those that obscure it.
RECS.i: When evaluating AI vendors, weight value-tracking capability — telemetry, reporting, integration with measurement tools. Tools that don't expose usage data make value tracking harder. Choose for measurement; the value case depends on it.

RECS.r: Define AI value metrics (time, cost, quality, revenue)
RECS.b: Provides the language to defend continued AI investment.
RECS.i: Define metrics with units — hours saved per role per month, document quality scores, revenue uplift on AI-augmented processes. Be specific. Without measurable metrics, AI value claims become unfalsifiable and lose credibility under budget scrutiny.

RECS.r: Capture baseline metrics before AI deployment
RECS.b: Without baseline, improvement claims can't be defended.
RECS.i: Before AI rollout, capture baseline metrics for the processes being targeted — cycle time, output volume, error rate. The baseline is small effort and large payoff. Skipping it makes future value claims unprovable.

RECS.r: Run pilots with measurable success criteria
RECS.b: Forces clear thinking about what success looks like.
RECS.i: Run AI pilots with predefined success criteria — quantitative thresholds, time-bound. Decide upfront what will trigger scale-up vs retirement. Open-ended pilots tend to drift indefinitely; criteria-driven pilots produce decisions.

RECS.r: Build a standardised AI business case template
RECS.b: Drives consistency and rigour across investments.
RECS.i: Build a standard AI business case template covering costs, expected value, key assumptions, success criteria. Reuse across initiatives. Standardisation makes business cases comparable and disciplines the conversation about AI investment.

RECS.r: Validate AI value claims with finance
RECS.b: Adds credibility programme self-reporting can't.
RECS.i: Have finance validate value claims on completed AI deployments. Builds credibility — claims signed off by finance survive scrutiny. Mature programmes build the value-tracking partnership with finance early.

RECS.r: Build dashboards showing AI value across use cases
RECS.b: Aggregates value across initiatives for portfolio defence.
RECS.i: Build a portfolio dashboard summarising AI value across use cases. Refresh quarterly. Aggregate value compounds across small wins; visibility supports continued investment when individual initiatives might look modest.

RECS.r: Tie AI investment decisions to realised value data
RECS.b: Closes the feedback loop between investment and outcome.
RECS.i: Use realised value data to drive subsequent investment decisions — scale up winners, retire underperformers, rebalance the portfolio. Treats AI as a managed investment portfolio rather than a series of pilots. Drives more disciplined growth over time.

RECS.r: Run quarterly portfolio reviews with executive sponsors
RECS.b: Embeds AI value in business governance, not just IT reporting.
RECS.i: Each quarter, review the AI portfolio with executive sponsors covering value, capacity, risks, future investment. Builds executive ownership of AI as a business outcome. Without that ownership, AI programmes plateau under cost pressure.

RECS.r: Track AI cost-to-value ratios across initiatives
RECS.b: Stops investment from over-rotating to expensive low-value tools.
RECS.i: For each AI initiative, track total cost (license, infrastructure, integration, training) against realised value. Ratios identify outliers. Mature programmes manage cost-to-value actively; immature ones manage cost or value alone but rarely the ratio.

RECS.r: Tie AI investment to specific business unit P&L lines
RECS.b: Builds executive engagement by linking AI to financial outcomes.
RECS.i: Map AI outcomes to specific business unit P&L lines — cost saved, revenue enabled, capacity freed. Mature programmes have executive sponsors who can articulate AI impact in their own financial language. Without that mapping, executives stay disengaged.

RECS.r: Tie AI value to revenue, customer NPS, and competitive position
RECS.b: Reframes AI as a competitive capability rather than productivity tool.
RECS.i: Track AI's contribution to revenue (faster deals, better win rates), customer NPS (faster, more personal), competitive position (capabilities competitors don't have). Mature companies measure AI competitively, not just productively. The reframing unlocks investment that productivity-only framing can't.

RECS.r: Apply continuous AI value optimisation across the portfolio
RECS.b: Treats AI value as continuously managed rather than measured at intervals.
RECS.i: Build continuous value optimisation — A/B testing AI configurations, fine-tuning prompts and models, retiring underperformers as data emerges. Mature programmes optimise continuously; immature ones deploy and forget. The compounding gains over a year are substantial.

RECS.r: Position AI value publicly as a strategic differentiator
RECS.b: Makes external commitment that disciplines internal investment.
RECS.i: Communicate AI value publicly — to investors, customers, prospective hires. Mature companies make AI a recognised differentiator. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment and protects against short-term cost-cutting impulses.

RECS.r: Build AI value into talent strategy and recruiting
RECS.b: Captures value AI creates for the workforce experience.
RECS.i: Use AI value in talent strategy — capability for staff, work that's more interesting because AI handles tedium, productivity that translates to outcomes. AI-strong companies attract better talent. Mature companies treat AI value across both productivity and talent dimensions.

RECS.r: Continuously benchmark AI value against industry leaders
RECS.b: Resets the standard against external evidence rather than internal history.
RECS.i: Use independent benchmarks (Gartner, McKinsey, sector analyses) to compare AI value with industry leaders. The external comparison drives ambition. Internal-only comparison rewards modest progress against modest history; external benchmarks raise the floor continuously.

RECS.r: Inventory all reporting and data sources currently in use
RECS.b: Surfaces the fragmentation that needs solving.
RECS.i: Build a basic inventory of where reporting data lives — system extracts, analyst spreadsheets, departmental databases, BI tools. The inventory is usually shocking. Use it to scope the consolidation effort and to prioritise which sources matter most.

RECS.r: Identify the top 5 'whose number is right?' problems
RECS.b: Anchors the data platform investment on real pain points.
RECS.i: Identify the top reporting conflicts — different teams reporting different numbers for the same thing (revenue, headcount, customers). The conflicts are the most powerful argument for centralisation. Real examples justify investment more reliably than abstract ones.

RECS.r: Stand up a basic shared analytics environment
RECS.b: Provides somewhere to start consolidating, even before full warehouse build.
RECS.i: Stand up a shared analytics environment — a basic data warehouse, shared Power BI workspace, even a curated set of SQL views. Start consolidating the highest-value reports there. Real warehouse build can come later; the foundation is what matters now.

RECS.r: Define golden datasets for top business metrics
RECS.b: Creates authoritative sources for the most-disputed numbers.
RECS.i: Define golden datasets for the 5-10 most important business metrics — revenue, active customers, COGS. Document the definitions, sources, refresh cadence. Even before warehouse maturity, golden datasets reduce 'whose number is right?' arguments.

RECS.r: Establish a small data engineering capability
RECS.b: Concentrates expertise that would otherwise scatter across analyst hands.
RECS.i: Stand up a small data engineering capability — even one or two people. Without dedicated engineers, the platform never gets built; analysts default to whatever spreadsheets work. The capability is the prerequisite to any real platform progress.

RECS.r: Build a centralised data warehouse or lakehouse
RECS.b: Provides one source of truth instead of conflicting reports from siloed systems.
RECS.i: Build a centralised analytical platform — modern data warehouse, lakehouse (Databricks/Fabric/Snowflake) — covering critical business data. Decommission shadow analytical sources progressively. Single source of truth eliminates the 'whose number is right?' debates.

RECS.r: Migrate critical reporting to the central platform
RECS.b: Moves reporting off fragile spreadsheets and isolated tools.
RECS.i: Migrate critical reporting to the central platform progressively. Start with highest-value, highest-friction reports. Retire the originals. Resist the urge to leave 'just in case' duplicates — they undermine trust in the central platform.

RECS.r: Decommission shadow data sources where possible
RECS.b: Eliminates the alternate-truth sources that produce inconsistent reporting.
RECS.i: Inventory shadow data sources — analyst spreadsheets, departmental databases, CSV exports. Plan migration or retirement. Provide adequate capability in the central platform first; users only switch when the central option is genuinely better.

RECS.r: Establish data integration patterns (ELT, streaming)
RECS.b: Builds the pipelines that keep the warehouse current.
RECS.i: Define and implement standard data integration patterns — ELT for batch sources, streaming for high-frequency ones. Standard tooling (Fabric, Databricks, Snowflake-native, Fivetran). Without standard patterns, every integration is bespoke and maintenance crushes the team.

RECS.r: Build a data catalogue covering critical datasets
RECS.b: Makes the warehouse usable by people who didn't build it.
RECS.i: Implement a data catalogue (Microsoft Purview, Atlan, Collibra) covering critical datasets — descriptions, owners, definitions. Without a catalogue, finding the right dataset is the rate-limiter on analytical productivity. Even basic catalogue beats none.

RECS.r: Implement data quality controls and lineage
RECS.b: Builds the trust required for the platform to be the basis for decisions.
RECS.i: Implement data quality controls — completeness, accuracy, freshness — and capture lineage from source to report. Surface issues fast. Without quality controls, central platforms accumulate garbage and lose credibility. Without lineage, debugging is impossible.

RECS.r: Make the platform self-service for business users
RECS.b: Multiplies analytical capacity without scaling the central team.
RECS.i: Provide self-service capability — certified datasets, governed semantic layer, training in BI tools. Move users from 'request a report' to 'build your own'. Central teams shift to building reusable platform capability, not bespoke reports.

RECS.r: Build a semantic layer with consistent business definitions
RECS.b: Stops the same metric meaning different things in different reports.
RECS.i: Build a semantic layer with consistent definitions of business metrics — calculated measures, hierarchies, KPIs. Reports consume the semantic layer rather than recreating definitions. Mature platforms have a semantic layer; immature ones have hundreds of slightly-different metric definitions.

RECS.r: Establish data product ownership across the business
RECS.b: Distributes data ownership to the people who understand the data.
RECS.i: Establish data product ownership — each domain (finance, sales, ops) owns the quality, definitions, and access of its data. Central platform team builds platform; business owns data products. Mature companies operate a data mesh pattern; immature ones bottleneck on the central team.

RECS.r: Train the analyst community on platform-native patterns
RECS.b: Lifts the productivity of the people who consume the platform.
RECS.i: Train analysts and citizen developers on platform-native patterns — semantic layer use, certified datasets, governed self-service. Investment compounds because every trained analyst multiplies the platform's reach. Mature programmes invest in capability deliberately.

RECS.r: Build AI-native data platforms with embedded analytics
RECS.b: Treats AI as a first-class consumer of the data platform, not bolt-on.
RECS.i: Architect data platforms for AI consumption — vector stores, retrieval-augmented patterns, semantic layers AI can query. Mature platforms support AI use cases natively; immature ones add AI as bolt-on. The architectural difference shapes what AI can deliver.

RECS.r: Run continuous data quality monitoring with auto-remediation
RECS.b: Treats quality as automatically managed rather than periodically assessed.
RECS.i: Build continuous quality monitoring with automated detection and remediation — anomaly detection, schema drift, freshness alerts, auto-recovery from common failures. Mature platforms self-heal; immature ones rely on user reports of broken data.

RECS.r: Position data platform as a competitive product
RECS.b: Reframes investment as growth rather than IT overhead.
RECS.i: Communicate the data platform externally — for partners that integrate, customers that benefit, talent attracted by working with strong data. Mature companies treat data capability as a recognised differentiator. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment.

RECS.r: Apply AI to data engineering itself
RECS.b: Multiplies the data engineering team's productivity.
RECS.i: Apply AI to data engineering tasks — auto-documentation, query optimisation, anomaly detection, code generation. Mature platforms have AI-augmented engineering; immature ones have engineers manually doing what AI could do. The productivity gap compounds quickly.

RECS.r: Continuously benchmark data capability against industry leaders
RECS.b: Keeps the standard rising rather than drifting to internal complacency.
RECS.i: Use independent benchmarks to compare data platform maturity, time-to-insight, and analytical capacity with industry leaders. The external comparison drives continuous capability uplift. Internal-only comparison rewards modest progress against modest history.

RECS.r: Document a basic data classification scheme
RECS.b: Provides the categories that downstream controls reference.
RECS.i: Document a simple classification scheme — typically Public, Internal, Confidential, Highly Confidential. Define each level with examples. Without a scheme, classification conversations stall on definitions. Cheap and high-leverage starting point.

RECS.r: Manually label the highest-sensitivity content first
RECS.b: Targets initial effort where the value is highest.
RECS.i: Identify the highest-sensitivity content (M&A, board materials, payroll, customer PII repositories) and apply manual labels. Don't try to label everything; focus where the harm of mishandling is greatest. Sets a foundation for broader classification later.

RECS.r: Train staff to classify documents they create
RECS.b: Shifts new content into a labelled state going forward.
RECS.i: Train staff to classify documents at creation — quick training, integration into Office and Teams. New content gets classified; legacy content can be addressed later. Without classification at creation, the unclassified backlog grows faster than remediation.

RECS.r: Apply default labels to common locations
RECS.b: Closes the gap when users forget or skip classification.
RECS.i: Apply default sensitivity labels to high-sensitivity locations (board sites, finance team libraries) so all content there inherits the label. Reduces dependency on user behaviour. Mature programmes use defaults extensively; immature ones rely entirely on user choice.

RECS.r: Communicate handling rules per classification level
RECS.b: Reinforces with users what classification actually means in practice.
RECS.i: Document and communicate handling rules — what each level means for sharing, storage, transmission. Without handling rules, classification is theatre. With them, classification produces the protection it's meant to.

RECS.r: Run data classification across all repositories
RECS.b: Provides the foundation for any data-protection control to function.
RECS.i: Run data classification across email, SharePoint, OneDrive, Teams, file shares, databases. Use auto-classification for common patterns; manual labelling for unique. Without classification, DLP, retention, and access control all operate blind.

RECS.r: Apply sensitivity labels with auto-detection
RECS.b: Reduces dependency on manual labelling that always lags reality.
RECS.i: Implement sensitivity labels with auto-detection rules for common patterns — credit card numbers, IDs, contract language. Manual labelling alone never keeps pace; auto-detection covers the volume while manual handles the nuance.

RECS.r: Implement role-based access controls per data category
RECS.b: Ensures sensitive data is only seen by those who need it.
RECS.i: Define access policies per data classification level. Apply consistently across systems. Combine with regular access reviews — entitlement creep is constant. The classification is what makes least-privilege practically achievable.

RECS.r: Define DLP policies aligned to classification levels
RECS.b: Operationalises classification through automated controls.
RECS.i: Build DLP policies aligned to sensitivity levels — confidential content can't be shared externally without justification, highly confidential blocked entirely. Tune to balance protection and productivity. Mature programmes have classification-aligned DLP; immature ones have DLP and classification operating in parallel.

RECS.r: Apply retention and disposal rules based on classification
RECS.b: Closes the lifecycle from creation to deletion.
RECS.i: Build retention and disposal rules into the classification model — confidential content kept for X years, then deleted. Reduces hoarding risk. Mature companies dispose of data deliberately; immature ones accumulate forever and the breach exposure grows accordingly.

RECS.r: Use DLP to prevent unauthorised data movement
RECS.b: Catches policy violations the user might not know they're committing.
RECS.i: Implement DLP policies that block or warn on sensitive data leaving controlled boundaries — email, cloud sharing, AI prompts, endpoints. Tune to manage false positives. The system catches errors training alone never prevents.

RECS.r: Audit access patterns and remediate over-sharing
RECS.b: Surfaces the access drift that accumulates silently.
RECS.i: Run regular audits of access patterns — orphaned permissions, public sharing, inactive accounts with sensitive access. Remediate findings. Access entropy is constant; explicit audit-and-remediate cycles are the only way to keep ahead of drift.

RECS.r: Build classification into AI access controls
RECS.b: Stops AI from amplifying classification gaps into broad exposure.
RECS.i: Configure AI tools (Copilot, custom models) to respect classification — exclude highly confidential content from indexing, restrict outputs based on requester clearance, log AI access to classified content. AI dramatically scales the consequence of classification gaps.

RECS.r: Monitor classification accuracy and remediate gaps
RECS.b: Catches drift between actual sensitivity and applied labels.
RECS.i: Sample-audit classified content to validate accuracy — is the label appropriate? are gaps in auto-detection? Remediate. Mature programmes treat classification accuracy as a measured property; immature ones assume the label is right because the tool applied it.

RECS.r: Tie classification to broader information protection programme
RECS.b: Makes classification one part of a coherent programme rather than standalone.
RECS.i: Integrate classification with broader information protection — encryption (auto-applied based on label), retention, e-discovery readiness, regulatory compliance. Mature programmes treat classification as the connective tissue; immature ones implement classification and DLP separately.

RECS.r: Use AI for context-aware classification across all data
RECS.b: Improves classification accuracy beyond pattern-based detection.
RECS.i: Apply AI/ML models for context-aware classification — understanding semantic content, not just pattern-matching credit card numbers. Modern platforms (Microsoft Purview, third-party DSPM tools) offer this. Mature programmes classify with intelligence; immature ones with patterns.

RECS.r: Apply classification to structured data and database content
RECS.b: Closes the gap most classification programmes leave open.
RECS.i: Extend classification beyond documents to structured data — database columns, data warehouse tables, API payloads. Most classification programmes stop at unstructured content; mature ones cover structured data with the same rigour. Database PII is often the largest exposure.

RECS.r: Position information protection as a customer trust signal
RECS.b: Reframes investment as competitive advantage rather than overhead.
RECS.i: Communicate information protection capability publicly — to customers, partners, regulators. Particularly valuable in B2B and regulated contexts. Mature companies make information protection a recognised differentiator. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment.

RECS.r: Build continuous compliance reporting on data handling
RECS.b: Replaces episodic audit reporting with continuous regulatory readiness.
RECS.i: Build continuous compliance reporting on data handling — automated evidence collection for ISO 27001, SOC 2, GDPR, sector regulators. Mature companies are continuously audit-ready; immature ones scramble during audits and miss continuous regulatory drift in between.

RECS.r: Embed classification into business processes and AI workflows
RECS.b: Makes classification a property of the system rather than user diligence.
RECS.i: Build classification into the way work happens — auto-classification at content creation, AI agents that respect labels, data pipelines that propagate classification, retention triggered by classification. Mature companies don't depend on users classifying repeatedly; the systems carry classification automatically.

RECS.r: Inventory current manual data extracts and CSV processes
RECS.b: Surfaces the fragility you're paying for invisibly.
RECS.i: Build an inventory of current manual extracts — analyst-run CSV exports, scheduled email reports, weekly database queries. The inventory exposes how fragile current data flow is. Use it to prioritise which to automate first.

RECS.r: Automate the highest-volume, highest-risk extracts first
RECS.b: Removes the manual processes most likely to break and have the greatest impact.
RECS.i: Pick the 5 most-painful manual data flows — high frequency, sensitive data, business-critical. Automate them first using basic ETL tools (Azure Data Factory, Fivetran, ADF). The wins are immediate and they fund broader investment.

RECS.r: Document what each pipeline produces and consumes
RECS.b: Builds basic lineage that pays back as the platform grows.
RECS.i: For each automated pipeline, document inputs, outputs, schedule, owners. Even a simple wiki page. Without documentation, pipelines become opaque black boxes that nobody dares change. Documentation is cheap during build and expensive to retrofit.

RECS.r: Set up basic alerting on pipeline failures
RECS.b: Catches breaks before consumers notice them.
RECS.i: Configure simple email/Teams alerts on pipeline failures — even unsophisticated alerting beats none. The first signal of failure should come from monitoring, not from a stakeholder asking why the report is empty. Embarrassment-driven detection is the worst kind.

RECS.r: Establish a small data engineering capability
RECS.b: Concentrates expertise that would otherwise scatter.
RECS.i: Stand up a small data engineering capability — even one or two people. Without dedicated engineers, pipelines never get built; analysts default to manual processes that work for them. The capability is the prerequisite for any pipeline progress.

RECS.r: Build automated data pipelines from all source systems
RECS.b: Replaces manual exports with reliable, monitored data flow.
RECS.i: Build automated pipelines from all source systems (ERP, CRM, finance, ops) into the central platform. Eliminate manual CSV exports as the data refresh mechanism. Pipelines provide reliability, monitoring, and lineage that manual processes can't.

RECS.r: Implement central monitoring with failure alerting
RECS.b: Catches pipeline failures before stakeholders notice missing data.
RECS.i: Centralise pipeline monitoring with alerting on failure, latency, and quality issues. Track success rates by pipeline. Without central monitoring, individual pipeline failures only get noticed when downstream reports break — too late.

RECS.r: Standardise pipeline tooling and patterns
RECS.b: Reduces the maintenance burden of bespoke per-source builds.
RECS.i: Standardise on a small set of pipeline tools and patterns — Azure Data Factory, Databricks, Fivetran, dbt. Don't let every team pick its own. Standardisation makes maintenance scalable; fragmentation makes it impossible above modest scale.

RECS.r: Build basic data quality checks into pipelines
RECS.b: Catches bad data before it propagates downstream.
RECS.i: Add quality checks at pipeline boundaries — row count expectations, schema validation, null/range checks. Fail loudly on quality issues. Without quality gates, bad data flows through and contaminates reports before anyone notices.

RECS.r: Document data lineage from source to consumption
RECS.b: Makes downstream impact analysis possible when source changes.
RECS.i: Capture lineage from source through transformation to report. Modern tools (Microsoft Purview, dbt, Atlan) automate much of this. Without lineage, every source change becomes an archaeology project to figure out what breaks.

RECS.r: Use real-time streaming where business needs warrant it
RECS.b: Provides current-state data when daily batches aren't enough.
RECS.i: For use cases needing fresh data — operations dashboards, fraud detection, customer engagement — implement streaming pipelines. Reserve for cases that genuinely need it; batch is fine for most. Don't add streaming complexity where batch suffices.

RECS.r: Build self-healing pipelines with retry logic
RECS.b: Reduces manual intervention for transient failures.
RECS.i: Build pipelines with retry logic, backoff, and idempotent operations so transient failures self-resolve. Combine with alerting for persistent failures. Most pipeline incidents are transient; self-healing eliminates the routine reactive workload.

RECS.r: Track data freshness and quality SLAs
RECS.b: Provides quantitative evidence of platform reliability.
RECS.i: Define and track data freshness SLAs (data should be no older than X) and quality SLAs (completeness, accuracy). Report against them. SLAs make platform reliability visible and improvable rather than something stakeholders complain about anecdotally.

RECS.r: Adopt DataOps practices (CI/CD, version control)
RECS.b: Brings software discipline to data work.
RECS.i: Apply DataOps practices — version control on pipelines and transformations, CI/CD for deployment, testing, environments. Mature data platforms operate like software platforms. Treating data work as scripts-on-shared-drives stops scaling at modest size.

RECS.r: Run pipeline cost monitoring and optimisation
RECS.b: Stops cloud data costs from running away unnoticed.
RECS.i: Monitor pipeline costs (compute time, data transfer, storage). Optimise high-cost pipelines. Cloud data platforms can produce surprise bills at scale; mature platforms manage cost actively from the start. The work is much harder retroactively.

RECS.r: Apply AI to pipeline anomaly detection and root-cause analysis
RECS.b: Catches issues faster than threshold-based alerting.
RECS.i: Apply AI/ML to pipeline observability — anomaly detection on volumes, latency, quality. Auto-correlate to root cause. Mature platforms detect issues that threshold-based monitoring misses; immature ones rely entirely on hand-tuned thresholds.

RECS.r: Build self-optimising pipelines
RECS.b: Improves pipeline performance without engineer intervention.
RECS.i: Build self-optimising pipelines — automatic resource scaling, partition strategy adjustments, query plan improvements. Modern platforms (Databricks, Snowflake, Fabric) include some of this; mature engineering layers more on top. Continuous optimisation removes routine engineer load.

RECS.r: Position data reliability as a product commitment
RECS.b: Reframes investment as customer trust rather than IT overhead.
RECS.i: Communicate data reliability publicly — internal SLAs reported to stakeholders, external commitments to data customers. Mature companies treat data reliability as a product property; immature ones treat it as IT plumbing. The commitment drives sustained investment.

RECS.r: Treat the data platform as a SaaS product to internal customers
RECS.b: Drives engineering rigour through customer-style accountability.
RECS.i: Run the data platform as a product — SLAs, support model, roadmap, customer feedback. Internal data customers are real customers. Mature companies' data platforms are run with product discipline; immature ones run them as IT projects.

RECS.r: Continuously benchmark pipeline reliability and freshness against industry leaders
RECS.b: Resets the standard against external evidence.
RECS.i: Use independent benchmarks to compare pipeline reliability, freshness, and engineering productivity with industry leaders. The external comparison drives ambition. Internal-only comparison rewards modest progress against modest history.

RECS.r: Provide BI tool access to a small group of power users
RECS.b: Builds proof of concept while limiting governance risk.
RECS.i: Provide Power BI / Tableau / equivalent licences to a small group of analytical power users — finance analysts, business analysts. Let them build for themselves. The early experience surfaces what governance and capability is needed before broader rollout.

RECS.r: Identify the most-requested reports as candidates for self-service
RECS.b: Targets self-service investment where central capacity is most strained.
RECS.i: Audit IT report request queues — which reports are requested over and over with minor variations? These are prime candidates for self-service. Solving them lifts central team capacity for higher-value work and gives users faster turnaround.

RECS.r: Run basic BI tool training for power users
RECS.b: Lifts capability before scaling licences broadly.
RECS.i: Run focused training sessions for the initial power user cohort — basic data modelling, visualisation, sharing. Training first, licences second. Untrained users either build poor-quality reports or never use the licence; both outcomes are expensive.

RECS.r: Set up a small set of shared workspaces with controlled data access
RECS.b: Provides somewhere governed to build, not just personal sandboxes.
RECS.i: Create a small set of shared workspaces with appropriate data access — by team or by function. Lets users collaborate on reports rather than building isolated personal copies. The shared model is what self-service ultimately requires.

RECS.r: Establish basic naming and sharing conventions
RECS.b: Reduces the chaos that early self-service inevitably produces.
RECS.i: Document basic conventions — how to name datasets and reports, how to share, what's appropriate to publish broadly. Early discipline prevents the chaos that uncontrolled self-service can produce. Easier to set conventions now than retroactively.

RECS.r: Provide curated, certified datasets for self-service
RECS.b: Lets business users build with confidence rather than uncertainty.
RECS.i: Build a curated layer of certified datasets — modelled, documented, quality-controlled — for self-service consumption. Mark datasets as 'gold' (certified), 'silver' (curated), 'bronze' (raw). Helps users understand what they can trust at a glance.

RECS.r: Train users on Power BI or equivalent BI tools
RECS.b: Lifts capacity to consume and analyse data across the business.
RECS.i: Run structured training programs on the chosen BI tool — Power BI, Tableau, Looker. Tier from beginner to power user. Training ROI is high because trained users build for themselves and others. Untrained licences are wasted spend.

RECS.r: Build a central data catalog for discoverability
RECS.b: Lets users find and trust data without needing IT as a discovery service.
RECS.i: Implement a data catalog (Purview, Collibra, Atlan) covering datasets, owners, definitions, quality scores, and lineage. Make it the first stop for any data question. Catalogues turn 'who owns this data?' from a treasure hunt into a search.

RECS.r: Provide standard report templates and design patterns
RECS.b: Lifts the floor on report quality across the organisation.
RECS.i: Build standard report templates — visual style, common patterns, accessibility. Users start from templates rather than blank canvases. Drives quality consistency and reduces the time-to-build for users new to the tool.

RECS.r: Establish basic governance over shared content
RECS.b: Stops self-service from creating the chaos it's known for.
RECS.i: Build basic governance — workspace ownership, content lifecycle (review, retire), sensitive content review. Without governance, self-service produces hundreds of duplicate slightly-different reports with no clear authority. Mature self-service is governed self-service.

RECS.r: Establish data stewardship in business units
RECS.b: Embeds data quality ownership where the data is best understood.
RECS.i: Identify data stewards in each business unit responsible for data quality, definitions, and ownership in their domain. Provide training and direct access to the platform team. Stewardship distributes the work in a way central teams can't scale to.

RECS.r: Build a data product community of practice
RECS.b: Compounds capability across business units.
RECS.i: Establish a community of practice for data product owners and analysts — regular knowledge-sharing, common patterns, peer review. Communities of practice multiply capability investment far beyond formal training programmes. Mature programmes build them deliberately.

RECS.r: Run ongoing capability uplift and certification
RECS.b: Keeps the analyst community growing rather than stagnating.
RECS.i: Run ongoing capability programmes — advanced training, certifications, hackathons. Self-service plateaus quickly without sustained investment. Mature programmes treat analyst capability as a continuously managed asset.

RECS.r: Track self-service usage and value delivered
RECS.b: Justifies continued investment and identifies adoption gaps.
RECS.i: Track self-service metrics — active users, reports built, value (time saved, decisions improved). Report quarterly. Without metrics, self-service investment is hard to defend. With them, the value compounds visibly across the business.

RECS.r: Build curated semantic models with consistent metric definitions
RECS.b: Stops the same metric meaning different things in different reports.
RECS.i: Build semantic models centrally — calculated measures, hierarchies, business KPIs. Reports consume the semantic layer. Mature self-service has consistent metrics across the business; immature self-service has hundreds of slightly-different definitions.

RECS.r: Enable AI-assisted analytics for non-technical users
RECS.b: Democratises analytics beyond those who can write SQL or DAX.
RECS.i: Layer AI-assisted analytics — natural language queries, automated insights — on top of the data platform. Lets non-technical users explore data conversationally. Combine with curated datasets so the AI operates on quality data.

RECS.r: Build conversational AI agents tied to enterprise data
RECS.b: Moves analytics from dashboard-and-look to ask-and-answer.
RECS.i: Build conversational AI agents that answer business questions from enterprise data — sales agent, finance agent, ops agent. Mature companies are building these now; immature ones still deliver dashboards-only. The conversational pattern is dramatically more accessible.

RECS.r: Position data democratisation as a competitive capability
RECS.b: Reframes investment as growth and competitiveness rather than IT overhead.
RECS.i: Communicate data democratisation publicly — to investors, customers, talent. Mature companies treat broad analytical capability as a recognised differentiator. The external commitment drives sustained internal investment in keeping the platform usable.

RECS.r: Apply automated insight generation across business data
RECS.b: Surfaces value from data nobody asks the right questions about.
RECS.i: Build automated insight generation — anomaly alerts, trend detection, opportunity surfacing — across business data. Catches insights before users think to ask. Mature platforms surface value continuously; immature ones wait for a question that may never come.

RECS.r: Continuously benchmark data democratisation maturity against industry
RECS.b: Resets the standard against external evidence.
RECS.i: Use independent benchmarks to compare analyst-to-employee ratios, BI coverage, time-to-insight against industry leaders. The external comparison drives ambition. Internal-only comparison rewards modest progress against modest history.

RECS.r: Inventory the largest data stores and their growth rates
RECS.b: You can't manage retention on data you haven't located.
RECS.i: Build a basic inventory of the largest data stores — file shares, mailboxes, databases, SharePoint sites, backups — with size and growth rate. Reveals where retention will have most impact and where storage cost is silently compounding. Inventory is the prerequisite to any retention conversation.

RECS.r: Document retention rules for the most regulated data types
RECS.b: Starts the policy conversation with the data that has the clearest external requirements.
RECS.i: For data types with clear regulatory retention (financial records, HR files, customer data) document the required retention period. Don't try to cover everything; start with the data where the rule is unambiguous. Builds the muscle and produces immediate compliance evidence.

RECS.r: Identify the regulatory and contractual obligations that apply
RECS.b: Anchors retention to external requirements rather than guesswork.
RECS.i: Work with legal, finance, and HR to list the regulatory and contractual obligations that drive retention — privacy laws, sector regulations, customer contracts, tax records. The external obligations are the anchor; without them, retention conversations drift to opinion.

RECS.r: Communicate that 'keep everything forever' is no longer the default
RECS.b: Sets organisational expectation that retention will become managed, not infinite.
RECS.i: Communicate clearly to leadership and staff that the keep-everything default carries cost and risk — storage, e-discovery exposure, breach blast-radius. The cultural shift away from 'just keep it' makes future retention enforcement politically possible.

RECS.r: Plan a small retention pilot on a self-contained data set
RECS.b: Builds confidence before applying retention to mission-critical content.
RECS.i: Pick a self-contained data set with clear retention rules — old email archives, expired project sites, defunct system data — and run a retention pilot. Validates the technical mechanics and the political process. Successful pilot supports broader rollout.

RECS.r: Define retention policies by data type and regulatory need
RECS.b: Balances regulatory compliance against the cost and risk of holding data forever.
RECS.i: Define retention policies for each data type based on regulatory requirements, business need, and risk. Document the rationale. Holding everything forever is both expensive and a liability; defined retention manages the trade-off explicitly.

RECS.r: Tie retention to records management governance
RECS.b: Aligns IT retention with broader records management discipline.
RECS.i: Connect technical retention to the records management function so policies reflect legal, regulatory, and business retention needs. IT-only retention misses regulatory nuance; combined governance produces compliant, defensible retention practice.

RECS.r: Implement tiered storage for cost-effective archiving
RECS.b: Reduces cost of long-tail data while keeping it accessible if needed.
RECS.i: Implement tiered storage — hot, warm, cold, archive — with automated tiering based on access patterns. Cold and archive tiers cost a fraction of hot. Most data is rarely accessed after 30–90 days; tiering captures the savings without losing capability.

RECS.r: Apply retention labels to the highest-volume content stores
RECS.b: Moves retention from policy document to operational reality.
RECS.i: Apply retention labels (Purview, similar) to the high-volume stores — email, SharePoint, OneDrive, Teams. Configure policies for the data types where retention is clear. Labels are how policy meets practice; without them, retention remains aspirational.

RECS.r: Establish a retention exceptions process
RECS.b: Handles legal hold and investigation needs without breaking policy.
RECS.i: Define how retention exceptions are requested, approved, and tracked — legal hold, investigation, regulatory requests. Without an exceptions process, either retention is bypassed informally or legal needs aren't met. Documented process protects both sides.

RECS.r: Automate retention enforcement — don't rely on manual deletion
RECS.b: Manual deletion always lags; automation enforces consistency.
RECS.i: Automate retention enforcement using platform features (Purview retention, S3 lifecycle, database TTL). Manual deletion fails predictably under workload pressure. Automation makes retention reliable and audit-defensible.

RECS.r: Run periodic retention audits to verify compliance
RECS.b: Catches retention drift before it becomes regulatory exposure.
RECS.i: Run regular audits to confirm retention policies are working — sampling deletion, validating archive accessibility, checking exception logs. Catches misconfigurations and policy gaps before they become audit findings or regulatory issues.

RECS.r: Build e-discovery readiness on top of retention
RECS.b: Mature retention makes legal response fast and defensible.
RECS.i: Use retention infrastructure to underpin e-discovery — search across labelled content, preserve in place under hold, export for legal review. Mature retention turns legal response from weeks of effort to hours. The e-discovery capability is a direct dividend of disciplined retention.

RECS.r: Integrate retention with classification and DLP
RECS.b: Aligns the data-protection stack so the controls reinforce each other.
RECS.i: Connect retention to data classification and DLP so labels drive both protection and lifecycle. Confidential content is auto-classified, restricted, and retained per policy. The integrated stack is more reliable than three disconnected programmes attempting the same thing.

RECS.r: Track storage cost and risk reduction from retention enforcement
RECS.b: Demonstrates retention as a value programme, not a compliance tax.
RECS.i: Measure storage reclaimed, cost saved, and breach blast-radius reduced by retention enforcement. Mature retention produces tangible business returns — often material at scale. The metrics defend continued investment and shift the conversation from compliance burden to value delivery.

RECS.r: Apply AI to detect retention policy gaps and content drift
RECS.b: Surfaces classification or retention failures before they become incidents.
RECS.i: Use AI/ML to scan content for retention policy gaps — sensitive content without labels, content past retention still active, anomalous retention patterns. Catches drift the policy framework alone misses. Mature retention is continuously self-correcting rather than periodically audited.

RECS.r: Make retention value-aware, not just rule-driven
RECS.b: Optimises retention for business outcomes alongside compliance.
RECS.i: Layer business value into retention — keep high-value historical data longer for analytics and AI training; dispose low-value data faster. The value-aware approach treats retention as a strategic lever rather than a compliance constraint. Requires data on usage and value, which mature platforms produce.

RECS.r: Continuously align retention to evolving regulation
RECS.b: Mature retention tracks regulatory change actively rather than reactively.
RECS.i: Build a process — supported by legal and tooling — to track regulatory change and update retention policy continuously. New privacy laws, sector regulations, jurisdictional changes propagate into platform configuration within weeks. Reactive retention misses windows; continuous alignment stays ahead.

RECS.r: Use retention as input to data product and AI strategy
RECS.b: Aligns long-term retention with the data needed for future capability.
RECS.i: Treat retention decisions as inputs to data strategy — what training data will we need, what historical analytics matter, what regulatory replay is possible. Mature retention is a strategic asset, not a back-office function. Decisions made now shape capability available years out.

RECS.r: Position retention discipline as customer trust and competitive advantage
