RECS.b: Reframes retention investment as growth rather than overhead.
RECS.i: Communicate retention discipline externally — to customers, regulators, partners — as a commitment to handling data responsibly. Particularly powerful in regulated and B2B sectors. Mature companies turn retention rigour into trust narrative; immature ones treat it as cost.

RECS.r: Identify the data initiatives that should have produced business value
RECS.b: Surfaces the gap between data investment and business outcome.
RECS.i: List the major data initiatives of the past 18 months — platforms, dashboards, analytics work — and ask what business outcome each produced. The conversation often reveals that no one tracked outcomes at all. The gap itself is the most useful finding.

RECS.r: Capture the first anecdotal value stories from data work
RECS.b: Starts the value narrative even without rigorous measurement.
RECS.i: Identify a handful of cases where data clearly drove a better business outcome — a campaign improved by analytics, a cost avoided by a dashboard, a decision changed by a report. Capture them in plain language. Stories carry further than metrics in early-stage data programmes.

RECS.r: Make data work visible to business leaders
RECS.b: Visibility is the precursor to value being valued.
RECS.i: Brief business leaders on what the data team delivers — not the technical work but the business questions answered. Many leaders have no idea what their data team produces. Visibility is the precursor to value being valued; without it, the data function is invisible at budget time.

RECS.r: Pick one initiative and define what value would look like
RECS.b: Builds the discipline that will later apply across the portfolio.
RECS.i: Take one in-flight data initiative and define explicitly what business value would look like — revenue enabled, cost avoided, decision time reduced. Validate with the business sponsor. The exercise builds the discipline; one well-defined business case is more powerful than ten vague ones.

RECS.r: Acknowledge the cost of data work alongside the activity
RECS.b: Forces the conversation about value because cost is unavoidable.
RECS.i: Present the total cost of the data function — platform, tooling, people — to leadership alongside the activity it produces. The cost transparency forces the value conversation. Hidden cost lets data work continue indefinitely without scrutiny; visible cost demands business case.

RECS.r: Define value metrics for major data initiatives
RECS.b: Makes data investment defensible in business terms.
RECS.i: For each major data initiative — platform build, advanced analytics, data product — define value metrics with units. Revenue enabled, cost avoided, decision time reduced. Without metrics, data investment competes against tangible business projects and loses.

RECS.r: Build business cases with quantified data ROI
RECS.b: Lifts data work from cost-of-doing-business to investment-grade.
RECS.i: Build business cases for data initiatives quantifying both build cost and expected business value. Validate with stakeholders. Discipline of business-case rigour for data initiatives is what unlocks larger, longer-term investment.

RECS.r: Track outcomes of data-driven decisions
RECS.b: Demonstrates that the data platform enables better business outcomes.
RECS.i: Identify high-stakes business decisions that are data-driven and track their outcomes. Compare to gut-feel decisions historically. Builds the evidence that data work translates to better business results — without which the case for continued investment weakens.

RECS.r: Establish baselines so value claims are defensible
RECS.b: Without baselines, value claims are unprovable.
RECS.i: For each value metric, establish a pre-initiative baseline — current revenue, current cycle time, current cost. Value claims without baselines aren't defensible at executive review. The baseline-then-measure discipline turns intuition into evidence.

RECS.r: Categorise data work by value type, not just project
RECS.b: Reveals the patterns of where data investment is or isn't producing value.
RECS.i: Group data initiatives by value type — cost reduction, revenue growth, risk reduction, decision support, regulatory. Aggregate value by category. Reveals patterns: maybe one category produces all the value, another consumes resource without return. Drives portfolio rebalancing.

RECS.r: Build a data value realisation dashboard
RECS.b: Aggregates data value across the portfolio for executive visibility.
RECS.i: Build an executive dashboard summarising data value — initiatives delivered, business outcomes enabled, decisions supported. Update quarterly. Aggregate visibility defends the data program in budget cycles when individual initiatives might look modest.

RECS.r: Communicate data value stories to executives
RECS.b: Builds the narrative that justifies sustained investment.
RECS.i: Capture and tell concrete data value stories — the marketing campaign that doubled response, the supply chain decision that avoided cost, the customer insight that drove a product change. Stories carry further than dashboards in executive memory.

RECS.r: Run portfolio-level value reviews
RECS.b: Drives reallocation across the portfolio toward highest-value work.
RECS.i: Run quarterly value reviews across the data portfolio — initiatives delivering, initiatives stalled, initiatives no longer relevant. Reallocate resource toward what's working. Mature portfolios are constantly rebalancing; immature ones run inherited initiatives indefinitely.

RECS.r: Track value of data products against business outcomes
RECS.b: Treats data products as products with measurable customer value.
RECS.i: For each data product (datasets, dashboards, models), track usage, business outcomes attributed, and customer satisfaction. Treats data assets as products with lifecycles. Mature programmes retire low-value products; immature ones accumulate orphaned assets.

RECS.r: Tie data team objectives to business outcomes
RECS.b: Aligns team incentives with the value they're meant to produce.
RECS.i: Build data team OKRs and performance objectives around business outcomes the data work enables — not technical deliverables. Reframes the conversation from 'we delivered the platform' to 'the platform enabled X'. Mature teams own outcome; immature ones own output.

RECS.r: Treat data value as a continuously-optimised metric
RECS.b: Mature programmes manage data value the way mature operations manage uptime.
RECS.i: Track data value continuously — not at quarterly review but in near-real-time as initiatives produce or fail to produce returns. Mature data functions manage value the way mature operations manage uptime: continuous measurement, continuous correction. Quarterly cadence misses the inflection points.

RECS.r: Apply AI to surface emerging data-value opportunities
RECS.b: AI finds value patterns humans miss at scale.
RECS.i: Use AI/ML to analyse data usage, business outcomes, and decisions to surface where data is and isn't driving value. Highlights opportunities humans wouldn't spot — datasets with latent value, decisions data could improve. Mature programmes find new value continuously rather than at planning time.

RECS.r: Benchmark data value against industry leaders
RECS.b: External comparison resets the standard against external evidence.
RECS.i: Use independent benchmarks — analyst reports, peer networks, industry studies — to compare data ROI, time-to-insight, and value-per-data-FTE against industry leaders. The external comparison drives ambition. Internal-only comparison rewards modest progress against modest history.

RECS.r: Make data value central to business strategy conversations
RECS.b: Mature companies' strategy assumes data is a value driver, not a cost.
RECS.i: Make data value a standing item in strategy and capital allocation conversations — every major initiative considers what data role would amplify outcomes. Mature companies don't fund 'data projects' separately; data is a property of every strategic conversation. The integration is itself the maturity signal.

RECS.r: Position data value as customer commitment and competitive advantage
RECS.b: Reframes data investment as growth rather than overhead.
RECS.i: Communicate data capability externally — to customers, partners, the market — as a commitment that produces better outcomes for them. Personalisation, faster service, better insights. Particularly powerful in B2B and customer-data-rich sectors. Mature companies turn data competence into trust narrative; immature ones treat it as internal cost.
